Ayahs

حمٓ (١)
Ha, Meem, (1)
عٓسٓقٓ (٢)
‘Ayn, Seen, Qaf. (2)
كَذَٰلِكَ يُوحِىٓ إِلَيْكَ وَإِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٣)
Allah, the All-mighty and the All-wise, thus reveals to you and to those who were before you: (3)
لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٤)
whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth belongs to Him, and He is the All-exalted, the All-supreme. (4)
تَكَادُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِن فَوْقِهِنَّ ۚ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٥)
The heavens are about to be rent apart from above them, while the angels celebrate the praise of their Lord and plead for forgiveness for those [faithful] who are on the earth. Indeed, Allah is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful! (5)

Content of Surah Ash-Shura

The naming of this Surah is due to verse 38, which invites Muslims to consult (shura) in their affairs. Beyond this, while the Surah contains the general themes of Meccan Surahs—namely discussions on the Origin (Mabda'), the Resurrection (Ma'ad), the Quran, and Prophethood—it covers various topics summarized as follows:

The First Part: Which constitutes the most important part of this Surah, discusses Revelation (Wahy) and Allah's communication with the Prophets through this mysterious channel. It can be said that this theme overshadows the entire Surah; it begins with it, ends with it, and is mentioned throughout. In connection with this, discussions regarding the Quran, the Prophethood of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and the beginning of the Divine Mission from the time of Nuh عليه السلام are presented.

The Second Part: Contains meaningful references to the proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the signs of Allah in the horizons and within the souls, which complements the discussion on Revelation, as well as discussions on the Monotheism of Lordship (Tawhid al-Rububiyyah).

The Third Part: Refers to the issue of Resurrection and the fate of the disbelievers on the Day of Judgment. This section is brief compared to other parts of this Surah.

The Fourth Part: Consists of a series of ethical discourses expressed with a particular delicacy. Sometimes it invites toward noble traits such as steadfastness, repentance, forgiveness, forbearance, and extinguishing the fire of anger using graceful expressions.

At other times, it prohibits base traits—such as rebellion when Divine blessings arrive, stubbornness, world-worship, and lamentation during hardships—using vivid language.

Ultimately, it is a complete collection and a healing remedy for the wayfarers of the path of Truth.

The Virtue of Reciting This Surah

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

"Whoever recites Surah Ha-Mim 'Ain-Sin-Qaf is among those upon whom the angels send blessings, and they seek forgiveness and mercy for him." [^1]

In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever recites Surah Ash-Shura will be resurrected on the Day of Judgment with a face as white and radiant as the sun, until he comes before Allah. He will say: 'My servant! You continued the recitation of Surah Ha-Mim 'Ain-Sin-Qaf while you did not know its reward; but if you knew what reward it holds, you would never have tired of reciting it. However, today I shall give you your reward.' Then He commands that he be entered into Paradise and immersed in the special heavenly blessings." [^2]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah Ash-Shura. [^2]: Thawab al-A'mal, according to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 556 (slightly summarized).

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

حم (1) عسق (2) كَذلِكَ يُوحِي إِلَيْكَ وَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ اللَّهُ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ (3) لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ (4)

تَكادُ السَّماواتُ يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِنْ فَوْقِهِنَّ وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَلا إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ (5)

Commentary

The Heavens are Almost Rent Asunder!

Once again in this Surah, we encounter the "Disjointed Letters" (al-Huruf al-Muqatta'ah), reflected here in one of the most extensive forms, namely five letters (Ha-Mim, 'Ain-Sin-Qaf).

"Ha-Mim" appears at the beginning of seven Surahs of the Holy Quran (Surahs Al-Mu'min, Fussilat, Ash-Shura, Az-Zukhruf, Ad-Dukhan, Al-Jathiyah, and Al-Ahqaf); however, specifically in Surah Ash-Shura, "'Ain-Sin-Qaf" is added to it.

We have repeatedly stated that much has been said regarding the interpretation of the Disjointed Letters of the Quran, and each commentator has extensive discussions in this field. According to the great commentator, the late Tabarsi, eleven interpretations have been given for the Disjointed Letters, the significant parts of which we have previously provided at the beginning of Surahs Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, Al-A'raf, and Maryam. However, some other interpretations are not particularly noteworthy, so we refrained from mentioning them.

Nevertheless, we mention some others here that are somewhat notable, even though there is no definitive proof to establish them.

Among them is that the "Disjointed Letters" were intended to silence the disbelievers and draw people's attention to the content of the Quran. This is because the stubborn polytheists had specifically advised one another that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited the Quran, no one should listen to it, and by creating noise and clamor, they should prevent the Prophet's voice from reaching the people. Therefore, Allah placed the Disjointed Letters—which were a novel matter and attention-grabbing—at the beginning of many Surahs (29 Surahs).

Allamah Tabataba'i (may Allah be pleased with him) has innovated another possibility which can be counted as the twelfth interpretation for these letters, although he expressed it as a possibility and a conjecture.

In summary, it is as follows:

When we carefully examine the Surahs that begin with "Disjointed Letters," we see that Surahs beginning with the same type of letters share common themes. For instance, Surahs starting with "Ha-Mim" are immediately followed by the phrase "The revelation of the Book is from Allah..." or something of that meaning. Surahs starting with "Alif-Lam-Ra" are followed by "These are the verses of the Book..." or similar. Surahs starting with "Alif-Lam-Mim" are followed by "This is the Book, wherein there is no doubt..." or its concept.

From this, one can guess that there is a specific connection between the Disjointed Letters and the content of these Surahs, to the extent that, for example, Surah Al-A'raf, which begins with "Alif-Lam-Mim-Sad," has a content that combines the themes of the "Alif-Lam-Mim" Surahs and Surah "Sad."

Of course, this connection may be very deep and precise, such that ordinary understandings cannot reach it. Perhaps if we were to arrange the verses of these Surahs side by side and compare them, new matters would be discovered for us in this regard. [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 18, pp. 5-6.

Another interpretation we previously alluded to is that these letters may be indications and codes for the names of Allah, His blessings, and other matters. For example, in the Surah under discussion, Surah Ash-Shura, they have considered "Ha" as an indication of al-Rahman (The Beneficent), "Mim" of al-Majid (The Glorious), "'Ain" of al-'Alim (The All-Knowing), "Sin" of al-Quddus (The Holy), and "Qaf" of al-Qahir (The Subduer). [^1] Although some have objected that if the purpose of codes is to keep others from knowing, this meaning does not apply to the Disjointed Letters because these Great Names of Allah appear explicitly in other verses. However, it should be noted that indications and codes are not always for keeping matters confidential; rather, they sometimes serve as abbreviations. This practice existed in the past and has expanded greatly in our era, such that the names of many large institutions and organizations are in the form of acronyms, where each letter is chosen from the beginning of a word and then combined.

[^1]: This interpretation is narrated in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام (Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 9, p. 22/58).

After the Disjointed Letters, as is customary, the discourse on Revelation and the Quran begins. He says: "Thus does Allah, the Almighty, the All-Wise, reveal to you and to those before you" (كَذلِكَ يُوحِي إِلَيْكَ وَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ اللَّهُ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ).

In reality, "Thus" (kadhalika) refers to the content of this Surah and its lofty and sublime matters.

The source of Revelation is the same everywhere, and that is the knowledge and power of the Lord. The content of Revelation is also one in terms of principles and generalities for all Prophets, although changes occur in its specifics according to the needs of the time and the evolutionary path of humanity. [^1] It is noteworthy that in the verses under discussion, seven attributes of Allah's perfection are mentioned, each of which is involved in the matter of Revelation in some way. Among them are the two attributes we read in this very verse: al-'Aziz (The Almighty) and al-Hakim (The All-Wise).

His invincibility and power ('Izzat) necessitate that He has the ability to send Revelation and its grand content, and His Wisdom (Hikmat) necessitates that Divine Revelation be wise in every respect and harmonious with the needs of human evolution. The phrase "reveals" (yuhi), being in the present/future tense (mudari'), is evidence of the continuity of Revelation from the beginning of the creation of Adam until the era of the Final Prophet.

[^1]: Although commentators have mentioned various possibilities for what "Thus" (kadhalika) refers to, it appears that it refers to these very verses being revealed to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. Thus, the meaning of the verse becomes: "Revelation is just like this which Allah reveals to you and the previous Prophets." The use of a distal demonstrative for something present is for the sake of grandeur and respect, as we have mentioned before.

Then He adds: "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and He is the Most High, the Most Great" (لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ).

His ownership of whatever is in the heavens and the earth necessitates that He not be estranged from His creatures and their fate, but rather He manages their affairs and sends down their needs to them through Revelation. This is the third of the seven attributes of His perfection.

His "Highness of Station" ('Uluww) and "Greatness" ('Azamat)—which are the fourth and fifth attributes in these verses—indicate that He has no need or requirement for the obedience and servitude of His servants. If He has established programs for them and sent them through Revelation, it is only to bestow His grace upon the servants.

In the next verse, He adds: "The heavens are almost rent asunder from above them (due to the descent of this great Revelation from the Great Lord—or—due to the improper attributions the polytheists and disbelievers made to His Pure Essence, considering idols as His partners)" (تَكادُ السَّماواتُ يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِنْ فَوْقِهِنَّ). [^1] This sentence, as we indicated, has two interpretations, for each of which there is evidence:

First, that it is related to the issue of Revelation, which was the subject of the previous verses. In reality, it is similar to what appears in verse 21 of Surah Al-Hashr: "Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled and rent asunder from the fear of Allah." Yes, this is the Word of Allah, whose descent from the heavens causes them to tremble and almost shatters them. If it were revealed upon mountains, they would split apart, for it is a momentous word from an All-Wise Lord; it is only the heart of this stubborn and headstrong human that does not soften or submit before it!

Second, that the heavens are almost rent asunder due to the polytheism and idol-worship of these pagans, who equate the lowliest of beings with the Great Origin of the universe.

The first interpretation fits the verses under discussion regarding Revelation, and the second interpretation fits verses 90 and 91 of Surah Maryam, which, after mentioning the foul speech of the disbelievers who attributed a son to God, says: "The heavens are almost rent asunder therefrom, and the earth splits open, and the mountains fall in ruins, that they should attribute to the Beneficent a son." These two interpretations are not mutually exclusive and can be combined within the concept of the verse.

[^1]: Yatafattarna is from the root fatr, which originally means to split lengthwise.

Regarding how the heavens and mountains, which are inanimate objects, could split apart before the grandeur of Revelation or the foul speech of the polytheists, several interpretations exist, the details of which we provided in Surah Maryam under the aforementioned verses. The summary is as follows:

The entire universe, including inanimate objects, plants, and others, possesses a type of intellect and consciousness, even if we do not perceive it. Based on this, they glorify and praise Allah and are humble and submissive before His Word.

Alternatively, this expression is a metaphor for the grandeur and importance of the matter, just as we say an event was so great that it was as if the heavens and earth collapsed upon us.

Then, in the continuation of the verse, He adds: "And the angels glorify the praise of their Lord and seek forgiveness for those on the earth" (وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ).

The relationship of this sentence to the previous one, according to the first interpretation, is that the angels who carry this great heavenly Revelation constantly perform the praise and glorification of Allah, extolling Him for every perfection and sanctifying Him from every defect. Since the content of this great Revelation includes a series of Divine duties and obligations, and occasionally slips may occur for the believers, it says they hasten to the aid of the believers and seek forgiveness from Allah for their slips.

However, according to the second interpretation, the glorification and praise of the angels are to sanctify Allah from the attribution of partners to Him, and their seeking of forgiveness is for the polytheists who have awakened and believed, found the path of Monotheism, and returned to the One Lord.

Where angels seek forgiveness for this great sin in the case of the believing group, they will certainly seek forgiveness for their other sins as well, and perhaps the absolute nature of the verse is for this reason.

A similar great glad tiding also appears in verse 7 of Surah Al-Mu'min: "Those who bear the Throne and those around it glorify the praise of their Lord and believe in Him and seek forgiveness for those who believe: 'Our Lord, You have encompassed all things in mercy and knowledge, so forgive those who have repented and followed Your way.'"

At the end of this verse, He refers to the sixth and seventh attributes of the Lord, which are also in the context of forgiveness and mercy and have a close connection to the issue of Revelation and its content regarding the duties of the believers, saying: "Unquestionably, it is Allah who is the Forgiving, the Merciful" (أَلا إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ).

In this way, He expresses a complete set of the Beautiful Names of Allah in relation to the issue of Revelation, and meanwhile, it is a subtle indication of the acceptance of the angels' prayer regarding seeking forgiveness for the believers; rather, in addition to forgiveness, He adds His mercy to it, for His grace is immense.

Regarding the reality of "Revelation," we will speak at the end of this Surah in connection with verses 51-52.

Key Point: Do the Angels Seek Forgiveness for Everyone?

A question arises here: the phrase "and they seek forgiveness for those on the earth" is absolute here and indicates that the angels seek forgiveness for all the people of the earth, whether believer or disbeliever. Is this meaning possible?

The answer to this question is given by verse 7 of Surah Al-Mu'min, as it says: "they seek forgiveness for those who believe." Therefore, the condition for it is faith. Furthermore, they are infallible and never request the impossible for those who have no ground for forgiveness.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ٱللَّهُ حَفِيظٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْهِم بِوَكِيلٍ (٦)
As for those who have taken awliya besides Him, Allah is watchful over them, and you are not their keeper. (6)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ قُرْءَانًا عَرَبِيًّا لِّتُنذِرَ أُمَّ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا وَتُنذِرَ يَوْمَ ٱلْجَمْعِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۚ فَرِيقٌ فِى ٱلْجَنَّةِ وَفَرِيقٌ فِى ٱلسَّعِيرِ (٧)
Thus have We revealed to you an Arabic Quran that you may warn [the people of] the Mother of the Towns and those around it, and warn [them] of the Day of Gathering, in which there is no doubt, [whereupon] a part [of mankind] will be in paradise and a part will be in the Blaze. (7)
وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَعَلَهُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَلَـٰكِن يُدْخِلُ مَن يَشَآءُ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ وَٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ مَا لَهُم مِّن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (٨)
Had Allah wished, He would have surely made them one community; but He admits whomever He wishes into His mercy, and the wrongdoers do not have any guardian or helper. (8)

Commentary

An Uprising from "Umm al-Qura"

In accordance with the reference made in the previous verses to the issue of polytheism, the first verse under discussion addresses the outcome of the polytheists' actions and the end of their path, stating: "As for those who take protectors besides Him, Allah is Watchful over them, and He is aware of their intentions" (وَ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ اللَّهُ حَفِيظٌ عَلَيْهِمْ).

This is so He may settle their account in due time and grant them the necessary punishment.

Then, addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "And you are not a disposer of their affairs to compel them to accept the truth" (وَ ما أَنْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِوَكِيلٍ).

Your duty is only to convey the message and deliver Allah's word to all His servants. Similar phrases are abundant in the Quran: “You are not a dictator over them” (Al-Ghashiyah: 22); “And you are not one to compel them” (Qaf: 45); “And We have not made you a guardian over them [nor are you responsible for their deeds, and you were not sent to force them]” (Al-An'am: 107); “The Messenger’s duty is only to deliver the message” (Al-Ma'idah: 99).

This expresses the reality that Allah wills for His servants to be free and to tread His path by their own will and choice, for the true value of faith and righteous deeds lies in this very freedom; forced faith and actions hold no spiritual value.

The discourse returns once more to the issue of revelation. While the previous verses spoke of the essence of revelation, here the focus is on the ultimate goal of revelation. It says: "And thus We have revealed to you an Arabic Quran [eloquent and clear] so that you may warn Umm al-Qura (Makkah) and those around it" (وَ كَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لِتُنْذِرَ أُمَّ الْقُرى‌ وَ مَنْ حَوْلَها).

"And warn them of the Day of Gathering, of which there is no doubt" (وَ تُنْذِرَ يَوْمَ الْجَمْعِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ).

That Day when people will be divided into two groups: "A group will be in Paradise, and a group in the Blazing Fire" (فَرِيقٌ فِي الْجَنَّةِ وَ فَرِيقٌ فِي السَّعِيرِ).

The expression "And thus" (kadhalika) may refer to the meaning that just as We sent revelation to previous prophets in their own languages, We have revealed to you an Arabic Quran in your own language (thus, kadhalika refers to the phrase "and to those before you").

It could also refer to the subsequent clause, meaning: Our revelation to you is in this manner: in the form of an Arabic Quran with the aim of warning.

It is true that from the end of the verse—the phrase "A group will be in Paradise, and a group in the Blazing Fire"—it is understood that the Prophet's duty is both to warn and to give glad tidings. However, since "warning" (indhar) has a deeper impact on souls, especially among the ignorant and stubborn, the verse emphasizes "warning" twice. The difference is that in the first instance, it speaks of those who are to be warned, and in the second, it speaks of what they should fear, namely the Court of Resurrection.

A day whose scandal is extremely painful and severe due to the gathering of all humanity. [1] A question arises here: Does the phrase "so that you may warn Umm al-Qura and those around it" imply that the goal of the Quran's revelation is only to warn the people of Makkah and its surroundings? Does this expression contradict the universality of Islam? The answer becomes clear by noting one point: the word "Umm al-Qura," which is one of the names of Makkah, is composed of two words: "Umm," which originally means the foundation, origin, and beginning of anything (a mother is called "Umm" for this reason, as she is the foundation and origin of her children); and "Qura," which is the plural of "Qaryah," meaning any settlement or city, whether large or small, or a village. There are many evidences for this meaning in the Quran.

Now let us see why Makkah was named "Umm al-Qura" (the Mother and Origin of all settlements). Islamic narrations explicitly state that all the earth was initially submerged under water, and the dry lands gradually emerged from the water (modern science also accepts this concept). These narrations say: The first point to emerge from under the water was the site of the Kaaba, and then the dry lands of the earth expanded from its sides, which is referred to as Dahw al-Ard (the expansion of the earth). Given this history, it becomes clear that Makkah is the origin, foundation, and beginning of all settlements on earth. Therefore, whenever it is said "Umm al-Qura and those around it," it clearly includes all the people on earth. [1] Furthermore, we know that Islam expanded gradually:

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was first commanded to warn his close relatives, as we read in verse 214 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara: “And warn your closest kindred,” so that the core of Islam would become firm and ready for expansion.

Then, in the second stage, the Prophet was commanded to preach to and warn the Arab nation, as stated in verse 3 of Surah Fussilat: “An Arabic Quran for a people who know [and understand]” [2]; and in verse 44 of Surah Az-Zukhruf: “And indeed, it is a reminder for you and your people.”

When the foundations of Islam became strong and established among this people, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله received a broader mission and was commanded to warn the entire world, as we read in the first verse of Surah Al-Furqan: “Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion upon His servant that he may be a warner to the worlds” (and many other verses).

It was because of this mission that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله wrote letters to the great leaders of the world at that time outside the Arabian Peninsula, inviting the Chosroes, Caesars, and Neguses to Islam.

[1] Note that "warning" (indhar) takes two objects. In the verse under discussion, the first object is mentioned in the first clause, and the second object in the second clause. Sometimes the second object is accompanied by the preposition "with" (ba).

[1] This expression also appears in Surah Al-An'am, verse 92, and we have provided further explanation on this in Volume 5, page 44.

[2] This applies if we take "Arabic" to mean the Arabic language; however, if it is interpreted as "eloquent," the verse will have another meaning.

It was also based on this plan and program that his followers stepped into the whole world after him to propagate Islam and spread its teachings globally.

Regarding why the Day of Resurrection is called "The Day of Gathering" (Yawm al-Jam'), several interpretations exist: Some have said it is because the souls and bodies are gathered together. Some have said it is because a person and their deeds will be brought together. Or because the oppressor and the oppressed will be brought together. However, the apparent meaning is the gathering of all creation on that great day, from the first to the last, as stated in verse 50 of Surah Al-Waqi'ah: “Say: Indeed, the former and the latter will surely be gathered together for the appointment of a known Day.”

Since the phrase "A group will be in Paradise, and a group in the Blazing Fire" described the division of people into two groups, the next verse adds: "And if Allah had willed, He could have made them one community [and guided them by compulsion and made them believers]" (وَ لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ لَجَعَلَهُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً).

But what value does forced faith have? And how can it be a criterion for human perfection? Real perfection is that which a human achieves through their own will and in total choice and freedom.

The verses of the Quran are full of proofs for human choice and free will. Fundamentally, the distinction of humans from other living beings lies in this very issue. If human freedom were taken away, their humanity would effectively be taken away! This is the greatest distinction Allah has granted them, opening the path of perfection to them boundlessly; this is the unchangeable divine Sunnah.

It is strange that an uninformed group still advocates for the school of determinism (jabr) while simultaneously claiming to follow the school of the prophets, whereas accepting determinism is equivalent to denying the entire content of the prophets' teachings. Neither "duty" would have meaning, nor questioning and answering, nor advice and counsel, and most certainly not reward and punishment! A human would never hesitate in their actions, nor would regret have any meaning, nor the correction of past mistakes.

Then it addresses another important matter in this regard, describing the group who are the people of Paradise and felicity versus the group sent to Hell with this expression: "But He admits whom He wills into His mercy, and for the wrongdoers there is no protector or helper" (وَ لكِنْ يُدْخِلُ مَنْ يَشاءُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ وَ الظَّالِمُونَ ما لَهُمْ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَ لا نَصِيرٍ).

Given that the group destined for Hell is identified by the description "wrongdoing" (dhulm), it becomes clear that "whom He wills" (man yasha) in the first clause refers to the group who are not wrongdoers. Thus, the "just" are the people of Paradise, immersed in Allah's mercy, and the wrongdoers are the people of Hell.

However, it must be noted that "wrongdoer" (dhalim) here, and in many other verses of the Quran, has a broad and extensive meaning. it does not only include those who have oppressed others, but also those who have wronged themselves or those who are deviant in belief are also wrongdoers. And what wrongdoing is greater than polytheism and disbelief? Luqman says to his son: “O my son, do not associate anything with Allah; indeed, polytheism is a great wrongdoing” (Luqman: 13).

In another verse we read: “Unquestionably, the curse of Allah is upon the wrongdoers, who avert [people] from the way of Allah and seek to make it [seem] deviant while they are disbelievers in the Hereafter.”

Regarding the difference between "Protector" (Wali) and "Helper" (Nasir), some have said a Wali is one who helps a person without being asked, while Nasir is a more general concept. [1] It is also possible that Wali refers to a guardian who, by virtue of authority and without request, provides protection and aid, while Nasir is a rescuer who rushes to a person's aid after a request for help.

[1] Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 279 (under verse 22 of Al-Ankabut).

Ayahs

أَمِ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۖ فَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلْوَلِىُّ وَهُوَ يُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٩)
Have they taken awliya besides Him? [Say,] ‘It is Allah, who is the [true] Wali, and He revives the dead, and He has power over all things. (9)
وَمَا ٱخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِيهِ مِن شَىْءٍ فَحُكْمُهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبِّى عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ (١٠)
Whatever thing you may differ about, its judgement is with Allah. That is Allah, my Lord. In Him, I have put my trust, and to Him do I turn penitently. (10)
فَاطِرُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا وَمِنَ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ أَزْوَٰجًا ۖ يَذْرَؤُكُمْ فِيهِ ۚ لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِۦ شَىْءٌ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ (١١)
The originator of the heavens and the earth, He made for you mates from your own selves, and mates of the cattle, by which means He multiplies you. Nothing is like Him, and He is the All-hearing, the All-seeing. (11)
لَهُۥ مَقَالِيدُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (١٢)
To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth: He expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it [for whomever He wishes]. Indeed He has knowledge of all things.’ (12)

Commentary

Allah is the Absolute Guardian

Since the final verse of the previous discussion established the reality that there is no guardian or helper besides Allah, the verses under discussion present living proofs to confirm this reality and negate the guardianship of anyone other than God.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 20, p. 363

In a tone of wonder and denunciation, He asks: "Have they taken guardians besides Him?" (أَمِ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ) [1], while "Allah—He is the [only] Guardian" (فَاللَّهُ هُوَ الْوَلِيُّ).

Therefore, if they wish to choose a guardian and protector for themselves, they must choose Allah.

This is because the proofs of His guardianship were made clear through the description of His perfect attributes in the preceding verses: the God who is Mighty and Wise, the God who is the Owner, the Most High, and the Great, the Lord who is Oft-Forgiving and Merciful. These seven attributes mentioned previously are themselves the best evidence for the exclusivity of guardianship to Him.

Then, moving to another proof, He says: "And He it is who gives life to the dead" (وَ هُوَ يُحْيِي الْمَوْتى).

And since the Resurrection and the Return are in His hands, and man's greatest concern is the nature of his life after death, one must cling to His exalted mantle and none other.

Then, mentioning a third proof, He says: "And He is Powerful over all things" (وَ هُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

This indicates that the primary condition for a "Guardian" is the possession of power, and the True Powerful One is Him.

[^1]: Some commentators, such as al-Zamakhshari in al-Kashshaf and Fakhr al-Razi in al-Tafsir al-Kabir, have taken "am" (أَمْ) here to mean a rhetorical question of denial, while others like Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan and Qurtubi in al-Jami' li-Ahkam al-Quran have taken it to mean "bal" (nay/rather).

In the next verse, He explains the fourth proof of His guardianship as follows: "In whatever you differ, its judgment and ruling is with Allah" (وَ مَا اخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِيهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فَحُكْمُهُ إِلَى اللَّهِ); He alone can put an end to your disputes.

Indeed, one of the functions of guardianship is the ability to end the disputes of those under one's care through correct judgment. Do the idols and devils who have been taken as deities have the capacity for such a task? Or is this work exclusive to the God who is both Wise and aware of the ways to resolve every kind of difference, and is also capable of executing His ruling and judgment? Therefore, the Mighty and Wise God must be the Judge, not anyone else.

Although some commentators have sought to limit the concept of "whatever you differ in" to differences in the interpretation of ambiguous verses, or only to legal disputes and differences, the concept of the verse is broad. Any kind of difference that arises among humans—whether in divine knowledge and beliefs, legislative rulings, legal and judicial matters, or otherwise—must be resolved through the source of the effusion of Divine Knowledge and via Revelation, as human knowledge is limited and meager.

After mentioning these various proofs for the exclusivity of the station of guardianship to the Pure Essence of Allah, He says through the words of His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "That is Allah, my Lord, with these perfect attributes" (ذلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبِّي) [1], "and for this reason, I have chosen Him as my Guardian and Helper; in Him I have put my trust..."

[^1]: At the beginning of this sentence, the word "Qul" (Say) is implied. Therefore, only this sentence and what follows it are uttered by the Prophet, while the sentence "And in whatever you differ..." is a continuation of the Lord's words. Those who have chosen otherwise have apparently not followed the correct path.

"...and to Him I turn in difficulties, hardships, and lapses" (عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَ إِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ).

It is noteworthy that the phrase "That is Allah, my Lord" refers to the absolute Lordship (Rububiyyah) of Allah—that is, ownership coupled with management. We know that Lordship has two branches:

The creative branch (takwini), which relates to the management of the system of creation, and the legislative branch (tashri'i), which expresses rulings, the establishment of laws, and the guidance of people through Divine Messengers.

On this basis, the two issues of "trust" (tawakkul) and "turning" (inabah) are raised subsequently; the first is the entrusting of one's affairs in the creative system to Allah, and the second is the returning to Him in legislative matters (reflect on this). [1]

The next verse can be a fifth proof of the absolute guardianship of the Lord, or a proof of His station of Lordship and His worthiness for trust and turning. He says: "He is the Originator of the heavens and the earth" (فاطِرُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ). "Fatir" comes from the root "fatr," which originally means splitting something. This is in contrast to "qatt," which some say means a transverse cut. It is as if, at the time of the creation of beings, the dark veil of non-existence is split, and existences emerge from it. For this reason, when the sheath of a date cluster splits and the cluster emerges, it is called "fatar." [2] Of course, the meaning of "heavens and earth" here includes all the heavens and the earth and the beings that exist within and between them, for Allah's creativity encompasses all of them.

Then, moving to another description of His actions, He says: "He has made for you spouses from among yourselves, and of the cattle pairs; He multiplies you thereby" (جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً وَ مِنَ الْأَنْعامِ أَزْواجاً يَذْرَؤُكُمْ فِيهِ) [1]. This is itself one of the great signs of the Lord's management, Lordship, and guardianship—that He created for humans spouses of their own kind, who are on one hand a source of peace for the soul and spirit, and on the other, a means for the survival of the race, reproduction, and the continuation of existence.

Although the Quran, in view of the address "He multiplies you" (yadhra'ukum), expresses this meaning regarding humans, it goes without saying that this rule also applies to cattle and other living beings in terms of reproduction. In reality, Allah did not wish to combine them in one address and diminish man's high status; thus, He addressed humans alone, so that the rule for the rest becomes clear by following the example of humans.

In the third description mentioned in this verse, He says: "There is nothing like unto Him" (لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ).

This sentence is, in fact, the primary foundation for knowing all the attributes of Allah; without paying attention to it, one cannot grasp any of the Lord's attributes. This is because the most dangerous precipice on the path of those seeking "Knowledge of Allah" (Ma'rifat Allah) is the precipice of "anthropomorphism" (tashbih)—considering Allah similar to His creatures in any of His attributes. This leads one to fall into the "abyss of polytheism" (shirk).

In other words, He is a Being who is infinite and unlimited in every respect, while everything other than Him is limited and finite in every respect—in terms of lifespan, power, knowledge, life, will, action, and in short, everything. This is the line of "transcendence" (tanzih) and deeming Allah pure from the deficiencies of contingent beings.

For this reason, many concepts that are established for other than Allah have no meaning at all regarding His Pure Essence. For example, some tasks are "easy" for us and some are "hard"; some objects are "far" from us and some are "near"; some events occurred in the "past" and some occur in the "present" or "future"; likewise, some things are "small" and some are "large." This is because our existence is limited, and these concepts arise by comparing other beings with it. But for a Being who is infinite in every respect and encompasses pre-eternity and post-eternity, these meanings cannot be imagined. There is no far or near for Him—all are near; there is no difficulty or ease—all are easy; there is no future or past—all is "present" for Him. It is noteworthy that understanding these meanings requires precision and emptying the mind of what it is accustomed to.

For this reason, we say: knowing the principle of Allah's existence is easy, but knowing His attributes is difficult! The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), says in Nahj al-Balagha:

"The great and the subtle, the heavy and the light, the strong and the weak, are all equal in His creation." [1]

And at the end of the verse, expressing other attributes of His Holy Essence, He says: "And He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing" (وَ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ).

Yes, He is both the Creator and the Manager; He is both All-Hearing and All-Seeing, and yet He has no likeness, peer, or equal. For this reason, one must place oneself only under the shadow of His guardianship and Lordship, and remove the yoke of servitude to any other.

In the final verse under discussion, there is mention of three other parts of the attributes of action and essence of the Lord, each showing the issue of His guardianship and Lordship in a specific dimension:

First, He says: "To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth" (لَهُ مَقَالِيدُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

Therefore, whatever anyone has is from Him, and whatever one wants, one must ask of Him. Not only the "keys" but also the "treasures" of the heavens and the earth belong to Him: "And to Allah belong the treasures of the heavens and the earth" (Al-Munafiqun: 7).

"Maqalid" is the plural of "miqlid," meaning key. This word is often used metaphorically to mean complete control over something. It is said, "The key to this matter is in my hand," meaning the way, the plan, and the conditions for its success are all at my disposal. (We have provided a more detailed discussion on the root of this word and its characteristics under verse 63 of Surah Az-Zumar in Volume 19).

In the subsequent description, which is in fact a result of the previous one, He adds:

"He expands the provision for whom He wills and restricts it" (يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَقْدِرُ).

Since the treasures of the world are in His hand, all provisions and sustenance are also within the grasp of His power, and He distributes them according to His will, which stems from His wisdom and in which the interests of His servants are considered.

And since providing for all living beings requires knowledge and awareness of the amount, needs, location, and other characteristics, in the final description He adds: "Indeed, He is Knowing of all things" (إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ).

This is exactly like the point mentioned in verse 6 of Surah Hud: "And there is no creature on earth but that upon Allah is its provision, and He knows its dwelling place and its storage; all is in a clear register."

Thus, in the four verses under discussion, eleven other perfect attributes of the Lord (including attributes of essence and attributes of action) have been stated:

The attribute of His absolute guardianship, the reviving of the dead, power over all things, the creativity of the heavens and the earth, the creation of spouses and the multiplication of humans, the non-existence of a likeness or peer for Him, being All-Hearing, being All-Seeing, authority over the treasures of heaven and earth, being the Provider, and His knowledge of all things.

These attributes are complementary to one another in their expression, and all are proofs of His guardianship and Lordship, and consequently, a path for proving the Oneness of Worship (Tawhid al-Ibadah).

Key Points

1- Knowing the Attributes of Allah

Since our knowledge—indeed, our entire existence—is limited, we can never reach the core and reality of the Essence of Allah, which is infinite. This is because awareness of the core of something means encompassing it. How can a limited being encompass an infinite Essence? Likewise, knowing the core of Allah's "attributes," which are identical to His Essence, is also impossible for a limited being such as us.

Therefore, what we know of the Essence and attributes of Allah is a general knowledge, and it mostly revolves around His effects.

On the other hand, because our words were coined to meet the needs of our daily lives, they can never express the infinite Essence and attributes of the Truth. Thus, the words knowledge, power, life, guardianship, ownership, and other words that express His positive and negative attributes take on a different color. It is here that we sometimes encounter expressions that, at a superficial glance, seem contradictory, but upon reflection, it becomes clear that they all express one reality.

For example, we say Allah is both the "First" and the "Last," both the "Manifest" and the "Hidden." He is with everything, but not joined with them; and He is separate from everything, but not alienated from them.

Of course, if we speak with the criteria of the concepts of these words regarding limited and contingent beings, that which is first cannot be last, and that which is manifest cannot be hidden. But when we contemplate these words in the horizon of His infinite Essence, we see they all come together, for an infinite being is the Last while being the First, and the Hidden while being the Manifest.

It is here that we say the important thing in knowing His attributes of Beauty (Jamal) and Glory (Jalal) is to pay attention to this reality: "There is nothing like unto Him, and He is not similar to anything" (لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ).

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), expressed this reality clearly in the sermons of Nahj al-Balagha, where he says:

"He who assigns Him a quality does not believe in His Oneness, and he who assigns Him a likeness has not reached the reality of His Essence. And whoever likens Him to something has not intended Him. And he who points to Him or imagines Him in his thought has not deemed Him free from dimensions!" [1]

In another place, he says:

"Everything called 'one' other than Him is little." [2] (Meaning its unity implies limitation, whereas His Unity implies infinite greatness).

In short, in the chapter of Allah's attributes, one must always move with the lamp of "There is nothing like unto Him" and look at His Pure Essence in the light of "Nor is there to Him any equivalent" (Surah al-Ikhlas). The expression "Subhan Allah" (Glory be to Allah) in worship and otherwise is an allusion to this very reality.

2- A Linguistic Point

Given that the "kaf" in the sentence "Laysa ka-mithlihi shay'" is the "kaf" of comparison and means "like," the sentence would mean: "There is nothing like His likeness." This repetition has led many commentators to consider the "kaf" as redundant (za'idah), which is usually used for emphasis and is frequent in the speech of the eloquent.

However, there is a more subtle interpretation here: sometimes it is said, "The like of you does not flee from the field of events," meaning "Someone like you, with this courage, this intellect, and wisdom, should not flee." (In short, one who possesses your attributes must be such and such).

In the verse under discussion, the meaning becomes: The like of Allah, with these attributes that were mentioned—with this vast knowledge and great, infinite power—will have no equal.

It is also noteworthy that, according to some linguists, we have several terms that all convey the meaning of "likeness," but none have its conceptual comprehensiveness:

"Nidd" is used when only similarity in substance and essence is intended. "Shabah" is used when only quality is being discussed. "Musawi" is used only when quantity is the subject. "Shakl" is used where measure and area are involved.

But "mithl" (likeness) has a broad and general concept that encompasses all these meanings. Therefore, when Allah intends to negate every kind of peer and likeness from His Essence, He says: "There is nothing like unto Him" (لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ). [1]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 186. [^2]: Sermon 65. [^1]: Mufradat al-Raghib, root "m-th-l".

3- A Few Reminders Regarding Allah's Provision

A- The Criterion for the Expansion and Restriction of Provision: One should never imagine that the vastness of provision is a proof of Allah's love, or that the constriction of livelihood is a proof of His anger and wrath. For Allah sometimes tests man with the vastness of provision and places abundant wealth at his disposal, and sometimes clarifies the extent of his resistance and steadfastness through the constriction of livelihood, nurturing them through this path.

Sometimes great wealth is a source of calamity and torment for the souls of its owners, depriving them of all peace and rest, as the Holy Quran says in verse 55 of Surah at-Tawbah: "So let not their wealth or their children impress you. Allah only intends to punish them through them in worldly life and that their souls should depart while they are disbelievers!"

Elsewhere it says: "Do they think that what We extend to them of wealth and children—is it that We hasten for them in good things? Rather, they do not perceive" (Al-Mu'minun: 55-56).

B- The Ordainment of Provision Does Not Contradict Effort: One should not infer from the verses regarding the ordainment and measuring of provision by the Lord that efforts and endeavors have no role in this field, nor use them as an excuse for laziness and escaping responsibilities and struggles on an individual or social scale. Such a notion is contrary to numerous verses of the Holy Quran that consider endeavor and effort as the criteria for success.

The goal is that despite all efforts and endeavors, we still clearly see another hand at work—where sometimes the result of efforts is blown away and sometimes the opposite—so that people do not forget that behind the world of causes, the hand of power of the "Causer of Causes" is working. In any case, deprivations resulting from laziness and lethargy should never be blamed on the distribution of provision from Allah's side, for He Himself has said that He expands provision in proportion to efforts.

C- Provision is Not Only Material Bounties: Provision (rizq) has a broad meaning that also includes spiritual provisions; in fact, the primary provision is this spiritual one. In supplications, the term rizq is frequently used for spiritual blessings. Regarding the Hajj, we say: "O Allah, grant me the provision (urzuqni) of pilgrimage to Your Sacred House."

Regarding the success of obedience and distance from sin, it is narrated: "O Allah, grant me the provision of the success of obedience and distance from disobedience..."

In the supplications for the days of Ramadan, we read: "O Allah, grant me therein the provision of the obedience of the humble" (Supplication for the 15th day), and likewise regarding other spiritual bounties.

D- The Quran and the Causes of Increased Provision: The Quran introduces several matters that are themselves a constructive lesson for human education. In one place it says: "If you are grateful, I will surely increase you [in favor]" (Ibrahim: 7).

In another place, it invites people to effort, saying: "It is He who made the earth tame for you—so walk among its slopes and eat of His provision" (Al-Mulk: 15).

In another place, it makes piety and righteousness the criterion for the opening of provision, saying: "And if only the people of the cities had believed and feared Allah, We would have opened upon them blessings from the heaven and the earth" (Al-A'raf: 96).

E- Constriction of Provision and Educational Issues: Sometimes the constriction of provision is to prevent the rebellion of the people, as we read in verse 27 of Ash-Shura (this very Surah): "And if Allah had extended provision for His servants, they would have tyrannized throughout the earth!"

F- The Quran emphasizes that humans should consider only Allah as their Provider and not request from others, and following this faith and trust, they should rely on their own strength, effort, and endeavor. In verse 3 of Fatir, it says: "Is there any creator other than Allah who provides for you from the heaven and earth?" and in verse 17 of al-Ankabut we read: "So seek from Allah provision."

In this way, it revives the spirit of self-sufficiency, the abandonment of dependency, and self-respect in humans.

Regarding the distribution of provisions, the struggle for life, and the causes and sources of provision, we had detailed discussions in Volume 11, page 310 onwards (under verse 71 of an-Nahl) and in Volume 9, page 18 onwards (under verse 6 of Hud).

Ayahs

۞ شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِۦ نُوحًا وَٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِۦٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰٓ ۖ أَنْ أَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ كَبُرَ عَلَى ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ مَا تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَيْهِ ۚ ٱللَّهُ يَجْتَبِىٓ إِلَيْهِ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِىٓ إِلَيْهِ مَن يُنِيبُ (١٣)
He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have [also] revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, declaring, ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided in it.’ Hard on the polytheists is that to which you summon them. Allah chooses for it whomever He wishes, and He guides to it whomever returns penitently [to Him]. (13)
وَمَا تَفَرَّقُوٓا۟ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى لَّقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُورِثُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ (١٤)
They did not divide [into sects] except after the knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves; and were it not for a prior decree of your Lord [granting them reprieve] until a specified time, decision would have been made between them. Indeed those who were made heirs to the Book after them are in grave doubt concerning it. (14)

Commentary

Your Religion is the Essence of the Religion of All Prophets

Since many of the discussions in this Surah are directed at the polytheists, and the preceding verses also touched upon this subject, the verses under discussion clarify the reality that the Islamic call to monotheism (Tawhid) is not a new invitation; rather, it is the call of all the Ulul-'Azm (Arch-Prophets). Not only the principle of monotheism, but all the fundamental principles of the prophets' missions in core issues have been one and the same across all heavenly religions.

It states: "He has ordained for you of religion what He enjoined upon Noah," the first of the Ulul-'Azm prophets (شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً).

"And that which We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], and what We enjoined upon Abraham and Moses and Jesus" (وَ الَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَ ما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ مُوسى‌ وَ عِيسى‌).

Thus, whatever existed in the laws of all the prophets is present in your Sharia, and "what all the good ones possess, you possess alone!" The expression "of religion" (min al-din) indicates that the harmony of heavenly laws is not limited to the issue of monotheism or the principles of belief; rather, the entire divine religion is one in terms of its foundation and roots everywhere. However, the evolution of human society necessitates that subsidiary legislations and laws evolve in harmony with human progress until they reach the ultimate stage and the Finality of Religions.

For this reason, there are numerous evidences in other verses of the Quran showing that the general principles of beliefs, laws, and duties have been identical in all religions.

For instance, in the accounts of many prophets in the Holy Quran, we read that their first call was: "O my people, worship Allah" [1]. Elsewhere we read: "And We certainly sent into every nation a messenger, [saying], 'Worship Allah...'" (وَ لَقَدْ بَعَثْنا فِي كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولًا أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ).

Warning about the Resurrection is also mentioned in the missions of many prophets (Al-An'am 130, Al-A'raf 59, Ash-Shu'ara 135, Ta-Ha 15, Maryam 31).

Moses, Jesus, and Shu'ayb عليه السلام speak of prayer (Ta-Ha 14, Maryam 31, Hud 87). Abraham invites people to the Hajj (Al-Hajj 27).

And fasting existed among all previous nations (Al-Baqarah 183).

Therefore, in the continuation of the verse, as a general command to all these great prophets, it adds: "We enjoined them all to establish the religion and not be divided therein" (أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَ لا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ‌).

This is a recommendation for two important matters: First, the establishment of God's religion in all fields (not just practicing it, but its iqamah—establishment and revival).

Second, avoiding the great calamity, which is division and hypocrisy in religion.

Following this, it adds: "Though this call of yours is very difficult for the polytheists" (كَبُرَ عَلَى الْمُشْرِكِينَ ما تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَيْهِ‌).

Due to ignorance and long-standing prejudice, they have become so accustomed to polytheism and idol-worship—which has permeated the depths of their being—that the call to monotheism causes them terror. Furthermore, in polytheism, the illegitimate interests of the polytheist leaders are preserved, whereas monotheism leads to the uprising of the oppressed and prevents self-indulgence and their oppressions.

Nevertheless, just as the selection of prophets is in the hands of Allah, the guidance of the people is also in His hands: "Allah chooses for Himself whom He wills and guides to Himself whoever turns back [to Him]" (اللَّهُ يَجْتَبِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَهْدِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ يُنِيبُ‌).

In this verse, there are points that must be noted:

  1. Shara'a (ordained) is derived from the root shar' (rhyming with zar'), which originally means a clear path. The path leading to rivers is also called shari'ah. Subsequently, this word was used for divine religions and heavenly laws because the clear path to prosperity lies within them, and they are the way to reach the "water of life" of faith, piety, peace, and justice.

Since water is the source of cleanliness, purity, and life, this term has a clear affinity with divine religion, which spiritually performs these same functions for the soul and spirit of man and human society [1].

  1. In this verse, only five of Allah's prophets are mentioned (Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله) because the Ulul-'Azm prophets—meaning the possessors of a new religion and law—are only these five. In reality, the verse points to the exclusivity of the law-bearing prophets to these five individuals.

  2. Noah is mentioned first because the first Sharia—meaning a religion containing all types of devotional and social laws—began with him. The prophets before him had limited programs and instructions [2]. For this reason, there is no mention of heavenly books before Noah in the Quran or Islamic traditions.

  3. It is noteworthy that in mentioning these five, Noah عليه السلام is spoken of first, then the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, followed by Abraham عليه السلام, Moses عليه السلام, and Jesus عليه السلام. This arrangement is because Noah is placed first due to being the initiator, and the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله immediately after him due to his greatness, and the others according to their chronological appearance.

  4. Another point of interest is that regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, the expression "We revealed to you" (awhayna ilayk) is used, while for the others, the term "enjoined" (wassayna) is used. Perhaps this difference in expression is due to the significance of Islam relative to other heavenly religions.

  5. At the end of the verse, regarding the manner of choosing prophets, the expression "whom He wills" (man yasha') is used, which is a subtle reference to the inherent merits of the divine messengers.

However, regarding the nations, the expression "whoever turns back" (man yunib—one who returns to Allah, repents from sin, and enters the door of obedience) is used, so that the criteria for divine guidance and its conditions become clear to everyone, and they may find the way to the sea of His mercy.

It is stated in a Hadith Qudsi: "Whoever draws near to Me by a span, I draw near to him by a cubit; and whoever comes to Me walking, I go to him running!" [1]

The possibility has also been suggested in the interpretation of the final sentence that ijtiba' (selection) is not exclusive to prophets but includes all sincere and devoted servants who possessed a high divine station.

Since one of the two pillars of the call of the Ulul-'Azm prophets is the lack of division in religion, and surely all of them preached this matter, the question arises: what is the source of all these religious differences?

The next verse addresses this question, stating the primary source of religious differences: "And they did not become divided until after knowledge had come to them, out of jealous animosity between themselves" (وَ ما تَفَرَّقُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ‌).

Yes, the worldly-minded oppressors and the spiteful envious ones rose up against the unified religion of the prophets and led every group down a different path to strengthen the foundations of their own leadership, secure their worldly interests, and manifest their envy and enmity toward the true believers and the school of the prophets. But all of this happened after the proof had been completed for them.

Thus, the source of religious differences was not ignorance or lack of information, but rather baghy (transgression), oppression, deviation from the truth, and the imposition of personal views.

"World-seeking scholars" and "prejudiced, spiteful commoners" joined hands and laid the foundations of these divisions.

This verse is a clear answer to those who say that religion has created division among mankind and caused much bloodshed throughout history. For if one looks closely, religion has always been a factor for unity and integration in its environment (as happened in the case of Islam and the tribes of the Hejaz and even nations outside the Arabian Peninsula, ending differences and creating a single Ummah).

However, colonial policies created division among the people, fanned the flames of discord, and caused bloodshed. Adding personal tastes and imposing them on heavenly religions was another major factor of division, which also stemmed from baghy.

Baghy, as mentioned in its original linguistic root, is "the desire to transgress and deviate from the middle path and a tendency toward excess or deficiency," whether this desire is acted upon or not. Sometimes it is in the quantity of something, and sometimes in its quality; for this reason, it is most often used to mean oppression and tyranny.

Sometimes it also appears in the sense of any "seeking or requesting," even if it is a good and worthy matter.

Therefore, Raghib in Al-Mufradat divides baghy into two branches: "praiseworthy" and "blameworthy." The first is exceeding the limit of justice to reach excellence (ihsan) and altruism, and exceeding obligations to reach recommended acts (mustahabbat). The second is exceeding the truth and inclining toward falsehood.

Then the Quran adds: "And if it were not for a word that preceded from your Lord [postponing punishment] until a specified term, it would have been settled between them" (وَ لَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِلى‌ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ‌). Allah would have destroyed the followers of falsehood and made the followers of truth victorious.

Yes, the world is a house of trial, nurturing, and perfection, and this is not possible without freedom of action. This is the ontological decree of Allah that has existed since the beginning of human creation and is unchangeable. This is the nature of worldly life. However, one of the distinctions of the Hereafter is the resolution of all these differences and humanity reaching complete unity; hence, the Day of Resurrection is called the "Day of Sorting" (Yawm al-Fasl).

In the final sentence, it explains the state of those who came after this group—those who did not witness the era of the prophets and opened their eyes when the hypocrites and dividers had darkened the atmosphere of human society with their devilish acts, and they were unable to properly perceive the truth.

It says: "And indeed, those who were made to inherit the Scripture after them are in unsettling doubt concerning it" (وَ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ أُورِثُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ لَفِي شَكٍّ مِنْهُ مُرِيبٍ‌) [1]. In the definition of rayb (unsettling doubt), this condition is mentioned: it refers to a doubt that will eventually be unveiled and turn into certainty. Perhaps this refers to the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله with clear proofs, which removed the traces of doubt and suspicion from the hearts of truth-seekers.

Key Point

In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the verse "He has ordained for you of religion..." that he said: The addressee in the phrase "establish the religion" is the Imam, and the phrase "and do not be divided therein" is an allusion to the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام [2].

Obviously, the intention is not to restrict religion to the Wilayah (guardianship) of Ali عليه السلام; rather, the goal is to express the reality that the issue of the Wilayah of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, is also considered one of the pillars of religion.

[^1]: Al-A'raf 59, 65, 73, 85; Hud 50, 61, 84, which appear respectively regarding Noah, Hud, Salih, and Shu'ayb. [^1]: This meaning is mentioned briefly in Lisan al-Arab, Al-Mufradat by Raghib, and other lexicons. [^2]: We have provided an explanation on this in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 213 (Vol. 2, p. 58). [^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr Razi, Vol. 27, p. 157 (under the verses discussed). [^1]: According to this interpretation, which is fully consistent with the preceding sentences, the pronoun "after them" refers to the first nations who created division in religions, not to the prophets mentioned in the previous verse (note carefully). [^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 567.

Ayah

فَلِذَٰلِكَ فَٱدْعُ ۖ وَٱسْتَقِمْ كَمَآ أُمِرْتَ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ ۖ وَقُلْ ءَامَنتُ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِن كِتَـٰبٍ ۖ وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَعْدِلَ بَيْنَكُمُ ۖ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّنَا وَرَبُّكُمْ ۖ لَنَآ أَعْمَـٰلُنَا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ ۖ لَا حُجَّةَ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمُ ۖ ٱللَّهُ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنَا ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٥)
So summon to this [unity of religion], and be steadfast, just as you have been commanded, and do not follow their desires, and say, ‘I believe in whatever Book Allah has sent down. I have been commanded to do justice among you. Allah is our Lord and your Lord. Our deeds belong to us and your deeds belong to you. There is no quarrel between us and you. Allah will bring us together and toward Him is the destination.’ (15)

Commentary

Stand Firm as You Have Been Commanded!

Since the previous verses discussed the issue of division among nations due to rebellion, injustice, and deviation, the verse under discussion commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to strive for the resolution of differences and the revival of the religion of the prophets, and to exercise the utmost steadfastness in this path.

It says: "Invite [mankind] toward the single divine religion, and deliver them from differences" (فَلِذلِکَ فَادْعُ) [^1]. Then, it commands steadfastness in this path, saying: "Stand firm as you have been commanded" (وَ اسْتَقِمْ کَما أُمِرْتَ).

The phrase "کما امرت" (as you have been commanded) may refer to the highest level of steadfastness, or it may indicate that steadfastness—in terms of quantity, quality, duration, and other specifics—must entirely conform to the divine command and program.

And since the whims and desires of the people are among the greatest obstacles in this path, in the third command He adds: "And do not follow their desires" (وَ لا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ).

This is because every group invites you toward their own personal inclinations and interests—the very invitation whose end result is division, scattering, and hypocrisy. Trample upon these desires and gather everyone around the single religion of the Lord.

And because every invitation has a starting point, He makes the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself that starting point, and in the fourth command He says: "Say, 'I have believed in whatever Book Allah has sent down'" (وَ قُلْ آمَنْتُ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ مِنْ کِتابٍ).

I do not discriminate between the Heavenly Books; I recognize them all, and I see them all as inviters toward monotheism, pure religious knowledge, piety, purity, truth, and justice. My religion is, in reality, the comprehensive whole and the completer of them all.

I am not like the People of the Book, where each group negates the other—the Jews negating the Christians and the Christians negating the Jews—and where even the followers of each faith accept only those verses of their own religious books that align with their desires and wants. I have accepted them all without exception, for the fundamental principles of all are one.

And since the "principle of justice" is necessary for creating unity, He raises it in the fifth command, saying: "Say, 'I have been commanded to do justice among you'" (وَ أُمِرْتُ لِأَعْدِلَ بَيْنَکُمُ).

Whether in judgment and arbitration, or in social rights and other matters [^1]. Thus, the verse under discussion consists of five important commands: it begins with the principle of invitation, then mentions the means of its progress (steadfastness), then points to the obstacles in the path (following desires), then states the starting point (which is from oneself), and finally mentions the ultimate goal, which is the expansion and generalization of justice.

Following these five commands, He points to the common aspects of all peoples, which are also summarized in five parts, saying:

"Allah is our Lord and your Lord" (اللَّهُ رَبُّنا وَ رَبُّکُمْ).

"For us are our deeds, and for you are your deeds, and each is responsible for his own actions" (لَنا أَعْمالُنا وَ لَکُمْ أَعْمالُکُمْ).

"There is no [need for] dispute between us and you, and none has an advantage over the other, and we have no personal grudge against you" (لا حُجَّةَ بَيْنَنا وَ بَيْنَکُمُ).

[^1]: A group of commentators have taken the "Lam" in "لذلك" to mean "toward" (ila), while others have taken it to mean "causality" (ta'lil). In the first case, "ذلك" refers to the religion of the previous prophets, and in the second case, it refers to the divisions among nations.

[^1]: A group of commentators have limited "justice" here to judicial judgment, while there is no evidence for such a limitation in the verse.

In principle, there is no need for argumentation and reasoning because the truth has become sufficiently clear. Furthermore, eventually we will all be gathered in one place, and "Allah will bring us together on the Day of Resurrection" (اللَّهُ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنا) [^1] and the Judge of us all on that day is One; indeed, "to Him is the final return" (وَ إِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ).

In this way, our God is One, our destination is one, and the Judge of the court and the authority of our affairs is one. Moreover, everyone is responsible for their own deeds, and no one has an advantage over another except through faith and pure action.

We conclude this discussion by mentioning a comprehensive Hadith: In a narration from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"Three things are saviors, and three things are destroyers. As for the saviors: justice in [times of] pleasure and anger, moderation in wealth and poverty, and the fear of Allah in secret and in public. And as for the destroyers: greed that is obeyed, desire that is followed, and a person’s conceit regarding himself." [^2]

[^1]: The first-person plural pronoun in "بيننا" refers to the Prophet and the believers, and the plural pronoun in "بينكم" refers to all the disbelievers, including the People of the Book and the polytheists.

[^2]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verses discussed, and Tuhaf al-'Uqul, the words of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ يُحَآجُّونَ فِى ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ٱسْتُجِيبَ لَهُۥ حُجَّتُهُمْ دَاحِضَةٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ (١٦)
Those who argue concerning Allah, after His call has been answered, their argument stands refuted with their Lord, and upon them shall be [His] wrath, and there is a severe punishment for them. (16)
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ ۗ وَمَا يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ قَرِيبٌ (١٧)
It is Allah who has sent down the Book with the truth and [He has sent down] the Balance. What do you know—maybe the Hour is near! (17)
يَسْتَعْجِلُ بِهَا ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهَا ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مُشْفِقُونَ مِنْهَا وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهَا ٱلْحَقُّ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُمَارُونَ فِى ٱلسَّاعَةِ لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (١٨)
Those who do not believe in it ask [you] to hasten it, but those who have faith are apprehensive of it, and know that it is true. Indeed those who are in doubt about the Hour are in extreme error! (18)

Commentary

Do Not Hasten, the Resurrection is Coming!

Since the previous verses mentioned that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was commanded to implement justice among all people while respecting the content of the Heavenly Books and to cease all hostility and disputation with them, the verses under discussion complete this discourse. To show that the truthfulness of the Prophet of Islam needs no further proof, He says: "And those who argue concerning Allah after He has been responded to—their argument is invalid in the sight of their Lord" (وَ الَّذِينَ يُحَاجُّونَ فِي اللَّهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما اسْتُجِيبَ لَهُ حُجَّتُهُمْ داحِضَةٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ).

"And upon them is [His] wrath" (because they continue their opposition out of knowledge and intent) (وَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ).

"And for them is a severe punishment" in the Hereafter (وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ), for stubbornness and enmity yield no other fruit.

Regarding what is meant by the phrase "مِنْ بَعْدِ ما اسْتُجِيبَ لَهُ" (after He has been responded to), commentators have mentioned various interpretations:

Sometimes it is said that it refers to the response of the masses of pure-hearted and unbiased people who, inspired by their primordial nature (Fitrah) and witnessing the content of the revelation and the various miracles of the Prophet of Islam, bowed in submission to him.

At other times, it is mentioned as the answering of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) prayer against the opponents on the day of the Battle of Badr, which led to the destruction of a large portion of their army and the shattering of their grandeur.

And sometimes it is considered an allusion to the acceptance by the People of the Book themselves, who, before the appearance of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), were awaiting him and would read his signs to the people from their own books, expressing affection and faith toward him. However, after the emergence of Islam, because their illegitimate interests were endangered, they took the path of denial.

The first interpretation is the most appropriate, because the second interpretation would require these verses to have been revealed after the Battle of Badr, while there is no clear evidence for this, and it appears that all these verses were revealed in Mecca.

The third interpretation does not align with the tone of the verse, as it would have to say: "min ba'di istajabu lahu" (after they responded to him).

Furthermore, the literal meaning of the phrase "يُحَاجُّونَ فِي اللَّهِ" (they argue concerning Allah) refers to the polytheists' discourse regarding God, not the People of the Book regarding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). But what issues does this "invalid argument" refer to? This is again a matter of discussion.

Some have said: It refers to the claim of the Jews who said, "Our religion existed before Islam and is superior to it." Or: "Since you claim monotheism, come and accept the religion of Moses (عليه السلام) which is accepted by both parties."

However, as we have said, it is unlikely that the address in these verses is to the Jews and the People of the Book, because "arguing concerning Allah" is more consistent with the polytheists. Therefore, the above phrase refers to the baseless and decayed arguments the polytheists brought for accepting shirk (polytheism), including the intercession of idols or following the religion of their ancestors.

In any case, stubborn individuals who continue their obstinacy and persistence after the truth has become clear are disgraced in the eyes of God's creation and are subject to the Creator's wrath in this world and the next.

Then, He addresses one of the proofs of Monotheism and the power of the Lord—which simultaneously implies the proof of Prophethood—against the illogical disputants, saying:

"It is Allah who has sent down the Book in truth and [also] the balance" (اللَّهُ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ وَ الْمِيزانَ).

"Truth" (Haqq) is a comprehensive word that includes true knowledge and beliefs, as well as correct reports and programs harmonized with primordial and social needs, and the like. This is because truth is that which corresponds to external reality and is realized, not something subjective or imaginary.

Similarly, "Balance" (Mizan) in such contexts has a comprehensive meaning. Although its literal meaning is "scale" and a tool for measuring weight, in a metaphorical sense, it is applied to every criterion of measurement, the correct Divine law, and even the person of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the true Imams (علیهم السلام) whose existence is the criterion for distinguishing truth from falsehood. The Balance of the Day of Resurrection is also an instance of this meaning.

In this way, Allah has sent down a Book to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) which is both the truth and the balance for evaluating values. Thus, reflecting on the content of this Book—from its knowledge and beliefs to its methods of logical reasoning, and from its social laws to the programs designed for the purification of souls and the perfection of humans—is proof of its veracity. This sublime content, with such depth and grandeur, coming from an unlettered individual who rose from the most backward of environments, is itself a proof of the grandeur of the Lord and the existence of the metaphysical world, as well as a proof of the truthfulness of the one who brought it.

Thus, the above phrase is an answer for both the polytheists and the People of the Book.

And since the result of all these matters, especially the full manifestation of truth, justice, and the balance, occurs in the Resurrection, He says at the end of the verse: "And what will make you know? Perhaps the Hour is near" (وَ ما يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّ السَّاعَةَ قَرِيبٌ).

The same Resurrection which, when it is established, everyone will appear in its court of justice and stand before a balance of measurement that precisely weighs even the weight of a mustard seed or smaller.

Then, He addresses the stance of the disbelievers and the believers toward it, saying: "Those who do not believe in it are impatient for it, [saying: 'When will this Resurrection come?']" (يَسْتَعْجِلُ بِهَا الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِها).

They never say this out of love for the Resurrection or to reach the meeting with the Beloved; rather, they make such a request out of mockery, ridicule, and denial. If they knew what the Resurrection would bring upon them, they would never make such a request.

"But those who believe are fearful of it and know that it is the truth" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مُشْفِقُونَ مِنْها وَ يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهَا الْحَقُّ) [1]. Of course, the moment of the Resurrection's occurrence is hidden from everyone, even from the Messenger Prophets and the archangels of God, so that it may be a means of continuous training for the believers and a test and completion of proof for the deniers; however, the fact of its occurrence is beyond doubt.

From this, it becomes clear what a deep educational impact faith in the Resurrection and the great court of Divine justice has on believers, especially considering the meaning that its occurrence is possible at any moment.

At the end of the verse, as a public declaration, He says: "Unquestionably, those who dispute concerning the Hour are in extreme error" (أَلا إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُمارُونَ فِي السَّاعَةِ لَفِي ضَلالٍ بَعِيدٍ).

This is because the system of this world is itself a proof that it is a prelude to another world, without which the creation of this world would be vain and meaningless, consistent neither with Allah's wisdom nor His justice.

The expression "extreme error" (dalalin ba'id) indicates that sometimes a person loses the way but is not far from it; with a little effort and searching, they might find the path. But sometimes the distance is so great that finding the way becomes difficult or impossible for them.

It is noteworthy that in a Hadith from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read:

"A man asked the Messenger of Allah in a loud voice during one of his journeys, saying: 'O Muhammad!...' The Prophet also replied in a loud voice like his own: 'What do you say?' He asked: Mata al-sa'ah? 'When will the Day of Resurrection be?' The Prophet said: 'It is coming; what have you prepared for it?' He replied: 'Love for Allah and His Messenger!' The Prophet said: 'You will be with the one you love!'" [1]

[^1]: "Mushfiqun" is from the root "ishfaq," meaning affection coupled with fear. Whenever it is transitive with "min," the aspect of fear predominates in it, and whenever it is transitive with "'ala," the issue of attention and care will predominate; hence a person says to his friend, "ana mushfiqun 'alayka" (Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani and Mufradat al-Raghib).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 25, p. 32.

Ayahs

ٱللَّهُ لَطِيفٌۢ بِعِبَادِهِۦ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ (١٩)
Allah is all-attentive to His servants. He provides for whomever He wishes, and He is the All-strong, the All-mighty. (19)
