مَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ نَزِدْ لَهُۥ فِى حَرْثِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ ٱلدُّنْيَا نُؤْتِهِۦ مِنْهَا وَمَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِن نَّصِيبٍ (٢٠)
Whoever desires the tillage of the Hereafter, We will enhance for him his tillage, and whoever desires the tillage of the world, We will give it to him, but he will have no share in the Hereafter. (20)

Commentary

The Field of This World and the Hereafter

Since the preceding verses discussed Allah’s severe punishment, and a demand was made by the deniers of the Resurrection as to why the Day of Judgment is not established sooner, the first verse under discussion—in order to blend that "wrath" with "grace" and to respond to the meaningless haste of the deniers—states: "Allah is Subtle with His servants, and possesses grace and mercy" (اللَّهُ لَطِيفٌ بِعِبادِهِ).

If He threatens severe punishment in one place, He promises grace in another—a grace that is limitless and vast. And if He does not hasten the punishment of the arrogant ignorant, that too is from His grace.

Then, He mentions one of the great manifestations of His deep grace, which is His expansive provision, saying: "He gives provision to whom He wills" (يَرْزُقُ مَنْ يَشاءُ).

The meaning is not that a group is deprived of His provision, but rather it refers to the expansion of provision for whomever He wills, as stated in Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 26: اللَّهُ يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَقْدِرُ: "Allah extends provision for whom He wills and restricts it."

A few verses later in this same Surah, we also read that the expansion and limitation of His provision follow a specific calculation, because "if Allah were to extend provision for all His servants, they would surely rebel on the earth": "وَ لَوْ بَسَطَ اللَّهُ الرِّزْقَ لِعِبادِهِ لَبَغَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ" (Verse 27 of this Surah).

It is clear that "provision" (rizq) here encompasses both spiritual and material provisions, both physical and soulful. When He is the source of grace and the Provider, why then do you turn to idols that are neither providers nor subtle, who neither solve anyone's problems nor grant any openings?! At the end of the verse, He adds: "And He is the Powerful, the Exalted in Might" (وَ هُوَ الْقَوِيُّ الْعَزِيزُ).

If He promises provision and grace to His servants, He is capable of fulfilling this matter, and for this reason, there is never any breach in His promises.

It is necessary to note that "Latif" (Subtle/Gracious) has two meanings: one is what we mentioned above, meaning the Possessor of grace, love, and mercy; the other is being aware of precise and hidden matters. Since providing for all servants requires being aware of all of them in every corner across the heavens and the earth, He first refers to His being "Latif" and then to His status as the Provider (Razzaq). Thus, in Surah Hud, verse 6, after saying: "The provision of every creature on earth is upon Allah," He adds: وَ يَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّها وَ مُسْتَوْدَعَها "He knows its dwelling place and its repository."

Of course, there is not only no contradiction between these two meanings, but they complement each other. The "Latif" is one who is perfect both in terms of knowledge and in terms of grace and love toward the servants. Since Allah is both well-aware of the needs of His servants and responds to their needs in the best way, He is the most deserving of this name.

In any case, the above verse refers to four attributes of the Lord: the station of Grace (Lutf), Provision (Razzaqiyyah), Power (Quwwah), and Might (Izzah), which are the best evidence for the station of "Rububiyyah" (Lordship), because the "Rabb" (Owner and Regulator) must possess these attributes.

In the next verse, with a subtle metaphor, He compares the people of the world—in relation to the Lord's provisions and how they utilize them—to farmers, some of whom sow for the Hereafter and some for this world, and He specifies the result of each of these two harvests, saying: "Whosoever desires the harvest of the Hereafter, We give him increase in his harvest" (مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ الْآخِرَةِ نَزِدْ لَهُ فِي حَرْثِهِ).

"And those who sow only for this world, and whose effort and striving are for the enjoyment of this fleeting and transitory gain, We give them only a little of what they seek, but in the Hereafter they shall have no portion or share" (وَ مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ الدُّنْيا نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها وَ ما لَهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنْ نَصِيبٍ) [1]. This is a fascinating metaphor and a beautiful allusion: all humans are farmers and this world is our field, our actions are its seeds, and divine facilities are the rain that falls upon it. However, these seeds are very different; the harvest of some is unlimited, eternal, and its trees are always lush, green, and fruitful, while the harvest of others is very meager, short-lived, and fleeting, with bitter and unpleasant fruits.

The expression "yurid" (desires/intends) is actually an allusion to the difference in people's intentions, and the entire verse is an explanation regarding what appeared in the previous verse about the bounties and provisions of the Lord—that one group uses these bounties as seeds for the Hereafter and another group for the enjoyment of this world.

Interestingly, regarding the sowers of the Hereafter, He says: نَزِدْ لَهُ فِي حَرْثِهِ (We increase his harvest), but He does not say they are deprived of the enjoyment of this world. However, regarding the sowers of this world, He says: "We give them a portion of what they desire, then He adds that in the Hereafter they have no share."

In this way, neither do the world-seekers reach all they desire, nor are the seekers of the Hereafter deprived of the world; but with the difference that the first group enters the abode of the Hereafter empty-handed, while the second group enters with full hands! A similar meaning appears in Surah Al-Isra, verses 18 and 19, in another form:

"Whoever desires the immediate [life], We hasten for him from it what We will to whom We intend. Then We have made for him Hell, which he will [enter to] burn, censured and banished.

[1]: The word "Harth," as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, originally means scattering seed in the ground and preparing it for cultivation. In the Holy Quran, it is frequently used in this sense, but it is unclear why a group of commentators have interpreted it as "action and earning"?!

And whoever desires the Hereafter and exerts the effort due to it while he is a believer—it is those whose effort will be appreciated."

The expression "نَزِدْ لَهُ فِي حَرْثِهِ" (We increase for him in his harvest) is consistent with what has appeared in other verses of the Quran, including: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها: "Whoever comes [on the Day of Judgment] with a good deed will have ten times the like thereof" (Al-An'am: 160) and "لِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَ يَزِيدَهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ": "That He may give them in full their rewards and afford them more out of His grace" (Fatir: 30).

In any case, the above verse is a vivid depiction of the Islamic vision regarding the life of this world: the world that is desired for its own sake, and the world that is a prelude to the other world and desired for the sake of something else. Islam views the world as a field whose harvest is reaped in the Resurrection.

The expressions found in the language of narrations or some other verses of the Quran are a confirmation and emphasis of this same meaning.

For example, in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 261, those who spend are compared to a seed from which seven ears grow, and in each ear a hundred grains, and sometimes more; this is an example of sowing for the Hereafter.

And in a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "And does anything cast people on their faces into the Fire except the harvests of their tongues?" [1]

And in another Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, it is stated: "Indeed, wealth and children are the harvest of this world, and righteous deeds are the harvest of the Hereafter, and sometimes Allah gathers both of them for certain peoples." [2]

This point can also be derived from the above verse: that both the world and the Hereafter require effort, striving, and toil, and neither is obtained without trouble and hardship, just as no seed or harvest exists without toil. How much better it is for a person to nurture a tree with his toil whose fruit is sweet, eternal, permanent, and lasting, rather than a tree that soon withers and is destroyed.

We conclude this discussion with a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: He said: "Whoever's intention is this world, Allah scatters his affairs, places poverty before his eyes, and nothing of the world comes to him except what was written for him. And whoever's intention is the abode of the Hereafter, Allah gathers his affairs, places richness in his heart, and the world comes to him submissively!" [3]

The famous saying among scholars, "The world is the field of the Hereafter" (الدنيا مزرعة الآخرة), is in fact an adaptation of the sum of the above statements.

[1]: Mahajjat al-Bayda, Vol. 5, p. 193 (Book of the Afflictions of the Tongue). [2]: Al-Kafi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 569. [3]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed.

Ayahs

أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكَـٰٓؤُا۟ شَرَعُوا۟ لَهُم مِّنَ ٱلدِّينِ مَا لَمْ يَأْذَنۢ بِهِ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةُ ٱلْفَصْلِ لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٢١)
Do they have deities [besides Allah] who have ordained for them a religion not permitted by Allah? Were it not for a [prior] conclusive word, judgement would have been made between them, and indeed a painful punishment awaits the wrongdoers. (21)
تَرَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ وَهُوَ وَاقِعٌۢ بِهِمْ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فِى رَوْضَاتِ ٱلْجَنَّاتِ ۖ لَهُم مَّا يَشَآءُونَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَضْلُ ٱلْكَبِيرُ (٢٢)
When it is about to befall them, You will see the wrongdoers fearful because of what they have earned; but those who have faith and do righteous deeds will be in the gardens of paradise: they will have whatever they wish near their Lord. That is the mighty grace. (22)
ذَٰلِكَ ٱلَّذِى يُبَشِّرُ ٱللَّهُ عِبَادَهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ۗ قُل لَّآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا إِلَّا ٱلْمَوَدَّةَ فِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ ۗ وَمَن يَقْتَرِفْ حَسَنَةً نَّزِدْ لَهُۥ فِيهَا حُسْنًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ (٢٣)
Such is the good news that Allah gives to His servants who have faith and do righteous deeds! Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it except the love of [my] relatives.’ Whoever performs a good deed, We shall enhance its goodness for him. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-appreciative. (23)

Circumstances of Revelation

In the commentary Majma' al-Bayan, a circumstance of revelation for verses 23 to 26 of this Surah is narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, the summary of which is as follows:

"When the Prophet entered Medina and the foundations of Islam became firm, the Ansar said: 'We shall go to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and say: If financial difficulties arise, these properties of ours are at your disposal without any conditions.' When they presented this offer to him, the verse: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى ('Say: I do not ask of you any reward for it except love for my relatives') was revealed. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited it to them and then said: 'Love my relatives after me.' They left his presence with joy, satisfaction, and submission. However, the hypocrites said: 'This is a statement he has fabricated against Allah, and his goal is to humiliate us before his kinsmen after him.' Then the next verse was revealed: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرى عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً ('Or do they say: He has fabricated a lie against Allah?'), answering them. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent for them and recited the verse to them. A group of them repented, wept, and became deeply distressed. Then the third verse was revealed: وَ هُوَ الَّذِي يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبادِهِ ('And He is the One who accepts repentance from His servants').

The Prophet sent for them and gave them the glad tidings that their sincere repentance was accepted in the sight of Allah." [1]

Commentary

Love for the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) is the Reward of the Mission

Since verse 13 of this Surah spoke of the legislation of religion by the Lord through the Arch-Prophets (Ulu al-'Azm), the first verse under discussion continues this by negating the legislation of others, emphasizing that no law is official against Divine law, and that fundamentally the right to legislate belongs exclusively to Allah. It says: "Or do they have partners who have legislated for them a religion which Allah has not authorized?" (أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكاءُ شَرَعُوا لَهُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما لَمْ يَأْذَنْ بِهِ اللَّهُ).

While He alone is the Creator, Owner, and Disposer of the universe, and for this reason, the right to legislate is also exclusive to His Pure Essence; no one can interfere in the realm of His legislation without His permission. Therefore, whatever stands against His legislation is void.

Following this, in a threatening tone, He warns the fabricators of false legislation, saying: "Had it not been for a decisive word from Allah to grant respite to such people, judgment would have been passed between them" (وَ لَوْ لا كَلِمَةُ الْفَصْلِ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ). The command for their punishment would have been issued, leaving them no opportunity.

At the same time, they must not forget this reality: "And indeed, for the wrongdoers is a painful punishment" (وَ إِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

The "decisive word" (kalimat al-fasl) refers to the appointed respite Allah has granted such individuals so they may have freedom of action and so that the proof may be completed against them.

The term "wrongdoers" (zalimin) is used for the polytheists who believed in legislations contrary to Divine laws because of the breadth of the concept of "wrong" (zulm), which applies to any act placed where it does not belong. It appears that "painful punishment" refers to the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, as this expression in the Holy Quran is usually used in this sense. The subsequent verse also testifies to this reality, and the view of some commentators (like al-Qurtubi) who took it to encompass both worldly and otherworldly punishment seems unlikely.

Then, He provides a brief explanation regarding the "punishment of the wrongdoers" and a more detailed explanation regarding the "reward of the believers" in contrast to them, saying: "On that day, you will see the wrongdoers fearful of what they have earned, but [to no avail, for] its punishment will surely befall them" (تَرَى الظَّالِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا وَ هُوَ واقِعٌ بِهِمْ). "But those who believe and do righteous deeds will be in the flowery meadows of the Gardens" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فِي رَوْضاتِ الْجَنَّاتِ).

Rawdat is the plural of rawdah, which refers to a place with abundant water and trees; hence, lush and green gardens are called rawdah. From this expression, it is clearly understood that the gardens of Paradise vary, and the believers who perform righteous deeds will reside in the best of them. The implication is that sinful believers, when included in Allah's forgiveness, will enter Paradise, even if their place is not in the rawdat.

However, Divine grace toward the righteous believers does not end there. They are so encompassed by His favors that "they will have whatever they wish for with their Lord" (لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ).

Thus, there is no proportion between their "deeds" and their "reward"; rather, their reward is unlimited in every respect, as the phrase "they will have whatever they wish" expresses this reality.

Even more striking is the expression "with their Lord" ('inda rabbihim), which signifies Allah's boundless grace toward them. What bounty could be greater than attaining the station of proximity to Allah? Just as He says regarding the martyrs: "Nay, they are alive with their Lord, receiving provision," He says regarding the righteous believers: "They will have whatever they wish with their Lord."

It is not without reason that at the end of the verse He says: "That is the great favor" (ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَضْلُ الْكَبِيرُ).

We have said many times that the description of Paradisiacal blessings cannot be contained in words. For us, prisoners of the material world, it is inconceivable to know what concepts are embedded in the phrase "they will have whatever they wish with their Lord." What do the believers desire? And what favors are found in the proximity of Allah?

Fundamentally, when the Great Allah describes something as a "great favor," it is evident that it possesses such grandeur that whatever we imagine, it is even higher.

In other words, these sincere servants reach a point where whatever they will is provided; meaning, a reflection of the infinite power of the Lord—إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ (Ya-Sin: 82)—manifests in their being. What virtue or bounty could be greater than this?

In the next verse, to express the magnitude of this great reward, He adds: "That is the [good news] of which Allah gives glad tidings to His servants who believe and do righteous deeds" (ذلِكَ الَّذِي يُبَشِّرُ اللَّهُ عِبادَهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ). He gives glad tidings so that the hardships of obedience, servitude, struggling against whims, and Jihad against enemies do not seem difficult for them, and so that through those immense rewards, they find greater strength on the ups and downs of life's paths to reach the pleasure of the Lord.

Since the delivery of this message by the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله might sometimes create the illusion of what wage or reward he seeks from the people for his mission, He commands the Prophet following this: "Say: I do not ask of you any reward for this except love for my relatives" (قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى).

Love for the relatives (dhawi al-qurba), as will be explained in detail, refers back to the issue of Wilayah (guardianship) and the acceptance of the leadership of the Infallible Imams عليه السلام from the progeny of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, which is in truth the continuation of the Prophet's line of leadership and the continuation of Divine Wilayah. It is perfectly clear that accepting this Wilayah and leadership, like the Prophethood of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, is the cause of happiness for the people themselves, and its result returns to them.

To explain:

Commentators have many discussions and various interpretations regarding this phrase. If we look at them with a mind free from prejudice, we see that due to various motives, they have strayed from the original meaning of the verse and chosen possibilities that are consistent neither with the content of the verse nor with the circumstances of revelation and other historical and traditional evidence.

Altogether, there are four famous interpretations for the verse:

  1. The one mentioned above: that "relatives" (dhawi al-qurba) refers to the kinsmen of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and love for them is a means for accepting the Imamate and leadership of the Infallible Imams عليه السلام from his progeny, and a support for the fulfillment of the mission. A group of early commentators and all Shia commentators have chosen this meaning, and numerous narrations from both Shia and Sunni sources have been cited in this regard, which we will mention later.

  2. The meaning is that the reward for the mission is to love the matters that invite you to "proximity to Allah" (qurb al-Ilahi). This interpretation, chosen by a group of Sunni commentators, is in no way consistent with the literal meaning of the verse, because in this case, the meaning would be: "I ask you to love Divine obedience and keep its affection in your hearts," whereas it should have said: "I ask of you Divine obedience" (not the love of Divine obedience). Furthermore, among the addressees of the verse, there was no one who did not wish to draw near to Allah; even the polytheists desired to draw near to Allah and fundamentally considered the worship of idols a means for this.

  3. The meaning is that you should love your own relatives as a reward for the mission and maintain ties of kinship. With this interpretation, there is no proportion between the mission and its reward, for how can loving one's own relatives be a service to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله? And how could it be set as the reward for the mission?!

  4. The meaning is: "My reward is that you preserve my kinship ties to you, and do not harm me because I have kinship ties with most of your tribes" (since the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was related to the tribes of Quraysh through lineage, to many other tribes through marriage, to a group of people in Medina from the Banu al-Najjar tribe through his mother, and to the Banu Sa'd tribe through his foster mother). This interpretation is the worst meaning given for the verse, because the request for the reward of the mission is from those who have accepted his mission. Whenever people accept the mission, there is no longer a need for such discussions; they respect the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as a Divine messenger and do not need to respect him for his kinship, as the respect arising from accepting the mission is above all this. In fact, this interpretation should be counted among the great errors that have affected some commentators and completely distorted the meaning of the verse.

To become better acquainted with the truth of the verse's content, the best way is to seek help from other verses of the Quran:

In many verses of the Holy Quran, we read that the Prophets would say: "I do not ask of you any reward for the call of the mission; my reward is only upon the Lord of the worlds" (ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلى رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ) [1].

Regarding the person of the Prophet of Islam himself, various expressions are seen: In one place it says: قُلْ ما سَأَلْتُكُمْ مِنْ أَجْرٍ فَهُوَ لَكُمْ إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلَى اللَّهِ ("Say: Whatever reward I have asked of you, it is for yourselves; my reward is only upon Allah") (Saba: 47).

In another place we read: قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلًا ("Say: I do not ask of you any reward for the delivery of the mission, except for those who wish to take a path to their Lord").

And finally, in another instance it says: قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ وَ ما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ ("I do not ask of you any reward, and I am not of those who impose") (Sad: 86).

If we put these three verses alongside the verse under discussion, the conclusion is easy to draw: In one place, it completely negates any reward or wage. In another, it says: "I only want a reward from one who seeks a path to Allah." In the third instance, it says: "The reward I have asked of you is for yourselves."

And finally, in the verse under discussion, it adds: "Love for the relatives is the reward of my mission," meaning: I have asked of you a reward that has these characteristics: it is absolutely not something whose benefit returns to me; it is one hundred percent for your own benefit; and it is something that paves your way toward Allah.

In this way, can it be anything other than the issue of continuing the line of the Prophet's school through Divine leaders and his Infallible successors, all of whom were from his household? However, since the issue of love (mawaddah) was the foundation of this connection, it is stated explicitly in this verse.

Interestingly, besides the verse under discussion, the word al-qurba is used in fifteen other places in the Holy Quran, in all of which it means "kinsmen" and "relatives." Despite this, it is unclear why some insist that qurba here exclusively means "proximity to Allah" and set aside its clear and obvious meaning used everywhere else in the Quran.

This point is also noteworthy: at the end of the verse under discussion, it adds: "And whoever earns a good deed, We shall increase for him good therein; indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Appreciative, and gives appropriate reward for the deeds of His servants" (وَ مَنْ يَقْتَرِفْ حَسَنَةً نَزِدْ لَهُ فِيها حُسْناً إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ).

What "good deed" (hasanah) is greater than a person always placing himself under the banner of Divine leaders, holding their love in his heart, following their path, seeking explanation from them where matters of Divine speech become ambiguous, making their actions his criterion, and taking them as his role models and examples?

Narrations Regarding the Interpretation of This Verse

Another eloquent witness for the above interpretation is the numerous narrations in both Sunni and Shia sources from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself, which show that "relatives" (qurba) refers to the Ahl al-Bayt, the kinsmen, and the close ones of the Prophet. For example:

  1. Ahmad in Fada'il al-Sahabah, with his chain from Sa'id ibn Jubayr from 'Amir, narrates: "When the verse قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى was revealed, the companions asked: 'O Messenger of Allah! Who are your relatives whose love has been made obligatory upon us?' He said: 'Ali, Fatimah, and their two sons (عليهما السلام),' and he said this three times!" [1]

  2. In Mustadrak al-Sahihayn, it is narrated from Imam 'Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام that when the Commander of the Faithful 'Ali عليه السلام was martyred, Hasan ibn 'Ali عليه السلام delivered a sermon to the people, part of which was: "I am from the Household whose love Allah has made obligatory upon every Muslim, for the Blessed and Exalted said to His Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: 'Say: I do not ask of you any reward except love for my relatives, and whoever earns a good deed, We shall increase for him good therein.' Thus, the earning of the good deed is love for us, the Ahl al-Bayt." [2]

  3. Al-Suyuti in al-Durr al-Manthur, under the verse in question, narrates from Mujahid from Ibn 'Abbas that in interpreting the verse قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى, he said: "It means that you should preserve my right regarding my Ahl al-Bayt and love them for my sake." [3] From this, it becomes clear that what was narrated from Ibn 'Abbas through another route—that it meant not harming the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله due to his kinship with various Arab tribes—is not certain, as we have seen its opposite also narrated from Ibn 'Abbas.

  4. Ibn Jarir al-Tabari in his commentary, with his chain from Sa'id ibn Jubayr and another chain from 'Amr ibn Shu'ayb, narrates that the meaning of this verse is "the relatives of the Messenger of Allah." [1]

  5. The late Tabarsi, the famous commentator, narrates from Shawahid al-Tanzil by Hakim al-Hasakani—one of the famous Sunni commentators and traditionists—from Abi Umamah al-Bahili: The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Indeed, Allah created the Prophets from various trees, but I and 'Ali (عليه السلام) are from a single tree; I am its root, 'Ali (عليه السلام) is its branch, Fatimah (عليها السلام) is its pollination, and al-Hasan and al-Husayn are its fruits, and our followers are its leaves..." until he said: "If a servant were to worship Allah between al-Safa and al-Marwah for a thousand years, then a thousand years, then a thousand years, until he becomes like a worn-out waterskin, but he does not possess our love, Allah will cast him on his face into the Fire." Then he recited: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً...

It is interesting that this hadith became so famous that the renowned poet Kumayt referred to it in his poems, saying: "We found for you in the Al-Ha-Mim Surahs a verse, which the cautious among us interpreted and the outspoken declared openly." [1]

  1. Also, al-Suyuti in al-Durr al-Manthur narrates from Ibn Jarir from Abi al-Daylam: "When 'Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام was brought as a captive and held at the gate of Damascus, a man from the people of Sham said: 'Praise be to Allah who killed you and uprooted you!' 'Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام said: 'Have you read the Quran?' He said: 'Yes.' He asked: 'Have you read the Ha-Mim Surahs?' He replied: 'No.' He said: 'Have you not read this verse: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى?' He asked: 'Are you the ones referred to in this verse?' He replied: 'Yes.'" [2]

  2. Al-Zamakhshari in al-Kashshaf narrated a hadith which Fakhr al-Razi and al-Qurtubi also adopted in their commentaries. The said hadith clearly states the status of the Progeny of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله and the importance of loving them. He says: The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"Whoever dies upon the love of the Progeny of Muhammad dies a martyr. Behold, whoever dies upon the love of the Progeny of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) dies forgiven. Behold, whoever dies upon the love of the Progeny of Muhammad dies repentant. Behold, whoever dies upon the love of the Progeny of Muhammad dies a believer of complete faith. Behold, whoever dies upon the love of the Progeny of Muhammad, the Angel of Death gives him glad tidings of Paradise, then Munkar and Nakir. Behold, whoever dies upon the love of the Progeny of Muhammad is escorted to Paradise as a bride is escorted to the house of her husband. Behold, whoever dies upon the love of the Progeny of Muhammad, two doors to Paradise are opened in his grave. Behold, whoever dies upon the love of the Progeny of Muhammad, Allah makes his grave a place of visitation for the angels of mercy. Behold, whoever dies upon the love of the Progeny of Muhammad dies upon the Sunnah and the Community. Behold, whoever dies upon the hatred of the Progeny of Muhammad will come on the Day of Resurrection with 'Despairing of Allah's mercy' written between his eyes. Behold, whoever dies upon the hatred of the Progeny of Muhammad dies a disbeliever. Behold, whoever dies upon the hatred of the Progeny of Muhammad will not smell the fragrance of Paradise." [1]

Interestingly, Fakhr al-Razi, after mentioning this noble hadith—which the author of al-Kashshaf cited as an established report—adds:

"The Progeny of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله are those whose affairs return to him; those whose connection is firmer and more complete are considered 'Progeny' (Al). There is no doubt that Fatimah, 'Ali, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn had the firmest bond with the Messenger of Allah, and this is among the established facts and derived from mutawatir (frequently narrated) hadiths. Therefore, it is necessary that we consider them the 'Progeny of the Prophet' صلى الله عليه وآله."

He then adds: "A group has differed regarding the concept of 'Progeny'; some consider them the close relatives of the Prophet, and some have said they are the Ummah (community) of the Prophet. If we take the word in the first sense, the Progeny of the Prophet are only those [four]. If we take it to mean the Ummah who accepted his call, still the close relatives of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله are considered his Progeny. Therefore, in any case, they are the Progeny. As for others, whether they are included in the word 'Progeny' or not is a matter of disagreement."

Then Fakhr al-Razi narrates from the author of al-Kashshaf: "When this verse was revealed, they asked: 'O Messenger of Allah! Who are your relatives whose love is obligatory upon us?' He said: 'Ali, Fatimah, and their two sons.' Thus, it is proven that these four are the relatives of the Prophet, and when this meaning is proven, it is obligatory that they enjoy extraordinary respect."

Fakhr al-Razi adds: "Various proofs indicate this matter:

  1. The phrase إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى, the method of arguing from which has been explained.

  2. There is no doubt that the Prophet loved Fatimah and said regarding her: 'Fatimah is a part of me; whatever harms her harms me.' It is proven through mutawatir hadiths from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله that he loved 'Ali, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn. When this meaning is proven, loving them is obligatory upon the entire Ummah because Allah has said: وَ اتَّبِعُوهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ ('And follow him so that you may be guided') [1], and He said: فَلْيَحْذَرِ الَّذِينَ يُخالِفُونَ عَنْ أَمْرِهِ ('Let those who oppose his command beware [of Divine punishment]') [2], and He said: قُلْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللَّهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللَّهُ ('Say: If you love Allah, then follow me, so that Allah may love you') [3], and He said: لَقَدْ كانَ لَكُمْ فِي رَسُولِ اللَّهِ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ ('Indeed, there was for you in the Messenger of Allah an excellent example') [4].

  3. Praying for the 'Progeny' is a great honor, and therefore this prayer was placed at the end of the Tashahhud in prayer: 'O Allah, bless Muhammad and the Progeny of Muhammad, and have mercy on Muhammad and the Progeny of Muhammad.' Such glorification and respect have not been seen for anyone other than the Progeny. Therefore, all these proofs show that loving the Progeny of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله is obligatory."

Finally, Fakhr al-Razi concludes his words on this matter with the famous poems of al-Shafi'i:

"O rider, stop at the pebbled ground of Mina, And call out to those dwelling in its Khif and those departing. At dawn, when the pilgrims pour into Mina, A flood like the surging Euphrates. If love for the Progeny of Muhammad is Rafd (heresy), Then let the two heavy weights (Jinn and Men) bear witness that I am a Rafidi!" [1]

Yes, this is the status of the Progeny of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, whose mantle we have grasped, whom we have accepted as our leaders and guides for religion and the world, whom we consider our role models and examples, and through whose Imamate we see the continuation of the line of Prophethood.

Of course, besides the above hadiths, numerous other narrations are cited in Islamic sources. For the sake of brevity and focusing on the exegetical aspects, we have limited ourselves to the seven narrations above. However, we find it appropriate to mention that in some theological sources like Ihqaq al-Haqq and its detailed commentary, the famous hadith regarding the interpretation of the verse قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى has been narrated from about fifty books of the Sunnis, which shows how widespread and famous the narration of this hadith has been, regardless of the numerous sources that narrate this hadith through the channels of the Ahl al-Bayt.

Key Points

1. A Word with the Famous Commentator "al-Alusi"

Here, a question is raised by some, which al-Alusi, the famous commentator, presented in Ruh al-Ma'ani as an objection against the Shia. we present it here as a question and examine it. The summary of his words is as follows:

"Some of the Shia have cited the verse in the context of arguing for the Imamate of 'Ali عليه السلام, saying that 'Ali عليه السلام is 'obligatory to love' (wajib al-mahabbah), and everyone who is obligatory to love is 'obligatory to obey' (wajib al-ta'ah), and everyone who is obligatory to obey possesses the station of Imamate. From this, they concluded that 'Ali عليه السلام possesses the station of Imamate and considered the verse a proof for this subject.

However, their argument is subject to objection from several aspects:

First: Arguing from the verse for the obligation of love is contingent upon interpreting the verse as the love of the Prophet's relatives, while a large group of commentators have not accepted this interpretation. They said this is not appropriate for the station of Prophethood because it makes the Prophet subject to accusation, as it resembles the work of world-seekers who start activities and then demand benefits for their children and relatives in return. Furthermore, it contradicts the verse: وَ ما تَسْئَلُهُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ('You do not ask them for any reward') (Yusuf: 104).

Second: We do not accept that the obligation of love is proof of the obligation of obedience. Thus, Ibn Babawayh says in the book al-I'tiqadat: 'The Imamiyyah agree that love for the Alawids is necessary, while they do not consider all of them obligatory to obey!'

Third: We do not accept that every person who is obligatory to obey possesses the station of Imamate, meaning the supreme leadership (al-za'amah al-kubra); otherwise, every Prophet in his time would have possessed such a station, while in the story of Talut, we read that he became the leader of the people while a Prophet also existed at that time.

Fourth: The verse would require all the Ahl al-Bayt to be obligatory to obey, and for the same reason, all should be Imams, while the Imamiyyah do not hold such a belief." [1]

Analysis and Review

With the understanding we have of the verse under discussion, based on the numerous and firm evidences present in the verse and other verses of the Quran, the answer to many of these objections becomes clear:

For we said that this love is not a simple matter; it is the reward of Prophethood and the wage of the mission. Naturally, it must be something of equal weight and status to be able to serve as its reward.

On the other hand, the verses of the Quran testify that the benefit of this love is not something that returns to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; rather, its result returns one hundred percent to the believers themselves, or in other words, it is a spiritual matter effective in the perfection of the Muslims' guidance.

In this way, although nothing but the issue of the obligation of love is derived from the verse, with the evidences mentioned, this obligation of love leads to the issue of Imamate, which is the support for the station of Prophethood and the mission.

With this brief explanation, we examine the above objections:

First: As for the fact that some commentators have not interpreted the verse as love for the Ahl al-Bayt, it must be admitted that prejudices and mental residues prevented this. For example, a group of them interpreted qurba as "proximity to the Lord"! While in all verses of the Quran, wherever this word is used, it means "relatives."

Or that a group interpreted it as the Prophet's kinship with Arab tribes, while this interpretation completely disrupts the system of the verse. The reward of the mission is requested from one who has accepted the mission; and for one who accepts the Prophet's mission, what need is there to consider his kinship with him and refrain from harming him?

Furthermore, why should we set aside the many narrations that interpret the verse as the Wilayah of the Prophet's Ahl al-Bayt?! Therefore, it must be accepted that this group of commentators never approached the interpretation of the verse with an empty mind; otherwise, there is nothing complex in it.

From this, it becomes clear that requesting such a reward neither contradicts the station of Prophethood nor is it like the way of world-seekers. It is also perfectly consistent with verse 104 of Surah Yusuf, which negates any kind of reward, because the reward of love for the Ahl al-Bayt is in truth not a reward from which the Prophet benefits, but rather the Muslims themselves benefit from it.

Second: It is true that simple obligatory love is never proof of obligatory obedience. However, when we consider that this is a love placed as the proportionate equivalent of the mission, we become certain that the obligation of obedience is also embedded within it. From this, it becomes clear that the statement of Ibn Babawayh (al-Saduq) also does not contradict what we have said.

Third: It is true that not every obligation of obedience is proof of the station of Imamate and supreme leadership, but it must be noted that an obligation of obedience which is the reward of the mission and proportionate to it can be nothing other than Imamate.

Fourth: The Imam—meaning the leader—can be no more than one person in any era, and therefore the Imamate of all the Ahl al-Bayt [simultaneously] would not make sense. Furthermore, one should not overlook the role of narrations in this field, i.e., in understanding the meaning of the verse.

It is noteworthy that al-Alusi himself personally attributed great importance to the love of the Ahl al-Bayt. A few lines before the above discussion, he says: "The truth is that love for the relatives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is obligatory because of their kinship with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the stronger the kinship, the more obligatory the love." Finally, he says: "The effects of this love are glorification, respect, and fulfilling the rights of the Prophet's relatives, while many people have been lax in this matter to the point that they considered love for the Prophet's relatives a type of Rafidism! But I do not say so; rather, I say what al-Shafi'i said in his eloquent and expressive words."

He then mentions the poems we quoted above from al-Shafi'i and adds: "Nevertheless, I do not believe in departing from the beliefs of the great Sunni scholars regarding the companions, and I also consider loving them among the obligations." [1]

2. The Ark of Salvation!

Fakhr al-Razi, under this discussion, narrated a point and favored it, and al-Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani also cited it as a "subtle point" narrated from Fakhr al-Razi—a point through which they think some contradictions are resolved. It is that the Noble Prophet of Islam said on one hand:

"The likeness of my Ahl al-Bayt is like the Ark of Noah; whoever boards it is saved."

And on the other hand, he said:

"My companions are like stars; whichever of them you follow, you will be guided."

And we are now caught in the ocean of obligation, and the waves of doubts and desires strike us from every side. He who wishes to cross the sea needs two things: one is a ship free from any defect, and the other is bright, shining stars to show him the path. When a person boards the ship and fixes his eyes on the shining stars, there is hope for salvation. Likewise, whoever among the Sunnis boards the ship of love for the Progeny of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله and fixes his eyes on the stars of the companions, it is hoped that Allah will bring him to safety and happiness in this world and the hereafter. [1]

But we say: this poetic comparison, though beautiful, is not precise. Because, firstly, Noah's Ark was the vehicle of salvation, and on that day when surging waves of water covered the entire world, it was constantly in motion without having a destination like ordinary ships that move toward a destination with the help of stars. The destination was the ship itself, and salvation from the abyss, until the water subsided and the ship rested on Mount Judi.

Secondly, in some narrations cited in the books of our Sunni brothers, it is reported from the Noble Prophet of Islam:

"The stars are a security for the people of the earth from drowning, and my Ahl al-Bayt are a security for my Ummah from disagreement in religion." [2]

3. Interpretation of "وَ مَنْ يَقْتَرِفْ حَسَنَةً..."

Iqtiraf in the phrase "And whoever earns a good deed, We shall increase for him good therein" is originally from qarf, meaning to peel off extra bark from a tree or extra skin from a wound, which sometimes leads to refinement and healing. This word was later used for "earning" (iktisab), whether it be earning good or evil.

However, according to al-Raghib, this term is used for evils more than for goods (though in the verse under discussion, it is used for goods). Therefore, a famous proverb among the Arabs says: "Confession (al-i'tiraf) removes the earning of sin (al-iqtiraf)."

Interestingly, in some commentaries, it is narrated from Ibn 'Abbas and another early commentator named al-Suddi that the meaning of "earning a good deed" in the noble verse is love for the Progeny of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله. [1] In the hadith we previously narrated from Imam Hasan ibn 'Ali عليه السلام, it also states: "The earning of the good deed is love for us, the Ahl al-Bayt."

It is clear that the purpose of such interpretations is not to limit the meaning of earning a good deed to the love of the Ahl al-Bayt; rather, it has a broad and expansive meaning. However, since this phrase follows the issue of love for the relatives, the clearest instance of earning a good deed is this very love.

4. These Few Verses are Medinan

This Surah (Surah al-Shura), as we said at the beginning, is among the Meccan Surahs. However, a group of commentators believe that these four verses (verses 23 to 26) were revealed in Medina. The circumstance of revelation we narrated at the beginning of the commentary on these verses testifies to this meaning. The narrations that interpret the Ahl al-Bayt as 'Ali عليه السلام, Fatimah, and their two sons Imam Hasan and Imam Husayn are also appropriate for this meaning, as we know that the marriage of 'Ali عليه السلام and the Lady of Islam took place in Medina, and the birth of al-Hasan and al-Husayn عليه السلام was in the third and fourth years of the Hijrah according to the famous reports.

Ayahs

أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا ۖ فَإِن يَشَإِ ٱللَّهُ يَخْتِمْ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ ۗ وَيَمْحُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ وَيُحِقُّ ٱلْحَقَّ بِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٢٤)
Do they say, ‘He has fabricated a lie against Allah’? If so, should Allah wish He would set a seal on your heart, and Allah will efface the falsehood and confirm the truth with His words. Indeed He knows well what is in the breasts. (24)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يَقْبَلُ ٱلتَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَنِ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَفْعَلُونَ (٢٥)
It is He who accepts the repentance of His servants, and excuses their misdeeds and knows what you do. (25)
وَيَسْتَجِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَيَزِيدُهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ ۚ وَٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ (٢٦)
He answers [the supplications of] those who have faith and do righteous deeds and enhances them out of His grace. But as for the faithless, there is a severe punishment for them. (26)

Commentary

He Accepts the Repentance of His Servants

These verses are a continuation of the previous verses regarding the messengership, the reward of the messengership, and the love for the near of kin (Mawaddat Dhi al-Qurba) and the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them).

In the first verse, it says: They do not accept this divine revelation, "Or do they say, 'He has invented a lie about Allah'? But these are the products of his own mind which he attributes to Allah" (أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً).

"While if Allah willed, He could seal your heart and take away from you the power to manifest these verses" (فَإِنْ يَشَإِ اللَّهُ يَخْتِمْ عَلى‌ قَلْبِكَ‌).

This is, in fact, an allusion to a well-known logical argument: if someone claims prophethood and miracles and clear signs appear through his hand and tongue, and he receives divine support and victory, yet he attributes lies to Allah, the wisdom of Allah necessitates that He withdraw those miracles and His support from him and disgrace him. This is as stated in verses 44 to 46 of Surah Al-Haqqah: وَ لَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنا بَعْضَ الْأَقاوِيلِ لَأَخَذْنا مِنْهُ بِالْيَمِينِ ثُمَّ لَقَطَعْنا مِنْهُ الْوَتِينَ‌: "And if he had made up about Us some [false] sayings, We would have seized him by the right hand; then We would have cut from him the aorta."

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 20, p. 426

Commentators have suggested other possibilities for the interpretation of this sentence, but what was mentioned above seems the clearest of all.

It is also noteworthy that one of the false accusations the polytheists and disbelievers leveled against the Noble Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) was that he considered the reward for his messengership to be the love for his kinsfolk, and that he had forged this lie against Allah (in connection with the discussion in the preceding verses); the above verse also refutes this accusation.

However, the meaning of the verse is not limited to this sense, because according to other verses of the Quran, the enemies of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) leveled this accusation against the entirety of the Quran and revelation. In verse 38 of Surah Yunus, we read: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ‌: "Or do they say, 'He invented it?' Say, 'Then bring forth a surah like it...'"

A similar meaning, with slight variations, appears in verse 13 of Surah Hud and verse 35 of Surah Hud, as well as some other verses of the Quran. These verses serve as evidence for the interpretation we chose for the verse.

Then, to emphasize this point, it adds: "Allah eliminates falsehood and establishes the truth by His words" (وَ يَمْحُ اللَّهُ الْباطِلَ وَ يُحِقُّ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ‌) [1]. It is Allah's duty, based on His wisdom, to make the truth manifest and to disgrace falsehood. Given this, how could He allow someone to forge lies against Him while simultaneously helping him and manifesting miracles through his hands?

And if it is imagined that the Prophet might do such a thing hidden from Allah's knowledge, it is a great mistake, "for He is Knowing of that within the breasts" (إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ).

As we mentioned in the commentary of verse 38 of Surah Fatir, the word "Dhat" in the Arabic language does not originally mean the essence or reality of things; rather, this is a term used by philosophers [2]. Instead, Dhat means "possessor" or "owner." Thus, the phrase "إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ" means that Allah is aware of the thoughts and beliefs that govern the hearts and have, as it were, become their owner and master. This is a subtle allusion to the dominance and establishment of thoughts within the hearts and souls of humans (reflect on this).

[1]: Note that "يمح" (yamhu) was originally "يمحو" (yamhuw), but the waw is usually dropped in many scripts of the Quran, similar to وَ يَدْعُ الْإِنْسانُ بِالشَّرِّ (Al-Isra: 11) and سَنَدْعُ الزَّبانِيَةَ (Al-Alaq: 18). In all these cases, the waw is mentioned in modern orthography but is often omitted in Quranic manuscripts. [2]: Refer to Mufradat by Raghib.

Since Allah always keeps the path of return open to His servants, He repeatedly mentions the openness of the doors of repentance in the verses of the Quran after censuring the evil deeds of the polytheists and sinners. In the verses under discussion, following the previous discourse, He adds: "And He is the one who accepts repentance from His servants and pardons misdeeds" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبادِهِ وَ يَعْفُوا عَنِ السَّيِّئاتِ‌).

However, if you pretend to repent but do something else in secret, do not imagine that it will remain hidden from the sharp eye of the Lord's knowledge; no, "He knows what you do" (وَ يَعْلَمُ ما تَفْعَلُونَ‌).

In the occasion of revelation mentioned at the beginning of the previous verses, it was said that after the revelation of the verse of "Mawaddat" (Love), a group of hypocrites or those weak in faith said: "This is a word that Muhammad has forged against Allah; he wants to humble us before his relatives after he is gone." Then the verse "أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً" was revealed and answered them. When they learned of the revelation of the verse, a group became regretful, wept, and were distressed. Then the verse "وَ هُوَ الَّذِي يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ ..." was revealed and gave them the glad tidings that if they repent sincerely, Allah will forgive their lapse.

In the final verse under discussion, He describes the great reward of the believers and the painful punishment of the disbelievers in short sentences, saying: "And He answers [the prayer of] those who have believed and done righteous deeds" (وَ يَسْتَجِيبُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ‌). "And He increases [reward] for them from His bounty," and He even grants them things they did not ask for (وَ يَزِيدُهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ‌). "But for the disbelievers is a severe punishment" (وَ الْكافِرُونَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ).

Regarding what request of the believers He answers, various interpretations have been given. A group of commentators have limited it to specific requests, including:

Some have said: He answers the prayers of believers for one another. Others have said: He accepts their acts of worship and obedience. And some relate it to their intercession for their brothers.

However, there is no evidence for these limitations; rather, Allah accepts any request that the righteous believers may have, and beyond that, He grants them from His bounty matters that perhaps did not even occur to them to ask of Allah. This is the ultimate grace and mercy of Allah toward the believers.

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) from the Noble Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) regarding the interpretation of "وَ يَزِيدُهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ‌", we read: "That which Allah increases for them from His bounty is the intercession for those who did good to them in the world, but (due to their evil deeds) have become deserving of the fire of Hell." [1] The meaning of this profound hadith is not that Allah's additional bounty is limited to this, but rather it describes one of its clear examples.

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed.

Ayahs

۞ وَلَوْ بَسَطَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِعِبَادِهِۦ لَبَغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَـٰكِن يُنَزِّلُ بِقَدَرٍ مَّا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِعِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرٌۢ بَصِيرٌ (٢٧)
Were Allah to expand the provision for [all] His servants, they would surely create havoc on the earth. But He sends down in a [precise] measure whatever He wishes. Indeed, He is all-aware, all-seeing about His servants. (27)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُنَزِّلُ ٱلْغَيْثَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا قَنَطُوا۟ وَيَنشُرُ رَحْمَتَهُۥ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْوَلِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (٢٨)
It is He who sends down the rain after they have been despondent, and unfolds His mercy, and He is the Guardian, the All-laudable. (28)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ خَلْقُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَثَّ فِيهِمَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ ۚ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ جَمْعِهِمْ إِذَا يَشَآءُ قَدِيرٌ (٢٩)
Among His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth and whatever creatures He has scattered in them, and He is able to gather them whenever He wishes. (29)
وَمَآ أَصَـٰبَكُم مِّن مُّصِيبَةٍ فَبِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَن كَثِيرٍ (٣٠)
Whatever affliction that may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense of yours]. (30)
وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (٣١)
You cannot frustrate [Allah] on the earth, and you do not have besides Allah any guardian or helper. (31)

Occasion of Revelation

It has been narrated from the famous companion, Khabbab ibn al-Aratt, that the first verse (وَ لَوْ بَسَطَ اللَّهُ‌ ...) was revealed concerning us. This was because we looked upon the abundant wealth of the Jewish tribes of Banu Qurayza, Banu Nadir, and Banu Qaynuqa, and we wished that we too possessed such wealth. The verse was revealed and warned us that if Allah were to enlarge the provision for His servants, they would surely rebel [1]. In the commentary of al-Durr al-Manthur, another hadith is narrated stating that this verse was revealed regarding the Ashab al-Suffah (People of the Bench), as they wished for their worldly affairs to be prosperous [2]. (Regarding the Ashab al-Suffah and who they were, we will provide an explanation at the end of these verses, God willing).

Commentary

The Rebellious Affluent!

The connection between these verses and the preceding ones may be from the perspective that the last of the previous verses mentioned that Allah answers the prayers of the believers. Following that, the question arises: why then are a group among them poor, and no matter how much they supplicate, they do not reach a state of wealth?

He says: "And if Allah had extended [excessively] provision for His servants, they would have committed tyranny throughout the earth" (وَ لَوْ بَسَطَ اللَّهُ الرِّزْقَ لِعِبادِهِ لَبَغَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

"But He sends it down in a measure as He wills [and deems appropriate]" (وَ لكِنْ يُنَزِّلُ بِقَدَرٍ ما يَشاءُ).

In this way, the issue of the distribution of provision is based on a precise calculation that the Lord has regarding His servants, "for He is, of His servants, All-Aware and All-Seeing" (إِنَّهُ بِعِبادِهِ خَبِيرٌ بَصِيرٌ).

He knows the measure and the existential capacity of every individual and grants them provision according to their best interest; He does not give so much that they rebel, nor so little that their cries rise from poverty.

A similar meaning appears in verses 6 and 7 of Surah al-Alaq: "Indeed, man transgresses because he sees himself self-sufficient" (إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَيَطْغى‌ أَنْ رَآهُ اسْتَغْنى‌).

And truly it is so; studying the condition of humans is a truthful witness to this reality—that when the world turns toward them, they possess a comfortable life, and the course of events aligns with their desires, they no longer serve Allah. They quickly distance themselves from Him, drown in the sea of lusts, do what is unmentionable, and spread all kinds of oppression and corruption on earth.

In another interpretation from Ibn Abbas regarding this verse, we read that the meaning of "baghy" here is not oppression and rebellion, but rather baghy in the sense of "seeking"—meaning if Allah were to expand provision for His servants, they would still seek more and would never be satisfied! However, the first interpretation, chosen by many commentators, seems more correct, as the expression "يَبْغُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌" (they rebel/act wickedly on earth) has repeatedly appeared in Quranic verses to mean corruption and oppression on earth, such as: "But when He has delivered them, at once they insolently transgress upon the earth without right" (Yunus: 23) and "The way is only against those who wrong the people and tyrannize upon the earth without right" (Verse 42 of this same Surah).

It is true that "baghy" also comes with the meaning of "seeking," but when it is accompanied by "fi al-ard" (in the earth), it means corruption and oppression on earth.

Here, two questions arise

First, if the program for distributing provision is such, then why do we see a group who have abundant provision and have rebelled, corrupted, and brought the world to ruin, and Allah has not stopped them—both on the scale of individuals and on the scale of predatory, bullying governments?

In response to this question, one must note that sometimes the expansion of provision is a means for trial and testing, for all humans must be tested in this world; a group is also tested with wealth.

And sometimes it is so that both they and other humans may know that wealth does not create happiness; perhaps they will find the way and return to Allah. Even now, we see societies drowned in all kinds of blessings, wealth, and comfort, yet simultaneously afflicted with all kinds of calamities and miseries; insecurity, killing, extensive moral pollution, anxiety, and various worries have overwhelmed their bodies and souls.

Sometimes, excessive wealth is a Divine punishment that Allah afflicts some with; the vista of their lives is enchanting, but if we look closely, we see that none are more miserable than themselves! In this regard, there are many stories of the world's "sultans of wealth" that many have heard, and recounting them would lead to prolixity.

The second question: Does this not mean that whenever a person is poor and deprived, they should not strive to expand their provision, on the possibility that Allah has deemed their interest to be in this state?

In response to this question as well, it must be noted that sometimes the lack of provision is due to the person's own lethargy and laziness. These shortages and deprivations are not the absolute Will of Allah, but rather the result of their own actions that have overtaken them. Islam, based on the principle of effort and striving—which is reflected both in the verses of the Quran and in the Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of Guidance علیهم السلام—calls everyone to struggle and endeavor.

However, whenever a person exerts their utmost effort and yet the doors remain closed to them, they should know there was a wisdom in this matter; they should not be restless, should not despair, should not open their tongue to ingratitude, and should continue their effort while also being submitted to the Divine Pleasure.

It is also worth mentioning that the expression "عباده" (His servants) never contradicts their rebellion in the event of expanded provision, for this expression is used for good, bad, and average servants alike, such as: "Say, 'O My servants who have transgressed against themselves [by sinning], do not despair of the mercy of Allah'" (قُلْ يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللَّهِ‌).

It is true that Allah sends down provision with calculation so that the servants do not rebel, but it is not such that He deprives or bars them; therefore, in the next verse He adds: "And He is it who sends down the beneficial rain after they had despaired and spreads His mercy" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي يُنَزِّلُ الْغَيْثَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما قَنَطُوا وَ يَنْشُرُ رَحْمَتَهُ‌).

And it must be so, "for He is the Protector, the Praiseworthy" (وَ هُوَ الْوَلِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ). This verse, while being an expression of the blessing and grace of the Lord, also speaks of the signs and indicators of Monotheism (Tawhid), for the descent of rain has a very precise and calculated system. From the moment the sun shines upon the oceans and separates the delicate particles of water from salts, sending them to the sky in the form of cloud masses, and when the cold upper layer of air condenses them, and then the winds carry them on their shoulders and take them over thirsty and parched lands, and due to specific coldness and air pressure, they turn into small drops of rain that sit softly on the earth and penetrate it without creating destruction.

Yes, if we examine this system with precision, the signs of Allah's knowledge and power are manifest in it. He is the Praiseworthy Protector who provides for the needs of the servants and encompasses them with His graces.

It is noteworthy that "ghayth"—as many commentators and some linguists have specified—means "beneficial rain," whereas "matar" is said for any kind of rain, whether beneficial or not.

Therefore, it is followed by the phrase "و ينشر رحمته" (and He spreads His mercy).

What a beautiful and comprehensive expression! He spreads His mercy in reviving dead lands, in growing plants, in washing the air, in providing drinking water for humans and other living creatures, and in short, in all fields.

If a person wishes to understand the concept of this Quranic sentence, they should walk into the mountains, plains, and wilderness during a sunny hour after the rain has fallen and observe the delicacy, beauty, and freshness—which is the spread mercy of Allah—everywhere.

This use of the word "ghayth" is perhaps because it shares a common root with the word "ghawth," which means "succor" or "relief." For this reason, some commentators have considered the above expression an allusion to every kind of Divine succor after despair and the spreading of His mercy [1].

And again, on this occasion, in the next verse, He mentions one of the most important signs of the Lord's knowledge and power, saying: "And of His signs is the creation of the heavens and earth and what He has dispersed throughout them of creatures" (وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ خَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَثَّ فِيهِما مِنْ دابَّةٍ).

The heavens with all that grandeur, with those solar systems and galaxies, and millions upon millions of great and brilliant stars; the heavens with that system whose study leaves man in awe; and the earth with its various life resources, with diverse and colorful plants, flowers, and fruits, with various blessings and graces, and its types of beauties—all are His signs and indicators on one hand.

On the other hand, the creatures of the earth and sky—types of birds, hundreds of thousands of types of insects, types of wild and domestic animals, reptiles, and very small, delicate fish as well as giant and massive ones, and types of aquatic life and the wonders and marvels employed in the structure of each of them—and most importantly, the very reality of "life" and the mysterious principles governing it, which even after thousands of years of study by millions of scientists, no one has reached its depth—all, all are the signs of Allah.

Interestingly, "dabbah" (creature/moving thing) includes both microscopic living beings that have delicate and mysterious movement, and giant animals that are tens of meters long and weigh tens of tons; each in its own way glorifies the Truth and sings His praise, and with the language of its state, expresses the grandeur and power of His endless knowledge.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "And He, for gathering them when He wills, is All-Powerful" (وَ هُوَ عَلى‌ جَمْعِهِمْ إِذا يَشاءُ قَدِيرٌ) [1]. Regarding what is meant by "gathering all creatures" in this verse, many commentators have taken it to mean gathering for the reckoning and recompense of deeds on the Day of Resurrection, and one can take the verses that refer to the Resurrection as the "Day of Gathering" (Yawm al-Jam') as evidence for this meaning (such as verse 7 of this same Surah ash-Shura and verse 9 of at-Taghabun).

In this case, the question arises: will all creatures be resurrected on the Day of Judgment, even non-humans? Rather, it is sometimes said that "dabbah" is usually applied to non-humans; in this case, the problem arises as to how non-human creatures have resurrection and reckoning when they lack intellect, consciousness, and religious obligation?

The answer to this question was given under verse 38 of Surah al-An'am: "And there is no creature on [or within] the earth or bird that flies with its wings except [that they are] communities like you. We have not neglected in the Register a thing. Then unto their Lord they will be gathered."

We said that the lives of many animals are mixed with an interesting and wonderful system; what prevents these actions from expressing a type of intellect and consciousness in them? What necessity is there to categorize all of these as instinct? In that case, a type of resurrection and reckoning would be conceivable for them (for more detail on this subject, please study Volume 5, page 224 onwards, under the aforementioned verse).

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse under discussion that "gathering" (jam') is the opposite of "dispersing" (bath), meaning that "bath" refers to the creation and expansion of types of living beings and creatures, then He says whenever Allah wills, He "gathers" them and destroys or makes them extinct.

Just as throughout history until now, many types of creatures on earth have found a strange expansion, and at a time after that, they became extinct and were "gathered." Both their expansion is by the hand of Allah and their gathering; in reality, it is similar to the verses that say: the Giver of life is Allah, and the Giver of death is also He.

In this case, the issue of the reckoning and recompense of animals would not be raised in this verse.

The Inhabited Stars of the Sky

Among the notable points derived from this verse is that it indicates the existence of types of living beings in the heavens. Although scientists do not yet judge decisively in this field, they say cryptically: among the celestial bodies, there are most likely many stars that possess living beings.

However, the Quran, with the phrase "وَ ما بَثَّ فِيهِما مِنْ دابَّةٍ" (and what He has dispersed throughout them [the heavens and the earth] of creatures), explicitly declares this reality—that in the expanse of the sky, there are also many living creatures.

And the fact that some commentators have suggested that "fihima" (in both of them) is restricted to the planet Earth seems very unlikely, because the "dual pronoun" refers to both the heaven and the earth, just as interpreting "dabbah" as the angels of heaven is a very unlikely interpretation, because "dabbah" is usually applied to material moving creatures.

This meaning is also derived from several other verses of the Holy Quran.

In a famous hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Imam Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام, it is narrated that he said: "These stars that are in the sky are cities like the cities on earth; every city is connected to another city (every star to another star) by a pillar of light" [1]. Several other narrations have also been reported in this regard (for more information, one can refer to the book al-Hay'ah wa al-Islam).

Since the previous verses spoke of Allah's mercy, this naturally raises the question: where do these calamities that we grapple with come from? The next verse answers this question: "And whatever strikes you of disaster—it is for what your hands have earned" (وَ ما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ‌).

And even then, this is not the full punishment for your improper deeds, for "He pardons much" (وَ يَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ).

Calamities That Overtake Us

In this verse, there are points that must be noted:

  1. This verse clearly shows that the calamities that overtake man are a type of Divine punishment and warning (though there are exceptions that will be mentioned later), and in this way, one of the philosophies of painful events and life's problems becomes clear. Interestingly, in a hadith from the Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام, we read that he narrates from the Noble Prophet of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله who said: "The best verse in the Book of Allah is this verse! O Ali! There is no scratch from a piece of wood, nor a stumble of a foot, except due to a sin, and what Allah pardons in the world, He is too Generous to reconsider it (on the Day of Judgment), and what He has punished for in this world, He is too Just to repeat it upon His servant!" [1] In this way, such calamities, in addition to lightening a person's burden, will control them regarding the future.

  2. Although the appearance of the verse is general and includes all calamities, as is usual with generalities, there are exceptions, such as the calamities and problems that overtook the Prophets and the Infallible Imams علیهم السلام, which were for the elevation of their status or for their testing. And also calamities that overtake non-infallibles and have the aspect of a trial. Or calamities that occur due to lack of wisdom, lack of precision, lack of consultation, and negligence in affairs, which are the ontological effect of the person's own actions. In other words, reconciling various verses of the Quran and narrations necessitates that the generality of this verse be specified in certain cases, and this is not a new matter to cause debate among some commentators. In short, calamities and hard difficulties have various philosophies, which have been mentioned in discussions of Monotheism and Divine Justice: the blossoming of talents under the pressure of calamities, warning regarding the future, Divine trial, awakening from pride and negligence, atonement for sin, etc. However, since for most people it has that aspect of punishment and atonement, the above verse has presented it in a general form. Therefore, we read in a hadith: when Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام entered upon Yazid, Yazid looked at him and said: "O Ali! Whatever strikes you of disaster—it is for what your hands have earned!" (alluding that the events of Karbala were the result of your own actions). But Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام immediately replied: "Not so! This verse was not revealed concerning us; rather, what was revealed concerning us is: 'No disaster strikes upon the earth or among yourselves except that it is in a register before We bring it into being—indeed that, for Allah, is easy—in order that you not despair over what has eluded you and not exult [in pride] over what He has given you.' So we are those who do not grieve over what has eluded us of worldly matters, nor do we exult over what we have been given" [1]. This means: the purpose of these calamities is your lack of attachment to the fleeting blessings of the world and is a type of training and trial for you. Then the Imam added: "We are people who will never be grieved for what we have lost, and we are not happy for what we have in hand (we consider all to be fleeting and have our eyes set on the grace and favor of Allah)." We end this discussion with another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: when he was asked for the interpretation of the above verse, he said: "Do you know that Ali عليه السلام and his Household after him were afflicted with calamities; was it because of their deeds? While they are the Household of Purity and infallible from sin." Then he added: "The Messenger of Allah used to repent to Allah and seek forgiveness every day and night a hundred times without any sin; indeed, Allah singles out His friends for calamities so that He may reward them for them, without any sin" [2].

  3. Sometimes some have doubted whether "calamities" in the above verse refers to the calamities of the world, since the world is a house of action, not a house of reward and punishment. But this is a great mistake, for numerous "verses" and "narrations" show that sometimes man sees a portion of the punishment for his deeds in this very world. And the saying that the world is not the house of punishment means that all accounts are not settled, not that there is absolutely no punishment. Denying this reality, for those familiar with verses and narrations, is like denying a self-evident matter.

  4. Sometimes calamities have a collective aspect and are the product of collective sins, as we read in verse 41 of Surah ar-Rum: "Corruption has appeared throughout land and sea by [reason of] what the hands of people have earned so He may let them taste part of [the consequence of] what they have done that perhaps they will return." It is clear that this is about human societies, which fall into disorders because of their actions. And in verse 11 of Surah ar-Ra'd it says: "Indeed, Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves." And similar verses which testify that there is a close connection and link between human actions and the ontological system of their life; if they walk upon the principles of nature (fitrah) and the laws of creation, Divine blessings encompass them, and whenever they become corrupt, their life tends toward corruption. And sometimes it is possible for this case to be true regarding individual humans, and everyone, in exchange for a sin they commit, is afflicted with a calamity in their body, soul, wealth, or belongings, as stated in the above verse [1].

In any case, some individuals might imagine that they can escape this certain law and inevitable Divine tradition; therefore, in the last verse under discussion, He says: "And you will not cause failure [to Allah] upon the earth" (nor is there a place for you in the heavens) (وَ ما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌) [2]. How can you escape the sphere of His power and sovereignty when the entire universe is the arena of the Lord's undisputed rule? And if you imagine that someone can rush to your aid, know that "you have not besides Allah any protector or any helper" (وَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَ لا نَصِيرٍ). The difference between "wali" (protector) and "nasir" (helper) might be from the perspective that a "wali" is a guardian for bringing benefit, and a "nasir" is for repelling harm; or that a "wali" is someone who rises independently for defense, and a "nasir" is someone who stands beside the person and helps them. In reality, the final verse visualizes the weakness and inability of man, and the previous verse visualizes the justice and mercy of the Lord [3].

Key Points

1- Your Calamities are from Yourselves!

This notion exists for many who imagine that the relationship between human actions and Divine recompense is the same as the conventional relationship found in worldly laws and their rewards and punishments. However, we have said many times that this connection is more similar to an ontological connection than a legislative and conventional one. In other words, rewards and punishments are mostly the natural and ontological reflection of human actions that overtake them. The above verses are a clear witness to this reality. In this regard, many narrations have been recorded in Islamic sources, a few of which we mention to complete this discussion:

  1. In one of the sermons of Nahj al-Balagha, it is stated: "No nation was ever taken from the embrace of the comfort and blessings of life except due to the sins they committed, for Allah is never unjust to His servants. If people, at the time of the descent of afflictions and the removal of blessings, were to cry out to their Lord with sincerity of intentions and with hearts full of yearning and love for God, asking Him for compensation, He would surely return to them everything that had fled and rectify for them every corruption" [1].

  2. In Jami' al-Akhbar, another hadith is narrated from the Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام who said: "Indeed, affliction is a discipline for the oppressor, a trial for the believer, a degree for the Prophets, and an honor for the saints" [1]. This hadith is a clear witness for what we explained regarding the exceptions to the verse.

  3. In another hadith in al-Kafi from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated that he said: "When a servant's sins increase and he does not have enough deeds to atone for them, He afflicts him with sorrow to atone for them" [2].

  4. In the book al-Kafi, a chapter is fundamentally dedicated to this subject, and 12 hadiths are mentioned therein in this same regard [3]. Furthermore, all of these are aside from the sins that Allah, according to the explicit text of the above verse, encompasses with His pardon and mercy, which are also numerous in their own right.

2- Correcting a Great Mistake

Some people might misuse this Quranic reality and welcome any calamity that overtakes them with open arms, saying that one must submit to every unpleasant event, and derive the opposite result—namely a sedative result—from this instructive and movement-creating Quranic principle, which is very dangerous. The Quran never says to be submissive in the face of calamities, nor to not strive in removing problems, nor to yield to oppressions, tyrannies, and diseases. Rather, it says: if despite all the effort and striving you performed, difficulties still overcame you, know that you have committed a sin whose result and atonement has overtaken you; think about your past deeds, seek forgiveness for your sins, build yourself, and rectify your weaknesses.
