And if we see that in some narrations this verse is introduced as the best verse of the Quran, it is because of these important educational effects, and because of lightening the human burden on one hand, and reviving the light of hope and love for the Lord in his heart and soul on the other.

3- Who Were the Ashab al-Suffah?

Those who visit the Prophet's Mosque (Masjid al-Nabawi) صلى الله عليه وآله in Medina today see a place next to the mosque and near the Prophet's grave that is slightly higher than the ground, separated from the rest of the mosque with a brief barrier in a beautiful and pleasant manner. Many choose that place for performing prayer and reciting the Quran. This place is a reminder of the "Suffah" and the roofed platform that was prepared by the Prophet for a group of strangers who had accepted Islam and had no place to stay [1]. To explain: the first stranger who accepted Islam and had no place in Medina was a young man from the people of Yamamah named "Juwaybir," whose sensational marriage story with "Dulfa" is one of the most interesting scenes of the struggle against class distinctions in Islamic history. Since Juwaybir had no place for rest and residence, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله allowed him to sleep in the mosque at night. Later, when the number of stranger Muslims increased and they all resided in the mosque, and this matter disrupted the state of the mosque in every respect, an order was given to take them all outside the mosque, so that the mosque would be clean and pure in every way. All the doors that opened from the houses of the companions toward the mosque were closed by the command of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, except for the door of the house of Ali and Fatima al-Zahra (peace be upon them both). At this time, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered a place to be roofed with palm branches, and the strangers and poor of the Muslims to reside there. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself personally looked after them, bringing them bread, dates, and other food items, and other Muslims also looked after them, helping them through Zakat and other charities. They participated in Islamic battles and struggled sincerely. Some verses of the Quran were revealed regarding their virtue, sincerity, purity, and sanctity. Because of their residence in that "Suffah," they were named the "Ashab al-Suffah."

Ayahs

وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِ ٱلْجَوَارِ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ كَٱلْأَعْلَـٰمِ (٣٢)
Among His signs are the ships [that run] on the sea [appearing] like landmarks. (32)
إِن يَشَأْ يُسْكِنِ ٱلرِّيحَ فَيَظْلَلْنَ رَوَاكِدَ عَلَىٰ ظَهْرِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ (٣٣)
If He wishes He stills the wind, whereat they remain standstill on its surface. There are indeed signs in that for every patient and grateful [servant]. (33)
أَوْ يُوبِقْهُنَّ بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ وَيَعْفُ عَن كَثِيرٍ (٣٤)
Or He wrecks them because of what they have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]. (34)
وَيَعْلَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا مَا لَهُم مِّن مَّحِيصٍ (٣٥)
Let those who dispute Our signs know that there is no escape for them. (35)
فَمَآ أُوتِيتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَمَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ (٣٦)
Whatever you have been given are the wares of the life of this world, but what is with Allah is better and more lasting for those who have faith and who put their trust in their Lord (36)

Commentary

The Regular Blowing of Winds and the Movement of Ships are Among His Signs

Once again, these verses turn to the exposition of the signs of the Lord and the proofs of monotheism, continuing the discussion presented in the preceding verses.

Here, it addresses a matter with which humans are deeply involved in their material lives, especially coastal dwellers and sea travelers. It says: "And among His signs are the ships in the sea, like mountains" (وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ الْجَوارِ فِي الْبَحْرِ كَالْأَعْلامِ‌).

"Jawar" (الْجَوارِ) is the plural of "Jariyah," a description for "Sufun" (ships), which has been omitted from the phrase for brevity. Fundamentally, the emphasis of the verse is on the very flow and movement of the ships; for this reason, this specific attribute has been made the subject of the discourse.

The reason the Arabic language refers to young girls as "Jariyah" is due to the flow of youthful vitality throughout their entire being.

"A'lam" (الْأَعْلامِ) is the plural of "Alam" (pronounced like qalam), meaning "mountain." However, its root meaning is a sign or mark that conveys information about something, such as "Alam al-Tariq" (road signs) or "Alam al-Jaysh" (an army's banner) and the like. If a mountain is called "Alam," it is because it is visible from afar, and sometimes a fire was lit upon its peak to serve as a signal for wayfarers; however, the presence or absence of fire does not affect this naming.

In this way, the Holy Quran in this verse—as in numerous other verses—considers the movement of mountain-like ships upon the surface of the seas due to the blowing of regular winds as one of the signs of Allah.

If a small skiff or boat moves across the water due to the wind, it is not particularly significant. What is significant is that mountain-sized ships move by the blowing of gentle currents of air, carrying a large number of passengers and vast amounts of cargo, traversing thousands of kilometers between two points to reach their destination.

Truly, who created the oceans with such vastness, depth, and specific properties of water?

Who endowed wood and other materials, from which ships are built in that specific shape, with the property to remain buoyant upon the water?

And who commanded the winds to blow in a regular manner across the seas and oceans so that anyone wishing to travel from one point to another could utilize them?

Everywhere, order is a sign of intellect and knowledge, and here it is the same.

In principle, if one considers the maps mariners possess regarding wind movements, and the knowledge humanity has acquired about the blowing of winds from the Earth's poles toward the equator and from the equator toward the poles, as well as the alternating breezes from coasts and land toward the sea and from the sea toward the land, one realizes how precisely calculated this matter is.

Of course, in our era, the motive force of ships consists of powerful engines that drive the propellers; nevertheless, the blowing of winds still influences the movement of these ships.

Then, for further emphasis, it adds: "If He wills, He stills the wind, so they remain motionless on its back" (إِنْ يَشَأْ يُسْكِنِ الرِّيحَ فَيَظْلَلْنَ رَواكِدَ عَلى‌ ظَهْرِهِ‌).

And at the end of the verse, to draw a conclusion, it adds: "Indeed in that are signs for everyone patient and grateful" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ).

Yes, in this movement of winds, the flow of ships, the creation of seas, and the specific system and harmony governing these matters, there are various signs of His Pure Essence.

We know that the blowing of winds is primarily due to the difference in temperature between two points on Earth; for air expands due to heat and then moves upward. For this reason, on one hand, it exerts pressure on the surrounding air and sets it in motion, and on the other hand, as it moves upward, it gives its place to the surrounding air. If Allah were to take away only this property of expansion, a deathly stillness and silence would prevail, and sailing ships would remain bewildered and stranded, motionless upon the surface of the oceans.

"Sabbar" (صَبَّارٍ) and "Shakur" (شَكُورٍ) are both intensive forms (mubalaghah), one indicating an abundance of patience and the other an abundance of gratitude.

The reliance on these two attributes in the verse under discussion and several other instances in the Holy Quran [1] points to subtle insights:

These two attributes together provide an expressive depiction of the reality of faith, for the believer is patient in hardships and afflictions and grateful for blessings. Thus, we read in a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Faith is two halves: one half is patience, and one half is gratitude." [2]

[1]: Ibrahim: 5, Luqman: 31, Saba: 19, and the verse under discussion. [2]: Tafsir al-Safi, Majma' al-Bayan, Fakhr Razi, and Qurtubi under verse 31 of Surah Luqman.

Furthermore, studying the secrets of the system of creation requires, on one hand, patience, endurance, perseverance, and the spending of time, and on the other hand, the motivation to thank the Benefactor.

Whenever these two go hand in hand, they prepare a person to study these signs; in principle, studying the secrets of creation is itself a form of gratitude.

Thirdly, when a person boards a ship, these two attributes are more manifest in him than at any other time: patience in the face of the incidents and hardships of the sea, and gratitude upon reaching the intended shore!

Again, in the following verse, to illustrate the greatness of this divine blessing, it adds: "Or He may destroy them because of what their [passengers] have earned" (أَوْ يُوبِقْهُنَّ بِما كَسَبُوا). As we read in previous verses, the calamities that befall man are often the result of his own actions.

Yet, despite this, the grace of Allah encompasses man: "and He pardons much" (وَ يَعْفُ عَنْ كَثِيرٍ).

For if He did not pardon, no one except the Infallibles (Ma'sumin) and the chosen and pure ones would be safe or exempt from divine punishment, as we read in verse 45 of Surah Fatir: "And if Allah were to punish people for what they have earned, He would not leave a moving creature on its back, but He gives them respite until a specified term."

Yes, He can stop the winds from moving so that ships remain motionless in the heart of the oceans, and He can also turn the winds into turbulent storms to crush mountain-like ships and toss them like a blade of straw amidst the waves; but His universal grace prevents this.

"And [so] those who dispute concerning Our signs may know that they have no place of escape" (وَ يَعْلَمَ الَّذِينَ يُجادِلُونَ فِي آياتِنا ما لَهُمْ مِنْ مَحِيصٍ‌) [1]. Indeed, they are not those who would be included in Allah's pardon, for they have risen in opposition knowingly and continue their strife out of enmity and stubbornness. They are barred from the gushing spring of His pardon and mercy, and they have no escape from the clutches of His punishment.

"Mahis" (مَحِيصٍ) is derived from the root "Hays" (rhyming with hayf), meaning to return, deviate, or turn away from something. Since "Mahis" is a noun of place, this word means a place of flight or a sanctuary. [2]

In the final verse under discussion, the discourse is addressed to everyone, saying: "Whatever you have been given is but the passing enjoyment of the life of this world" (فَما أُوتِيتُمْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ فَمَتاعُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا).

Let it not deceive you or make you heedless, nor should you imagine it is always at your disposal. It is a flash of lightning that strikes and is soon extinguished; it is a flame in the face of the wind,

[1]: The phrase "wa ya'lama al-ladhina yujadiluna..." (وَ يَعْلَمَ الَّذِينَ يُجادِلُونَ‌), according to Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf, is in the subjunctive (mansub) because it is conjoined to an omitted causal clause, and the estimation is: "so that He may take vengeance upon them and so that those who dispute may know..." The goal is for Allah to take vengeance on this group and for the disputers to know there is no way of salvation. [2]: In Volume 10 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 325, this word was mentioned as being from the root "mahas," which should be corrected.

a bubble on the surface of the water, and dust in the path of a storm. "But what is with Allah is better and more lasting for those who have believed and upon their Lord they rely" (وَ ما عِنْدَ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ وَ أَبْقى‌ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ‌).

If you can exchange this fleeting enjoyment of the lowly and limited life of the material world for that eternal capital, you will have gained a highly profitable trade and a peerless success! For the blessings of this world are never free from trouble; there is always a thorn beside every rose, and a sting beside every honey. In contrast, the divine reward is pure good, free from any unpleasantness.

On the other hand, whatever these blessings are, they are fleeting, while those are enduring and eternal. What intellect allows a person to forgo such a profitable transaction or become ensnared by pride and heedlessness, letting the glitz and glamour deceive him?! Thus, we also read in verse 38 of Surah At-Tawbah: "Are you satisfied with the life of this world rather than the Hereafter? But the enjoyment of the life of this world, compared with the Hereafter, is but little."

Fundamentally, "al-Hayat al-Dunya" (given its description) refers to the lower and lowly life. It is obvious that the enjoyment and means of utilizing such a life will be as worthless as the life itself.

Therefore, we read in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

"By Allah, the world in comparison to the Hereafter is only like one of you putting this finger of his into the sea; let him see what it brings back." [1]

It is noteworthy that in this verse, the emphasis is placed on the issue of faith and reliance (Tawakkul). This is because the hope for divine rewards is for those who, in addition to faith, have delegated their affairs to Him and are submitted to His will; for Tawakkul is the handing over of one's work and the delegation of the matter. The opposite of this group are those who, due to the love of the world and attachment to its fleeting enjoyment, rise to dispute the signs of Allah and trample upon the truths. In this way, the final verse is like a justification for the preceding verse which spoke of those who dispute the divine signs.

[1]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 3, p. 429 (under verse 38 of Surah At-Tawbah).

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبَـٰٓئِرَ ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ وَإِذَا مَا غَضِبُوا۟ هُمْ يَغْفِرُونَ (٣٧)
—those who avoid major sins and indecencies, and forgive when angered; (37)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لِرَبِّهِمْ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأَمْرُهُمْ شُورَىٰ بَيْنَهُمْ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (٣٨)
those who answer their Lord, maintain the prayer, and [conduct] their affairs by counsel among themselves, and they spend out of what We have provided them; (38)
وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَآ أَصَابَهُمُ ٱلْبَغْىُ هُمْ يَنتَصِرُونَ (٣٩)
those who, when afflicted by oppression, defend themselves. (39)
وَجَزَٰٓؤُا۟ سَيِّئَةٍ سَيِّئَةٌ مِّثْلُهَا ۖ فَمَنْ عَفَا وَأَصْلَحَ فَأَجْرُهُۥ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤٠)
The requital of evil is an evil like it, so whoever excuses and conciliates, his reward lies with Allah. Indeed, He does not like the wrongdoers. (40)

Commentary

People of Faith Do Not Submit to Oppression!

These verses continue the discussion found in the preceding verses regarding the divine reward for believers who put their trust in Allah.

After describing faith and trust (Tawakkul)—both of which are internal states of the heart—the text points to seven parts of their "practical programs," whether in terms of negation or affirmation, individual or social, and material or spiritual. This program outlines the pillars of a healthy society under a righteous and powerful government.

Interestingly, these verses were apparently revealed in Makkah, at a time when the Islamic society had not yet taken shape and an Islamic government did not exist. However, these verses show that from those early days, a correct Islamic vision in these fields was being granted to Muslims through these verses. During the Makkan period, they were under the continuous and persistent training of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) to prepare them for building the future Islamic society.

The first description begins with purification; He says: "The divine reward and what is with Allah is better and more lasting for those who avoid major sins and shameful, disgraceful deeds" (وَ الَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبائِرَ الْإِثْمِ وَ الْفَواحِشَ) [^1]. "Kaba'ir" is the plural of "Kabirah," meaning major sins. But what is the criterion for a sin being "major"? Some have interpreted it as sins for which a promise of divine punishment has been explicitly mentioned in the Quran, and sometimes as sins that entail a legal penalty (Hadd).

[^1]: According to most commentators, "الذين يجتنبون" is coordinated with "للذين آمنوا" in the previous verse, although some have suggested it is a subject (Mubtada) whose predicate (Khabar) is omitted (implied as: and those who avoid... for them is a similar reward), but the first meaning seems more appropriate.

Some have also suggested it refers to innovations (Bid'ah) and creating ideological doubts in the minds of the people.

However, as we have stated before, if we return to the literal meaning of Kabirah, it refers to any sin that is considered great and significant in the view of Islam. One sign of its significance is that a promise of punishment is mentioned regarding it in the Holy Quran. For this reason, in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), Kaba'ir is interpreted as: "Those for which Allah, the Almighty, has made the Fire obligatory" [^1]. Based on this, if the significance and gravity of a sin are discovered through other means, it also takes on the title of a major sin.

"Fawahish" is the plural of "Fahishah," meaning very ugly and indecent acts. Mentioning this term after "Kaba'ir" is a case of mentioning the specific after the general. In truth, after stating that true believers avoid all major sins, more emphasis is placed on shameful and disgraceful sins to highlight their gravity.

Thus, the first signs of faith and trust are abstinence and avoidance of major sins. How can a person claim faith and trust in Allah while being polluted with various sins and having a heart that is a nesting ground for Satan? In the second description, which also pertains to purification, the text speaks of self-control during anger and rage—the most critical state for a human—and says:

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 473.

"They are those who forgive when they are angry" (وَ إِذا ما غَضِبُوا هُمْ يَغْفِرُونَ).

Not only do they not lose the reins of self-control during anger, nor do they commit ugly acts or crimes, but they wash their hearts and the hearts of others from grudges with the water of pardon and forgiveness.

This is a quality that is not found except in the light of true faith and trust in the Truth.

It is interesting that He does not say "they do not get angry," because anger is part of human nature and in some cases—where it is for the sake of Allah and in the path of reclaiming the rights of the oppressed—it is necessary. Rather, He says they do not become polluted with sin when angry; moreover, they seek pardon and forgiveness. And it must be so; how can a person expect divine forgiveness while being vengeful and seeking retribution, recognizing no law when angry? If we see that "anger" is specifically emphasized here, it is because this state is a burning fire that flares up within the human soul, and many are unable to restrain themselves in that state, but true believers never submit to rage and anger.

In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), we read: "Whoever controls himself when he desires, when he fears, and when he is angry, Allah shall forbid his body to the Fire." [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 583.

The next verse points to their third through sixth qualities, saying:

"Those who have answered the call of their Lord and accepted His commands with soul and heart" (وَ الَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِرَبِّهِمْ).

"And have established prayer" (وَ أَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ).

"And their affairs are conducted by consultation among them" (وَ أَمْرُهُمْ شُورى‌ بَيْنَهُمْ) [^1] "And they spend out of what We have provided them in the way of Allah" (وَ مِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ يُنْفِقُونَ).

In the previous verse, the talk was about purifying their existence from sins and overcoming anger, but in the verse under discussion, the talk is about rebuilding their existence in various aspects. The most important of these is answering the call of the Lord and submitting to His command—a matter in which all goodness, virtues, and obedience to divine orders are gathered. They are submitted to His command with their whole being, and they have no will of their own against His will. It must be so, for after purifying the heart and soul from the effects of sin—which are obstacles to the path of Truth—submission and definitive response follow.

However, since among the divine commands there are very important matters that must be specifically pointed out, several important subjects are mentioned afterward, the most important of which is "Prayer." Prayer is the pillar of religion, the link between the creation and the Creator, the educator of souls, the ascension of the believer, and the restrainer from indecency and evil.

After that, the most important social issue is the principle of "Consultation" (Shura), without which all affairs are incomplete. No matter how intellectually powerful a person may be, they view various issues from only one or a few dimensions; thus, other dimensions remain unknown to them. But when issues are raised in consultation and different intellects, experiences, and viewpoints rush to help one another, the issues become fully matured, less flawed, and further from error.

Hence, in a meaningful hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), we read: "No man consults anyone except that he is guided to the right and desired path."

It is noteworthy that the expression here is such that it considers consultation a continuous program of the believers, not just for a fleeting or temporary task. It says all their affairs among them are by way of consultation. Interestingly, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) himself—despite being the Perfect Intellect and being connected to the source of Revelation—would sit in consultation with his companions on various social and executive matters, in war and peace and other important affairs. He would even sometimes prefer their opinion, even if difficulties arose from it, to serve as a model and example for the people, because the blessings of consultation far outweigh its potential harms.

Regarding the importance of "consultation," the "conditions of consultation," the "qualities of those who should be consulted," and the "duty of the consultant," we had detailed discussions under verse 159 of Surah Al-Imran (Vol. 3, p. 142 onwards), which we see no need to repeat. However, a few points must be added here:

A: Consultation is exclusively for executive matters and identifying subjects, not regarding the laws (Ahkam), which must only be taken from the source of Revelation and from the Book and Sunnah. The expression "امرهم" (their affairs) also points to this meaning, because the laws are not the people's business; they are Allah's business.

Therefore, if some commentators like Al-Alusi have expanded its scope to include laws where no specific text (Nass) has been revealed, it is baseless. Especially since we believe there is no matter in Islam except that a general or specific text has been issued regarding it; otherwise, "This day I have perfected for you your religion" (Al-Ma'idah: 3) would not be correct. (The explanation of this meaning should be studied in books of Usul al-Fiqh regarding the invalidity of Ijtihad in the sense of legislation in Islam).

B: Some commentators have said that the occasion of revelation for the phrase "أَمْرُهُمْ شُورى‌ بَيْنَهُمْ" concerns the Ansar. This is either because even before Islam their affairs were based on consultation, or it refers to that group of Ansar who believed before the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) migration, pledged allegiance to him at "Aqaba," and invited him to Madinah (since this Surah is Makkan and these verses were apparently revealed in Makkah).

In any case, the verse is not limited to its occasion of revelation and expresses a general and universal program.

We conclude this discussion with a hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), where he said: "There is no support like consultation, and seeking consultation is the essence of guidance." [^1] It is also noteworthy that the last description mentioned in this verse does not only state spending in financial matters, but spending from everything Allah has provided: from wealth, from knowledge, from intellect, intelligence, and experience, from social influence, and in short, from everything.

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 8, p. 425.

In another description, which is the seventh quality of true believers, He says: "And those who, when an injury or wrong reaches them, do not submit to oppression and seek help from others" (وَ الَّذِينَ إِذا أَصابَهُمُ الْبَغْيُ هُمْ يَنْتَصِرُونَ).

It goes without saying that alongside the duty of "Intisar" (seeking help) against oppression, others also have the duty to help, for seeking help without being helped is futile and vain. In reality, the oppressed is duty-bound to resist the oppressor and cry out, and other believers are duty-bound to respond to him, just as we read in verse 72 of Surah Al-Anfal: "And if they seek help from you in the matter of religion, then it is your duty to help."

This positive and constructive program warns oppressors that if they stain their hands with oppression, the believers will not remain silent and will rise against them. It also gives confidence to the oppressed that if they cry for help, others will rush to their aid.

"Yantasiroon" is from the root "Intisar," meaning seeking help, but some have interpreted it as "Tanasur" (rushing to help one another). However, the result of both is the same given the explanation we provided.

In any case, the duty of every oppressed person is that if they are unable to repel oppression and tyranny alone, they should not remain silent, and by using the strength of others, they should rise to confront the oppression. It is the duty of all Muslims to give a positive response to his call.

However, since helping one another must not deviate from the path of justice, nor lead to revenge, grudges, or exceeding the limits, the next verse immediately makes it conditional, adding: bear in mind that "the recompense for an evil is a punishment like it" (وَ جَزاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ سَيِّئَةٌ مِثْلُها).

Lest you exceed the limit because some of your friends have been oppressed and thus turn into oppressors yourselves. Especially since in societies like the Arab society at the dawn of Islam, the probability of exceeding the limit when responding to oppression was significant, and the act of helping the oppressed had to be separated from mere revenge-seeking.

Of course, the act of the oppressor must be called a "Sayyi'ah" (evil), but his punishment is certainly not an "evil." If the verse refers to it as a "Sayyi'ah," it is actually for the sake of linguistic parallelism, or because from the perspective of the oppressor being punished, it is an "evil." There is also the possibility that calling it a "Sayyi'ah" is because punishment involves causing pain or distress, and causing distress is inherently bad, even though it is considered a good deed when it is for retribution (Qisas) and punishing an oppressor.

This is similar to the expression found in verse 194 of Surah Al-Baqarah: "So whoever transgresses against you, then transgress against him in like manner as he has transgressed against you, and fear Allah (and do not exceed the limits)."

In any case, this expression can be an introduction to the command of forgiveness that comes in the next phrase, as if He is saying: punishment, whatever it may be, is a type of distress, and if the party has become regretful, they are worthy of forgiveness.

In such cases, forgive, for "whoever pardons and makes peace, his reward is with Allah" (فَمَنْ عَفا وَ أَصلَحَ فَأَجْرُهُ عَلَى اللَّهِ).

It is true that a right of his has been lost, and in return, he has apparently received nothing, but because of the forbearance he has shown—a forbearance that is the cause of social cohesion, the reduction of grudges, the increase of love, the cessation of revenge-seeking, and social peace—Allah has taken it upon Himself to grant his reward from His infinite grace. And what a beautiful expression is "على اللَّه" (upon Allah): as if Allah considers Himself indebted to such a person and says his reward is My responsibility! At the end of the verse, He says: "Indeed, He does not love the oppressors" (إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الظَّالِمِينَ).

This sentence may point to several keys:

First, the command to forgive is because, in the case of retribution and punishment, sometimes a person cannot control themselves precisely, exceeds the limit, and joins the ranks of the oppressors.

Second, if the command to forgive is given, it is not for the sake of defending the oppressors, for Allah never loves the oppressors; rather, the goal is to guide the strayed and strengthen social bonds.

Third, those worthy of forgiveness are those who dismount from the steed of oppression, regret their past, and set out to reform themselves—not those oppressors for whom forgiveness only makes them bolder and more insolent.

In clearer terms, forgiveness and punishment each have their specific place. Forgiveness is where a person has the power to take revenge, and if he pardons, it is not from a position of weakness. This forgiveness is constructive, both for the victorious oppressed—as it grants him self-mastery and purity of heart—and for the defeated oppressor, as it compels him to reform himself.

Punishment, revenge, and reciprocal action are for when the oppressor has not yet dismounted from the steed of Satan, and the oppressed has not yet solidified his foundations of power, and forgiveness would be from a position of weakness. It is here that one must take action for punishment.

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), it is stated: "When the Day of Resurrection comes, a caller (on behalf of Allah) will cry out: 'Whoever's reward is upon Allah, let him enter Paradise.' It will be asked: 'Who is it whose reward is upon Allah?' In response, they will be told: 'Those who forgave people.' They shall enter Paradise without an accounting!" [^1] This hadith is, in fact, the result derived from the last verse under discussion, and this is the fundamental line of Islam.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed.

Ayahs

وَلَمَنِ ٱنتَصَرَ بَعْدَ ظُلْمِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَا عَلَيْهِم مِّن سَبِيلٍ (٤١)
As for those who retaliate after being wronged, there is no ground for action against them. (41)
إِنَّمَا ٱلسَّبِيلُ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَظْلِمُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ وَيَبْغُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٤٢)
The ground for action is only against those who oppress the people and commit tyranny in the land in violation of justice. For such there will be a painful punishment. (42)
وَلَمَن صَبَرَ وَغَفَرَ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ (٤٣)
As for someone who endures patiently and forgives—that is indeed the steadiest of courses. (43)

Commentary

Seeking Help is Not a Fault; Committing Oppression is a Fault

These verses are, in fact, an emphasis, explanation, and completion of the previous verses regarding seeking assistance (intisar), punishing the oppressor, and granting pardon and forgiveness in appropriate circumstances. The objective is to establish that punishing and taking revenge against an oppressor is the right of the oppressed, and no one has the right to create obstacles in their path. At the same time, whenever the oppressed person becomes victorious and gains mastery over the oppressor, if they exercise patience and refrain from taking revenge, it will be a great virtue.

First, He says: "And whoever seeks help after being wronged, there is no blame upon them" (وَ لَمَنِ انْتَصَرَ بَعْدَ ظُلْمِهِ فَأُولئِكَ ما عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ) [^1]

[^1]: The expression "ظلمه" (his being wronged) is an instance of adding the infinitive to the object.

Neither does anyone have the right to prevent this action, nor to reproach, blame, or punish them, nor should anyone hesitate in helping such an oppressed person. This is because seeking assistance and help is the undeniable right of every oppressed person, and helping the oppressed is the duty of every free and awakened human being.

"The blame is only against those who oppress people and act insolently in the land without right" (إِنَّمَا السَّبِيلُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَظْلِمُونَ النَّاسَ وَ يَبْغُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِ). In addition to punishment and retribution in this world, "for them there is a painful punishment in the Hereafter" (أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

Regarding the difference between the phrase "oppress people" (يظلمون الناس) and the phrase "act insolently in the land without right" (يَبْغُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِ), some commentators have considered the first phrase to refer to the issue of "oppression and tyranny" and the second to "arrogance and self-superiority." [^1] Others have noted the first phrase as referring to "oppression" and the second to "rebellion against the Islamic government."

"Baghy" (insolence/rebellion) originally means the effort and striving to obtain something, but it is very often applied to efforts made to usurp the rights of others and to transgress Divine limits and rights. Therefore, "oppression" (zulm) has a specific meaning, while "baghy" has a general meaning, encompassing every kind of transgression and violation of Divine rights.

The expression "without right" (بغير الحق) is also an emphasis for this meaning, and thus—

[^1]: Tafsir "al-Kashshaf," "Ruh al-Ma'ani," and "Ruh al-Bayan" under the verses being discussed.

the second phrase is an instance of mentioning the general after the specific.

In the last verse under discussion, it returns once more to the issue of patience, endurance, and pardon, to emphasize again the reality that the right of revenge, retaliation, and punishment for the oppressed against the oppressor never prevents the matter of passing over, pardoning, and forgiving. He says:

"But whoever is patient and forgives—indeed, that is among the matters of steadfast determination" (وَ لَمَنْ صَبَرَ وَ غَفَرَ إِنَّ ذلِكَ لَمِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ). [^1] "Azm" originally means "the decision to perform a task," and it is applied to a firm will. The expression "azm al-umur" (matters of determination) might be an indication that this is among the tasks that Allah has commanded and will never be abrogated, or it is among the tasks toward which a human must have a firm resolve. Whichever of these two meanings it may be, it is evidence of the importance of this action.

It is noteworthy that the issue of "patience" is mentioned before "forgiveness," because if there is no patience and endurance, the matter will not lead to pardon and forgiveness; the reins of the self will slip from the person's hand, and they will insist upon revenge.

Once again, we remind of the reality that pardon and forgiveness are desirable only when they come from a position of power, and when the other party makes good use of it. The expression "لَمِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ" might also emphasize this same meaning, because decision-making occurs in a context where a person is capable and powerful enough to carry out a matter. In any case, a pardon that is imposed by the oppressor, or one that makes him bolder and more audacious in his actions, is never desirable.

[^1]: The lam in "لمن صبر" is the lam of oath, and in "لَمِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ" it is the lam of emphasis; both express the importance of this Divine command, which is forgiveness.

In some narrations, the above verses have been interpreted as referring to the uprising of Hazrat al-Mahdi (may Allah hasten his reappearance) and his revenge, along with his companions, against the oppressors and the corrupters on earth. As we have repeatedly stated, such interpretations are of the nature of explaining the most clear and prominent instance (misdaq) and do not prevent the generality of the verse's concept. [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 4, p. 585.

Ayahs

وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن وَلِىٍّ مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ ۗ وَتَرَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ يَقُولُونَ هَلْ إِلَىٰ مَرَدٍّ مِّن سَبِيلٍ (٤٤)
Those whom Allah leads astray have no guardian apart from Him. You will see the wrongdoers, when they sight the punishment, saying, ‘Is there any way for a retreat?’ (44)
وَتَرَىٰهُمْ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْهَا خَـٰشِعِينَ مِنَ ٱلذُّلِّ يَنظُرُونَ مِن طَرْفٍ خَفِىٍّ ۗ وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَأَهْلِيهِمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ فِى عَذَابٍ مُّقِيمٍ (٤٥)
You will see them being exposed to it, humbled by abasement, furtively looking askance. The faithful will say, ‘Indeed the losers are those who have ruined themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed, the wrongdoers will abide in lasting punishment. (45)
وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ يَنصُرُونَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن سَبِيلٍ (٤٦)
They have no awliya to help them besides Allah. There is no way out for those whom Allah leads astray.’ (46)

Commentary

Is There a Way to Return?

In the preceding verses, the discussion concerned the wrongdoers, the oppressors, and the transgressors. The verses under discussion point to the end of this group's affair and aspects of their punishments.

First, it counts them among the astray who have no guardian or protector, stating: "And he whom Allah sends astray—for him there is no protector after Him" (وَ مَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللَّهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ).

For those familiar with the Quranic expressions regarding guidance and misguidance, it is perfectly clear that neither guidance nor misguidance is forced; rather, they are the direct result of human actions. Sometimes a person performs an act such that Allah withdraws His success (tawfiq) from him, removes the light of guidance from his heart, and leaves him in the darkness of misguidance.

This is the very essence of free will, just as if someone, due to persistence in drinking wine, becomes afflicted with various diseases; these ominous effects were not unwanted, but rather he prepared them with his own hands. Allah's work is the linking of causes to effects and granting properties to things, and for this reason, the results are sometimes attributed to Him [1]. In any case, this is one of the most painful punishments for these wrongdoers. Then it adds: "And you will see the wrongdoers, when they see the punishment, saying, 'Is there any way to return [to the world to compensate for these misfortunes]?'" (وَ تَرَى الظَّالِمِينَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا الْعَذابَ يَقُولُونَ هَلْ إِلى‌ مَرَدٍّ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ).

[1]: A detailed explanation on this subject is provided in Volume 19 under verse 36 of Surah Az-Zumar, clarifying all aspects of this issue.

The Holy Quran repeatedly mentions the request of the disbelievers and wrongdoers to return. Sometimes this occurs at the threshold of death, as mentioned in verses 99 and 100 of Surah Al-Mu'minun: "Until, when death comes to one of them, he says, 'My Lord, send me back, that I might do righteousness in that which I left behind.'"

And sometimes it is on the Day of Resurrection, at the moment they are placed beside the Fire, as in: "If you could but see when they are made to stand before the Fire and will say, 'Oh, would that we could be returned [to the world] and not deny the signs of our Lord and be among the believers'" (Al-An'am: 27).

But in whatever form this request is made, it will be met with a negative response, because returning is impossible. This is an unchangeable Divine law; just as a human does not return from old age to youth, from youth to childhood, or from childhood to the embryonic world, a backward movement from the world of Barzakh and the Hereafter to this world is also impossible.

The next verse describes the third punishment of this group as follows: "And you will see them being exposed to it [the Fire], humbled by disgrace, looking with a stealthy glance" (وَ تَراهُمْ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْها خاشِعِينَ مِنَ الذُّلِّ يَنْظُرُونَ مِنْ طَرْفٍ خَفِيٍ) [1].

[1]: "Tarf" (rhyming with barf) is a verbal noun meaning the movement of the eye, and "tarfat al-ayn" means a single blink or glance. Additionally, the pronoun in "alayha" refers to the punishment; although "adhab" (punishment) is masculine, the feminine pronoun refers to it because it implies the Fire (Nar) or Hell (Jahannam).

A state of intense horror and anxiety dominates their entire being, and humiliation and submission envelop them from head to toe. There is no longer any sign of that arrogance, pugnacity, rebellion, oppression, tyranny, or the tormenting of the oppressed. They look at the fire of Hell with a stealthy glance! This is a depiction of the state of someone who is intensely afraid of something and does not want to see it with his full gaze, yet at the same time cannot remain heedless of it; thus, he constantly monitors it, but from the corner of his eye! Some commentators have said that "tarf khafi" here means looking with half-closed eyes, because due to the intensity of horror, they lack the power to open their eyes fully, or they are so exhausted and lifeless that they do not even have the strength to open their eyes completely! When a person's state before entering the Fire is like this, what will happen to him when he enters Hell and is placed amidst its painful torment?!

The final punishment mentioned here is hearing the painful reproach and blame of the believers, as stated at the end of the verse: "And those who believe will say: 'Indeed, the [real] losers are the ones who lost themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّ الْخاسِرِينَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَ أَهْلِيهِمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ).

What loss is greater than for a person to lose his own existence, and then his spouse, children, and relatives, and to be trapped within the Divine punishment, burning with the fire of regret and separation as well?

Then they add: "Unquestionably, the wrongdoers are in a lasting punishment" (أَلا إِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ فِي عَذابٍ مُقِيمٍ).

A punishment for which there is no hope of cessation, and for which no time or duration has been set; a punishment that burns the inner soul and the outer body of everyone! It is not unlikely that the speakers of these words are the believers of perfect faith, in the first rank of the Prophets, the Awliya (saints), and their special followers, because they are proud and honored for their lack of sin, and they have the right to say such things there. They are the oppressed who suffered much at the hands of these wrongdoers, and it is fitting that they should be the speakers of such words on that Day (this meaning is also pointed out in some narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام) [1]. It is also necessary to note that "eternal punishment" for these wrongdoers is an indication that the intended meaning is the disbelievers, just as this expression appears in some other verses of the Quran: "And the disbelievers—they are the wrongdoers" (Al-Baqarah: 254).

The next verse also bears witness to this reality, saying: "And they will have no protectors to aid them other than Allah" (وَ ما كانَ لَهُمْ مِنْ أَوْلِياءَ يَنْصُرُونَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ). They have severed their ties with the sincere servants, the Prophets, and the Awliya; therefore, they have no helper or supporter there. Material powers also become useless there, and for this reason, they stand alone against the Divine punishment.

To emphasize this meaning, it adds at the end of the verse: "And he whom Allah sends astray—for him there is no way [of escape]" (وَ مَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللَّهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ).

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 586.

In the previous verses, we read: "And he whom Allah sends astray—for him there is no protector after Him."

There, it negates a "protector and guardian," and here it negates a "way of salvation," because to reach a destination, there must be both a "way" and a "guide." However, these astray ones are deprived of both.

Ayahs

ٱسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لِرَبِّكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ يَوْمٌ لَّا مَرَدَّ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ مَا لَكُم مِّن مَّلْجَإٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّكِيرٍ (٤٧)
Respond to your Lord before there comes a day for which there will be no revoking from Allah. On that day you will have no refuge, nor will you have [any chance for] denial [of your sins]. (47)
فَإِنْ أَعْرَضُوا۟ فَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظًا ۖ إِنْ عَلَيْكَ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ۗ وَإِنَّآ إِذَآ أَذَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً فَرِحَ بِهَا ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌۢ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَإِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ كَفُورٌ (٤٨)
But if they disregard [your warnings], [keep in mind that] We have not sent you as their keeper. Your duty is only to communicate. Indeed when We let man taste Our mercy, he boasts about it; but should an ill visit him because of what his hands have sent ahead, then man is very ungrateful. (48)
لِّلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ يَهَبُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ إِنَـٰثًا وَيَهَبُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ٱلذُّكُورَ (٤٩)
To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. He creates whatever He wishes; He gives females to whomever He wishes, and males to whomever He wishes, (49)
أَوْ يُزَوِّجُهُمْ ذُكْرَانًا وَإِنَـٰثًا ۖ وَيَجْعَلُ مَن يَشَآءُ عَقِيمًا ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌ قَدِيرٌ (٥٠)
or He combines them males and females, and makes sterile whomever He wishes. Indeed, He is all-knowing, all-powerful. (50)

Commentary

Children are All His Gifts

Since the previous verses reflected a portion of the painful punishment and the horror and terror of the disbelievers and oppressors, the verses under discussion address all people, warning them to respond to their Lord's call and return to the path of truth before they are overtaken by such a sinister fate.

It says: "Respond to your Lord before there comes a Day which there is no averting from Allah" (اسْتَجِيبُوا لِرَبِّكُمْ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ يَوْمٌ لا مَرَدَّ لَهُ مِنَ اللَّهِ‌) [^1]. And if you imagine that on that day there is any refuge other than the shadow of His grace, or any defender other than His mercy, you are mistaken, for "on that Day there is for you no refuge to shield you from Allah's punishment, nor any helper to defend you" (ما لَكُمْ مِنْ مَلْجَإٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ نَكِيرٍ).

The phrase "a Day which there is no averting from Allah" refers to the Day of Resurrection, not the day of death. The expression "from Allah" indicates that against His will and command regarding the lack of return, no one else can make a different decision.

In any case, several ways are conceivable to escape punishment, all of which are closed on that day: one is returning from there to the world to compensate for errors and sins.

Another is the existence of a refuge where a person remains immune.

And finally, the existence of someone who rises to defend the person.

Each of the three phrases in the above verse refers to the negation of one of these three paths.

Some have interpreted the phrase "ما لَكُمْ مِنْ نَكِيرٍ" to mean that you can never deny your sins there, because the evidence and witnesses are so numerous that there is no room for denial; however, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

In the next verse, the address is directed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as a consolation to him, stating: despite all this, "if they turn away, do not be grieved; We have not sent you as a guardian over them to forcibly prevent them from deviation" (فَإِنْ أَعْرَضُوا فَما أَرْسَلْناكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظاً). "Your duty is only to convey the divine message," whether they accept it or not (إِنْ عَلَيْكَ إِلَّا الْبَلاغُ‌).

You must fully convey your divine mission and complete the proof (atmam al-hujjah) upon them; ready hearts will accept it, even if a large group ignorantly turns away—you have no responsibility in this regard.

A similar meaning appears at the beginning of this same Surah, stating: "And you are not a disposer of affairs over them" (وَ ما أَنْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِوكِيلٍ)—you are not commissioned to force them to accept the truth (Ash-Shura, Verse 6).

Then, it portrays the state of this faithless and turning-away group, saying:

"When We let man taste mercy from Us, he exults in it with a state of pride and negligence, becoming heedless of the remembrance of Allah" (وَ إِنَّا إِذا أَذَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً فَرِحَ بِها).

"But if an evil strikes them because of what their hands have sent forth, then indeed, man is ungrateful" (وَ إِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَإِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ كَفُورٌ).

Neither do divine blessings awaken him through the motivation of thanking the Benefactor to lead him toward gratitude, recognition, and obedience, nor do the punishments that overtake him because of his sins wake him from the sleep of negligence, nor does the call of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله affect him.

The means of guidance from the perspective of "legislation" (tashri') is the call of Allah's messengers, and from the perspective of "creation" (takwin), it is sometimes blessing and sometimes calamity. However, none of these matters prove effective for these heedless, blind-hearted people; they are the ones at fault, not you—you have fulfilled the duty of conveying the message.

The expression "When We let [him] taste" (اذا اذقنا) in the above verse regarding the Lord's mercy, and in some other verses of the Quran regarding divine punishment, may indicate that the blessings and calamities of this world, whatever they may be, are a small thing compared to the blessings and calamities of the Hereafter. Or it may mean that these low-capacity individuals become intoxicated and proud with a slight blessing, and hopeless and ungrateful with a slight calamity.

It is also noteworthy that He attributes the blessing to Himself, as it is the requirement of His mercy, while He attributes calamities and afflictions to them, as they are the result of their own actions.

We have also previously mentioned that the term "man" (al-insan) in such verses refers to the nature of the "unrefined human" who possesses short-sightedness and a weak, small spirit; its repetition in the above verse is an emphasis on this very meaning.

Then, to demonstrate the reality that every kind of blessing and mercy in this world is from Allah, and no one possesses anything of their own, He points to a general principle and a clear manifestation of it, saying: "To Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth; He creates what He wills" (لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ). For this reason, everyone is a recipient of His bounty and in need of His grace and mercy; therefore, neither pride at the time of blessing is logical, nor despair at the time of calamity! A clear example of this reality—that no one has anything of their own and everything is from Him—is that: "He bestows to whom He wills females, and He bestows to whom He wills males" (يَهَبُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ إِناثاً وَ يَهَبُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ الذُّكُورَ).

"Or He joins them, males and females; and He renders whom He wills barren" (أَوْ يُزَوِّجُهُمْ ذُكْراناً وَ إِناثاً وَ يَجْعَلُ مَنْ يَشاءُ عَقِيماً). Thus, people are divided into four groups: those who have only sons and long for a daughter, those who have daughters and long for a son, those who have both, and a group that lacks any children and whose hearts yearn for them.

And it is strange that no one—neither in past times nor today when sciences and knowledge have advanced greatly—has the power of choice in this matter. Despite all efforts and endeavors, no one has yet been able to grant children to the truly barren, or to determine the gender of the child according to human desire. Although the role of certain foods and medicines in increasing the probability of the birth of a boy or a girl cannot be denied, it must be known that these only increase the probability, and the result of none is certain.

This is a prominent example of human inability on one hand, and a clear sign of Allah's ownership, sovereignty, and creativity on the other. What a vivid and manifest example!

Interestingly, in these verses, females (inath) are mentioned before males (dhukur) to, on one hand, express the importance Islam gives to reviving the status of women, and on the other hand, to tell those who disliked the birth of a daughter due to false notions that He gives what you do not desire contrary to your wish—and this is proof that the choice is not in your hands.

The expression "bestows" (yahab) is clear evidence that both daughters and sons are divine gifts, and differentiating between the two is not correct from the perspective of a true Muslim; both are His "gift" (hibah).

The expression "He joins them" (yuzawwijuhum) here does not mean marriage, but rather the intention is the combining of these two blessings for a group of humans. In other words, the word "tazwij" sometimes means combining different things or various types, because "zawj" originally means two things or two persons that are paired together.

Some have interpreted the above expression to mean the birth of sons and daughters sequentially, and some as the birth of twins where one is a boy and the other a girl.

However, there is no sign of these interpretations in the above expression.

Furthermore, it is not consistent with the literal meaning of the verse, as the verse intends to inform of a third group who possess both daughters and sons.

In any case, not only in the subject of the birth of children but in everything, Allah's will is the absolute ruler, and He is an All-Knowing and All-Wise Sovereign whose knowledge and power are paired together. Therefore, at the end of the verse, it adds: "Indeed, He is All-Knowing, All-Powerful" (إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ قَدِيرٌ).

It is also necessary to note that "barren" (aqim) from the root "aqm" originally means dryness and stiffness that prevents the acceptance of an effect. Barren women are those whose wombs are not ready to accept the male's seed and nurture a child. "Barren" winds are called so because they are unable to pollinate rain-bearing clouds. A "barren" day refers to a day in which there is no joy or happiness, and the fact that the Day of Resurrection is referred to as a "barren day" (yawm aqim) is because there is no day after it in which they can compensate for the past.

Finally, if foods whose microbes have been completely killed are called "sterilized" (mu'aqqam), it is because these harmful organisms can no longer grow in them.

[^1]: "From Allah" (min Allah) in the above phrase may mean "from the side of Allah," meaning there is no return from Allah's side; and it may mean "against Allah," meaning that against the Lord's will, no one has the power to return [anyone] to the world.

Ayah

۞ وَمَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُكَلِّمَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا وَحْيًا أَوْ مِن وَرَآئِ حِجَابٍ أَوْ يُرْسِلَ رَسُولًا فَيُوحِىَ بِإِذْنِهِۦ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِىٌّ حَكِيمٌ (٥١)
It is not [possible] for any human that Allah should speak to him except through revelation or from behind a veil, or send a messenger who reveals by His permission whatever He wishes. Indeed He is all-exalted, all-wise. (51)

Circumstances of Revelation

Some commentators have mentioned a circumstance of revelation for this verse, the summary of which is as follows: A group of Jews came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "Why do You not speak with Allah? And why do You not look at Him? If You are a Prophet, just as Moses عليه السلام spoke with Him and looked at Him, then You do the same; we will never believe in You unless You do this." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied: "Moses never saw Allah." It was at this point that the above verse was revealed (explaining the nature of the prophets' communication with Allah Almighty). [^1]

Commentary

The Methods of Communication between Prophets and Allah

As we stated at the beginning of this Surah, there is a specific emphasis in this Surah on the issue of revelation (Wahy) and prophethood. The Surah began with the subject of revelation and ends with the subject of revelation in these very verses.

Since the preceding verses spoke of Divine blessings, these verses speak of the most important of the Lord's blessings and His most fruitful gifts to the world of humanity, which is the issue of revelation and the connection of the prophets with God.

First, He says: "It is not fitting for any human being that Allah should speak to him (and meet him face to face, for He is sanctified from body and physicality) except through revelation and mysterious inspiration to his heart" (وَمَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُكَلِّمَهُ اللَّهُ إِلَّا وَحْياً).

"Or hearing the words of the Lord from behind a veil" (أَوْ مِنْ وَرَاءِ حِجَابٍ).

Just as Moses bin Imran spoke at Mount Tur and heard the response, through sound waves that Allah created in the atmosphere without anyone seeing Him, for He cannot be seen.

"Or through sending a messenger who conveys the Divine message to him" (أَوْ يُرْسِلَ رَسُولًا).

Just as the Angel of Revelation and the Divine messenger, "Gabriel the Trustworthy," used to descend upon the Prophet of Islam.

"At this time, the Divine messenger, by the command of the Lord, reveals to His Prophet whatever Allah wills" (فَيُوحِيَ بِإِذْنِهِ مَا يَشَاءُ).

Indeed, there is no way for Allah to speak with His servants except through these three ways, "for He is Most High and All-Wise" (إِنَّهُ عَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ).

He is higher than to be seen or to speak with a tongue, and all His actions are wise, and His connection with the prophets is based on a precise plan.

This verse is, in fact, a clear answer to those who, due to ignorance, might imagine that the issue of revelation is proof that prophets see God and speak with Him. This verse reflects the spirit and reality of revelation in a concise and precise manner.

From the totality of the verse, it appears that the way prophets connect with God is limited to three methods:

  1. Casting into the heart—which occurred for many prophets, such as Noah, of whom He says: فَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْهِ أَنِ اصْنَعِ الْفُلْكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا وَوَحْيِنَا: "We revealed to Noah: 'Build the ship under Our eyes and according to Our command'" (Al-Mu'minun: 27).

  2. From behind a veil—just as Allah spoke with Moses at Mount Tur: وَكَلَّمَ اللَّهُ مُوسَى تَكْلِيماً (An-Nisa: 164). Some also consider "from behind a veil" to include true dreams (Ru'ya Sadiqa).

  3. Through sending a messenger—as mentioned regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: قُلْ مَنْ كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلَى قَلْبِكَ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ: "Say: Whoever is an enemy to Gabriel (is an enemy to Allah), for he has brought it (the Quran) down upon your heart by Allah's command" (Al-Baqarah: 97).

Of course, revelation to the Prophet of Islam was not limited to this method; it occurred through other methods as well.

It is also noteworthy that revelation through casting into the heart sometimes occurred while awake, as mentioned above, and sometimes in sleep and true dreams, as mentioned regarding Abraham and the command to sacrifice Ishmael (though some have considered that an instance of "from behind a veil").

Although the main branches of revelation are the three mentioned in the above verse, some of these branches themselves have sub-branches. For instance, some believe that the descent of revelation through sending the Angel of Revelation occurred in four ways:

  1. Where the angel, without appearing to the Prophet, would cast it into his spirit, as we read in a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "The Holy Spirit (Ruh al-Qudus) breathed into my heart that no soul shall die until it has completed its provision; so fear Allah and be moderate in seeking."

  2. Sometimes the angel would take the form of a human and address the Prophet, telling him the matters (as mentioned in Hadiths regarding Gabriel appearing in the form of Dihyah al-Kalbi [^1]).

  3. Sometimes it was in a form that sounded like a bell in his ear, and this was the hardest type of revelation for the Prophet, such that even on very cold days, his noble forehead would be drenched in sweat, and if he were riding a mount, the mount would become so heavy that it would involuntarily sit on the ground.

  4. Sometimes Gabriel would appear to the Prophet in his original form in which Allah created him, and this occurred only twice in the Prophet's lifetime (as will be explained in Surah An-Najm, verse 12). [^2]

[^1]: "Tafsir al-Qurtubi," Vol. 8, p. 5873. [^1]: "Dihyah bin Khalifah al-Kalbi" was the foster brother of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and was considered among the most handsome people of that time. When Gabriel came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, he would appear in that countenance (Majma' al-Bahrayn, root d-h-y). He was among the famous companions of the Prophet and known for his beautiful appearance. The Holy Prophet sent him as an envoy to the Roman Caesar "Heraclius" in the year 6 or 7 AH, and he lived until the time of Mu'awiyah's caliphate (Dehkhoda Dictionary). [^2]: Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 7, p. 306.

Key Points

1- Revelation in Lexicon, Quran, and Sunnah:

The origin of "Wahy" (revelation), as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, is a swift sign, whether it be through symbolic speech, a sound devoid of linguistic structure, a gesture with limbs (eyes, hand, or head), or through writing.

From these expressions, it is well understood that revelation involves a "sign" on one hand and "speed" on the other. For this reason, this word has been employed for the mysterious and swift connection of the prophets with the world of the unseen and the Pure Essence of the Lord.

In the Holy Quran and the language of narrations, "Wahy" is used in various meanings: sometimes regarding prophets, sometimes regarding other humans, sometimes regarding symbolic communication between humans, sometimes the mysterious communication of devils, and sometimes regarding animals.

The most comprehensive statement in this regard is what has been narrated from Ali عليه السلام in response to a person who asked about the issue of revelation. The Imam divided it into seven types:

  1. The revelation of messengership and prophethood, such as: إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ كَمَا أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَى نُوحٍ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ... "Indeed, We have revealed to You as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him..." [^1]

  2. Revelation in the sense of inspiration (Ilham), such as: وَأَوْحَى رَبُّكَ إِلَى النَّحْلِ: "And your Lord inspired the bee." [^2]

  3. Revelation in the sense of a gesture (sign), such as: فَخَرَجَ عَلَى قَوْمِهِ مِنَ الْمِحْرَابِ فَأَوْحَى إِلَيْهِمْ أَنْ سَبِّحُوا بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا: "So he (Zechariah) came out to his people from the prayer chamber and signaled to them to glorify (Allah) morning and evening." [^1]

  4. Revelation in the sense of decree (Taqdir), such as: وَأَوْحَى فِي كُلِّ سَمَاءٍ أَمْرَهَا: "And He assigned in every heaven its command (decree)." [^2]

  5. Revelation in the sense of a command, such as: وَإِذْ أَوْحَيْتُ إِلَى الْحَوَارِيِّينَ أَنْ آمِنُوا بِي وَبِرَسُولِي: "And [remember] when I commanded the disciples to believe in Me and My messenger." [^3]

  6. Revelation in the sense of fabricating lies, such as: وَكَذَلِكَ جَعَلْنَا لِكُلِّ نَبِيٍّ عَدُوًّا شَيَاطِينَ الْإِنْسِ وَالْجِنِّ يُوحِي بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَى بَعْضٍ زُخْرُفَ الْقَوْلِ غُرُوراً: "And thus We have made for every prophet an enemy—devils from mankind and jinn, inspiring to one another decorative speech in delusion." [^4]

  7. Revelation in the sense of informing, such as: وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْرَاتِ: "And We made them leaders guiding by Our command, and We informed them of the doing of good deeds." [^5] [^6]

Of course, some of these seven types can have branches, according to which the instances of the use of "Wahy" in the Book and Sunnah will increase. Thus, Taflisi in the book Wujuh al-Quran counted ten aspects for revelation, and some have considered the number even higher.

However, from one perspective, from the totality of the uses of "Wahy" and its derivatives, it can be concluded that revelation from the Lord is of two types: "Legislative Revelation" (Wahy Tashri'i) and "Creative/Existential Revelation" (Wahy Takwini).

"Legislative Revelation" is that which was sent to the prophets, being a special connection between them and God through which they received Divine commands and truths.

"Creative Revelation" is, in reality, those instincts, talents, conditions, and specific creative laws that Allah has placed within the various beings of the world.

2- The Mysterious Reality of Revelation

Much has been said regarding the "nature of revelation," but since this mysterious connection is beyond the limits of our perceptions, these statements cannot provide a clear depiction of the issue, and sometimes they have even led astray. What needs to be said is, in fact, expressed in a condensed and beautiful way in the verse under discussion, and beyond that, the efforts of scholars in this discussion have not reached anywhere. Nevertheless, it is necessary to mention some of the interpretations that ancient and modern philosophers have offered regarding revelation:

A- The Interpretation of Some Ancient Philosophers

Based on detailed premises, they believed that revelation consists of an extraordinary connection of the Prophet's soul with the "Active Intellect" (Aql al-Fa'al), the shadow of which also extends over the realm of "Common Sense" (Hiss Mushtarak) and "Imagination" (Khayal).

To explain: they believed that the human spirit possesses three powers: "Common Sense," with which it perceives the forms of sensible things; the "Power of Imagination," with which it perceives particular mental forms; and the "Intellectual Power," with which it perceives universal forms.

This is on one hand. On the other hand, they believed in the nine Ptolemaic spheres and believed in a "disembodied soul" for the spheres (like the soul for our body). They added: these celestial souls receive inspiration from disembodied beings called "Intellects," and thus they posited "nine intellects" corresponding to the "nine spheres."

Thirdly, they believed that human souls and spirits, to actualize their talents and perceive truths, must receive grace from a disembodied existence they called the "Active Intellect," which was named the "Tenth Intellect." It was called the "Active Intellect" because it was the cause of actualizing the talents of the particular intellects.

Fourthly, they believed that the stronger the human spirit, the greater its connection and union with the Active Intellect, which is the source and treasury of information. Therefore, a strong and perfect spirit can, in the shortest time, acquire the broadest information from the "Active Intellect" by Allah's command.

Furthermore, the stronger the power of imagination, the better it can clothe these concepts in the dress of sensory forms; and the stronger the common sense becomes, the better the human perceives external sensible forms.

Then, from the sum of these premises, they concluded: because the Prophet's spirit is extraordinarily strong, its connection with the Active Intellect is very great, and for this reason, he can, most of the time, receive information in a universal form from the Active Intellect.

And since his imaginative power is also very strong, and yet subordinate to the intellectual power, it can give suitable sensible forms to those universal forms received from the Active Intellect, and he sees them in sensory garments on the horizon of his mind! For example, if those universal truths are of the nature of meanings and rulings, he hears them in the form of very rhythmic words, in the ultimate eloquence and rhetoric, from the tongue of a person in ultimate perfection! And because his imaginative power has complete mastery over common sense, it can give these forms a sensory aspect, and the Prophet sees that person with his eyes and hears his words with his ears!

Analysis: Of course, this statement relies on premises that are today recognized as rejected, including the nine Ptolemaic spheres; the souls and intellects belonging to them are currently considered part of ancient myths because there is no evidence to prove them, or we even have evidence to the contrary.

On the other hand, this hypothesis is not consistent with what is clearly understood from the verses of the Quran regarding revelation. For the verses of the Quran explicitly count revelation as a type of connection with God that sometimes occurs through inspiration to the heart, sometimes through the mediation of the Angel of Revelation, or hearing sound waves. The belief that these are the result of the activity of the imaginative power and common sense and the like is very baseless and inconsistent with the Quranic verses. Another major flaw is that it ranks the Prophet alongside philosophers and geniuses, with a stronger intellect and a more powerful spirit, while we know that the path of revelation is distinct from the path of intellectual perceptions. This group of philosophers, without realizing it, has set about destroying the foundations of revelation and prophethood; "and because they did not see the truth, they struck the path of fable."

Further explanation on this subject will come within the upcoming discussions.

B- The Interpretation of a Group of Modern Philosophers Regarding Revelation

This group of philosophers briefly considers revelation as one of the manifestations of the "subconscious" or "unconscious mind."

In the Encyclopedia of the Twentieth Century, under the entry "Wahy," it states: "Westerners until the sixteenth century AD, like other nations, believed in revelation because their religious books were full of reports of the prophets. Modern science came and drew a line through all spiritual and metaphysical discussions, and the issue of revelation was also counted among ancient legends! ...

The nineteenth century AD arrived; the world of the spirit was proven by scientists with the help of sensory evidence, and the issue of revelation was revived once again. They followed these discussions on an empirical and practical basis and reached results that, although different from the view of Islamic scholars, are considered a prominent step toward proving an important subject that was once considered a superstition."

This group studied spiritual matters, and so far (at the time of the Encyclopedia's compilation), fifty large volumes have been written by the said group regarding the above topics, and many spiritual issues were solved with their help, including the issue of revelation. [^1]

There is much to say in this regard, but the gist of their words is that they consider revelation to be the "manifestation of the unconscious mind" (the hidden conscience), which is many times stronger and more powerful than the conscious mind. Since the prophets were extraordinary men, their hidden conscience was also very powerful, and its outpourings were extraordinarily important and noteworthy.

Analysis: It is obvious that what this group has said is merely a hypothesis, and they have provided no evidence for it. In reality, they have introduced the prophets as men with intellectual genius and greatness of personality, without accepting their connection with the Origin of the universe, Allah, and the receiving of knowledge from His side and from outside their own existence.

All their error stems from the fact that they wanted to understand revelation with the criteria of their empirical sciences and to negate whatever is outside this realm, considering the beings of the world equal to what they have perceived and considering what they have not perceived as non-existent.

This way of thinking has left sinister effects not only on the discussion of revelation but on many other philosophical and ideological discussions. Fundamentally, this way of thinking is ruined from its foundation because they have not proven with any evidence the restriction of all beings in the world to material beings and their accidents.

C- Intellectual Genius

Some others have taken the matter even further and have officially imagined revelation to be the result of the intellectual genius of the prophets. They say they were men of pure nature and extraordinary genius with which they perceived the interests of human society and presented them to humans in the form of teachings and laws.

This statement is, in reality, an explicit denial of the prophethood of the prophets, a rejection of all their words, and accusing them of various types of falsehood (Allah forbid).

To put it more clearly, none of these are interpretations of revelation; they are hypotheses that they have fashioned within the limits of their own thoughts. Because they did not want to accept that there are other truths beyond their knowledge, they were led into these wildernesses.

The Truth of the Matter Regarding Revelation

Without a doubt, we cannot gain much information about the connection of revelation and its reality, because this is a type of perception outside the limits of our perceptions and a type of connection outside our known connections. The world of revelation is for us an unknown world and beyond our perceptions.

Truly, how does a human made of clay connect with the Origin of the universe? And how does the Eternal, Everlasting, and Infinite Allah in every respect establish a connection with a limited and contingent creature? And at the moment of the descent of revelation, how does the Prophet gain certainty that this connection is from His side?! These are questions whose answers are difficult for us, and insisting on understanding them is very misplaced.

The only point that is reasonable and presentable for us here is the principle of the existence or the possibility of such a mysterious connection.

We say there is no intellectual reason that negates the possibility of such a thing; on the contrary, we see mysterious connections in our world that we are unable to explain, and these connections show that beyond our senses and connections, there exist other perceptions and visions.

It is not bad to clarify this subject by mentioning an example.

Suppose we live in a city of the blind (born blind, of course!) with two seeing eyes. All the people of the city are "four-sensed" (assuming we consider the total external human senses to be five). Only we are "five-sensed" people. We constantly see many events in that city with our eyes and inform the people of the city, but they are all surprised: what is this mysterious fifth sense whose sphere of activity is so wide and extensive? And no matter how much we want to discuss the sense of sight and its function for them, it is useless; nothing but a vague shadow comes into their minds. On one hand, they cannot deny it because they find and sense its various effects, and on the other hand, they cannot understand the reality of sight because they have not been seeing for even a moment in their entire lives.

We do not say revelation is a "sixth sense," but we say it is a type of perception and connection with the world of the unseen and the Pure Essence of Allah that, because we lack it, we do not understand its reality, even though we believe in its existence through its effects.

We see this much: that great men come to humans with a call whose content is beyond human thoughts and invite them to Allah and the Divine religion, and they bring with them miracles and extraordinary acts that are also beyond human power, which clarify their connection with the world of the unseen. The effects are apparent, but the reality of the matter is hidden.

Have we discovered all the secrets of this world that we should negate the phenomenon of revelation if we encounter it and find it difficult to understand its reality?

Even in the world of animals, we see mysterious phenomena that we are unable to explain. Are the migratory birds, which in their long journey sometimes travel eighteen thousand kilometers in a year and move from the North Pole to the South and vice versa—is their mysterious life clear to us?

How do they find their direction and know the path precisely? Sometimes they continue their long journey in the days and sometimes in dark nights, while we, if we wanted to travel even one-hundredth of their path without technical equipment and a guide, would soon get lost. This is something that science and knowledge have not yet been able to unveil. Groups of fish live in the depths of the seas and usually return to their original birthplace, which may be thousands of kilometers away, at the time of spawning. From where do they find their birthplace so easily?

And there are many such mysterious phenomena in the world in which we live, and it is these that prevent us from resorting to denial and negation, and remind us of the recommendation of the Master of Philosophers, Abu Ali Sina (Avicenna), that: "Whatever strange thing reaches your ear, place it in the realm of possibility, as long as a definitive proof does not bar you from it"!

Now let us see what the materialists have done to deny the issue of revelation.
