The Logic of the Deniers of Revelation

Some of them, when the issue of revelation is raised, give a hasty response and say: "Such a thing is contrary to science!" And if we ask where it is contrary to science, they say with a decisive and arrogant tone: "The mere fact that natural sciences have not proven something is enough for us to deny it! Fundamentally, a matter is acceptable to us only if it is proven by the criteria of empirical sciences! Beyond this, in scientific studies and research regarding the human body and soul, we have not encountered a mysterious sense that can connect us with the metaphysical world. The prophets were of our kind; how can it be believed that they had feelings or perceptions beyond our feelings and perceptions?"

The Perpetual Objection and the Perpetual Answer

This kind of encounter by materialists with the issue of revelation is not limited to this case; they take such a stance toward all issues related to the "metaphysical," and we also say everywhere to remove their error:

Do not forget that the realm of science (of course, wherever they say science, they mean empirical and natural sciences) is the world of matter. The criteria and tools accepted for scientific discussions—laboratories, telescopes, microscopes, and anatomy halls—all work within this limit. These sciences, with these tools and criteria, absolutely cannot speak regarding what is outside the limit of the world of matter, neither in negation nor in affirmation. The reason for this is also clear, which is that these criteria have limited power and a specific realm.

Rather, the tools of each of the natural sciences are also devoid of power and application for another science. For example, if we do not see the tuberculosis microbe through great astronomical telescopes, we cannot deny it; or if the planet Pluto is not observable with microscopes and magnifying glasses, it should not be questioned! The tool of cognition in every place is proportionate to that science, and the tool of cognition for the metaphysical will be nothing but powerful intellectual arguments that open our way toward that great world.

Those who take science out of its realm are, in reality, neither scientists nor philosophers; they are erroneous and misguided claimants.

We see this much: that great men came and presented us with matters that are beyond human power and make their connection with the outside of the world of matter certain. But how is this mysterious connection? It is not clear to us. The important thing is that we know such a connection exists.

Several Hadiths Regarding the Issue of Revelation

Numerous narrations regarding the issue of revelation have been recorded in Islamic sources, which clarify aspects of this mysterious connection of the prophets with the Source of revelation:

  1. It is understood from some narrations that when revelation descended upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله through the angel, the Prophet's state was normal. However, when the connection was established directly and without a mediator, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله felt an extraordinary heaviness, to the point that he would sometimes faint. As narrated in At-Tawhid by Saduq from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, he was asked: "What was the fainting state that used to afflict the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله when revelation descended upon him?" He replied: "That was when there was no one between him and Allah; that was when Allah manifested Himself to him!" [^1]

  2. Another point is that when Gabriel descended upon him صلى الله عليه وآله, it was very politely and accompanied by respect, as stated in a Hadith from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام: "When Gabriel came to the Prophet, he would sit before him like a servant, and he would not enter until he had asked his permission!" [^1]

  3. It is understood from other narrations that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, through a Divine success (and inner witnessing), would clearly recognize "Gabriel," as stated in a Hadith from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام: "The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله did not know that Gabriel was from Allah except through [Divine] success." [^2]

  4. In another Hadith narrated from Ibn Abbas, an interpretation is seen for the issue of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله fainting during the descent of revelation, which is noteworthy: He says: "When revelation descended upon the Prophet, he would feel severe pain, his head would ache, and he would feel a heaviness, and that is [the meaning of] His saying: 'Indeed, We will cast upon you a heavy word.' And I heard that Gabriel descended upon the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله sixty thousand times!" [^3]

Ayahs

وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ رُوحًا مِّنْ أَمْرِنَا ۚ مَا كُنتَ تَدْرِى مَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ وَلَا ٱلْإِيمَـٰنُ وَلَـٰكِن جَعَلْنَـٰهُ نُورًا نَّهْدِى بِهِۦ مَن نَّشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ۚ وَإِنَّكَ لَتَهْدِىٓ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٥٢)
Thus have We imbued you with a Spirit of Our command. You did not know what the Book is, nor what is faith; but We made it a light that We may guide by its means whomever We wish of Our servants. Indeed, you guide to a straight path, (52)
صِرَٰطِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ أَلَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ تَصِيرُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (٥٣)
the path of Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth. Indeed all matters return to Allah! (53)

Commentary

The Quran is a Spirit from Allah

Following the general and universal discussion regarding revelation in the previous verse, the verses under discussion speak of the descent of revelation upon the person of the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, stating:

"And thus We have revealed to you a Spirit by Our command; [just as We sent revelation to previous prophets through various means]" (وَ كَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ رُوحاً مِنْ أَمْرِنا).

The expression "And thus" (كَذلِكَ) may be an indication that all three types of revelation mentioned in the preceding verse were realized for the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. Sometimes He communicated directly with the Pure Essence of the Lord, sometimes through the Angel of Revelation, and sometimes by hearing a sound similar to sonic waves, as alluded to in Islamic narrations and explained in the commentary of the previous verse.

Regarding what is meant by "Spirit" (رُوح) here, two views are found among the commentators:

First, it refers to the Glorious Quran, which is the source of life for hearts and the vitality of souls. This view has been chosen by the majority of commentators. [^1] Raghib in Al-Mufradat also says: "The Quran is called a Spirit in His saying: 'And thus We have revealed to you a Spirit by Our command,' and that is because the Quran is the cause for the afterlife."

This meaning is completely consistent with various clues present in the verse, such as the expression "And thus" which refers to the matter of revelation, the expression "We revealed" (أَوْحَيْنا), and also the descriptions given about the Quran at the end of this same verse.

Although "Spirit" in other verses of the Quran often appears with different meanings, given the aforementioned clues, it is apparent that "Spirit" here means the Quran.

In the commentary of verse 2 of Surah An-Nahl (يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلى‌ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ‌), we also stated that clues indicate "Spirit" in that verse also means "the Quran, revelation, and prophethood," and in reality, these two verses interpret one another.

How could the "Quran" not be in the position of a "Spirit," while we read in verse 24 of Al-Anfal: "O you who have believed, respond to Allah and to the Messenger when he calls you to that which gives you life" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَ لِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ‌)?

The second interpretation is that it refers to the "Holy Spirit" (or an angel even superior to Gabriel and Michael who constantly accompanied the Prophet of Islam).

According to this interpretation, "We revealed" (أَوْحَيْنا) means "We sent down" (أَنْزَلْنا); meaning, We sent down the "Holy Spirit" or that great angel upon you (although awhayna is not seen with this meaning in other verses of the Quran).

In some narrations found in well-known hadith sources, support for this interpretation is also seen. However, as we said, the first interpretation is more harmonious with the multiple clues present in the verse. Therefore, it is possible that such narrations, which interpret the Spirit as the Holy Spirit or a high-ranking angel of God, refer to the inner (batin) meaning of the verse.

In any case, the continuation of the verse adds: "You did not know what the Book was, nor [what] faith [was], but We have made it a light by which We guide whom We will of Our servants" (ما كُنْتَ تَدْرِي مَا الْكِتابُ وَ لَا الْإِيمانُ وَ لكِنْ جَعَلْناهُ نُوراً نَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ نَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِنا).

This was the grace of Allah that encompassed you, and this heavenly revelation descended upon you, and you gained faith in its entire content.

Allah's will was such that, in addition to guiding you through this great heavenly Book and its teachings, He would guide His other servants in the light of this heavenly radiance, covering the East and West of the world—indeed, all centuries and eras until the end.

Some misguided individuals have imagined that this sentence indicates the Prophet did not have faith in God before his prophethood, whereas the meaning of the verse is clear; it says:

Before the revelation of the Quran, you did not know the Quran, and you were not aware of or had faith in its specific contents and teachings. This expression in no way contradicts the Prophet's monotheistic belief, his sublime gnosis, and his familiarity with the principles of worship and servitude. In short, lack of knowledge regarding the contents of the Quran is one thing, and lack of "Knowledge of Allah" (Ma'rifatullah) is another.

The personal life of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله before the era of prophethood, as recorded in history books, is also a living witness to this meaning. Even clearer is the statement from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in Nahj al-Balagha:

"From the time he (the Prophet) was weaned, Allah coupled him with the greatest of His angels to lead him day and night toward the paths of noble traits and the best ethics of the world." [^1]

At the end of the verse, it adds: "And indeed, [O Muhammad], you guide to a straight path" (وَ إِنَّكَ لَتَهْدِي إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ‌).

Not only is the Quran a light for you, but it is a light for everyone, and a means of guidance for the worlds toward the Straight Path. This is a great divine gift for the travelers of the path of truth and the water of life for all who are thirsty.

This same meaning is expressed in another way in verse 44 of Surah Fussilat: "Say, 'It is, for those who believe, a guidance and cure.' And those who do not believe—in their ears is deafness" (قُلْ هُوَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا هُدىً وَ شِفاءٌ وَ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ فِي آذانِهِمْ وَقْرٌ).

[^1]: Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Sheikh Tusi in At-Tibyan, Fakhr Razi in At-Tafsir al-Kabir, Maraghi in Tafsir al-Maraghi, and some others.

Then, as an interpretation of the "Straight Path," it adds: "The path of Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth" (صِراطِ اللَّهِ الَّذِي لَهُ ما فِي‌ السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ).

What path is straighter than the path that leads to the Origin of the universe?

What path is smoother than the path that reaches the Creator of the universe?

True happiness is the happiness to which Allah invites, and the way to reach it is only the path He has chosen for it.

The final sentence of this verse, which is also the final sentence of Surah Ash-Shura, is in fact a proof for the fact that the Straight Path is only the path that goes toward Allah. It says: "Unquestionably, to Allah do [all] matters evolve" (أَلا إِلَى اللَّهِ تَصِيرُ الْأُمُورُ).

Since He is the Owner of the universe and its Ruler and Disposer, and since the evolutionary programs of man must be under the care of this Great Disposer, therefore, the Straight Path is only the path that goes toward Him. Any other path besides this is a deviation because it leads toward falsehood. Is there any truth in the world of existence other than His Pure Essence?!

This sentence is simultaneously a glad tiding for the pious and a threat for the oppressors and sinners—that the return of all of them is toward Allah.

It is also a proof that revelation must only be from Allah, because the return of all things and their disposal is toward Him. For this reason, He must be the source of revelation to the prophets so that true guidance may occur. In this way, the beginning and end of these verses are connected and cohesive, and the end of the Surah is also harmonious with its beginning and the general line governing it.

Key Points

1- What Religion did the Prophet of Islam follow before his Prophethood?

There is no doubt that the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله never prostrated to an idol before his mission and never deviated from the line of monotheism; his life history reflects this well. However, as to which religion he followed, there is discussion among scholars.

Some consider him a follower of the religion of Jesus عليه السلام, because before the mission of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, his was the official and non-abrogated religion.

Others consider him a follower of the religion of Abraham عليه السلام, as he is the "Sheikh of the Prophets" and the father of the prophets, and in some verses of the Quran, the religion of Islam is introduced as the religion of Abraham: "The religion of your father, Abraham" (Al-Hajj, 78).

Some have expressed lack of knowledge, saying: We know he had a religion, but which one? It is not clear to us!

Although each of these views has a basis, none is certain. More appropriate than these is a fourth view: The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله personally had a specific program from Allah according to which he acted, and in reality, it was his own specific religion until the time Islam was revealed to him.

The witness to this is the hadith found in Nahj al-Balagha mentioned above, which says: "From the time the Messenger of Allah was weaned, Allah coupled him with the greatest of His angels to lead him day and night toward the paths of noble traits and the best ethics."

The mission of such an angel is proof of the existence of an exclusive program.

Another witness is that it is not recorded in any history that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله engaged in worship in the temples of the Jews or Christians or any other religion. He was neither with the disbelievers in the idol temples, nor with the People of the Book in their places of worship. At the same time, he continuously followed the line and path of monotheism and was strictly committed to the principles of ethics and divine worship.

Multiple narrations—as quoted by Allamah Majlisi in Bihar al-Anwar—have also appeared in Islamic sources stating that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was supported by the Holy Spirit from the beginning of his life, and with such support, he certainly acted based on the inspiration of the Holy Spirit. [^1] Allamah Majlisi personally believes that the Prophet of Islam held the rank of prophethood (nubuwwah) before the rank of messengership (risalah); sometimes angels spoke to him and he heard their voices, and sometimes divine inspiration came to him in true dreams. After forty years, he reached the rank of messengership, and the Quran and Islam were officially revealed to him. He mentions six proofs for this meaning, some of which are harmonious with what we brought above (further explanation can be studied in Volume 18 of Bihar al-Anwar, page 277 onwards).

2- Answer to a Question

Following this discussion, the question arises: given what was said about the faith and actions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله before his prophethood, why does the above verse say: "You did not know what the Book was, nor [what] faith [was]"?

Although we gave a concise answer to this question during the verse commentary, it is appropriate to provide more explanation in this regard.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 192 (Al-Qasi'ah). [^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 18, p. 288.

The meaning is that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, before the descent of the Quran and the legislation of the Islamic Sharia, was not aware of the details of this religion and the content of the Quran.

As for "faith," considering it was mentioned after "the Book," and considering the sentences that follow it in the verse, it becomes clear that the meaning is "faith in the content of this heavenly Book," not faith in an absolute sense. Therefore, it does not contradict what was said and cannot be a pretext for those with diseased hearts who wish to negate faith absolutely from the Prophet and ignore historical facts.

Some commentators have given other answers to this question, including:

A: Faith does not mean mere confirmation and belief, but rather the sum of belief, verbal profession, and actions, as it is applied in Islamic terminology.

B: Faith means belief in monotheism and messengership; we know the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was a monotheist before that, but he had not yet gained faith in his own messengership.

C: It refers to those parts of the pillars of faith that a person cannot reach through intellectual reasoning, and the only way to them is through transmitted (naqli) evidence (such as many specifics of the Resurrection).

D: There is an omitted part assumed in this verse, and the meaning is: "You did not know how to call the creation to faith." [^1]

But in our opinion, the most appropriate answer and the one most harmonious with the content of the verse is the first answer.

3- A Linguistic Point

There is discussion regarding what the pronoun refers to in the phrase "but We have made it a light" (لكِنْ جَعَلْناهُ نُوراً). Some have said it refers to the Quran, the great heavenly Book of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. The possibility also exists that this light refers to the divine light of "faith."

However, more appropriate than both is that it refers to both "the Quran and faith," and since these two lead to one reality, the return of a singular pronoun to them is permissible.

O Lord! Always illuminate our hearts with the light of faith, and guide us by Your grace to that which is our good and happiness. O God! Grant us such capacity and patience that we do not rebel during times of blessing, and do not fall to our knees before calamities and trials. O Allah! On that day when the oppressors and the arrogant are bewildered, wandering, and without refuge, and the believers are immune and protected under Your shelter, place us in the ranks of the sincere believers.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Ash-Shura and end of Volume 20 of Tafsir Nemouneh. 5 Jumada al-Ula 1405 / January 27, 1985.
